commits.subject: chore(sancio): test-helper consistency, spy hygiene, prefix-coupling note
commits.body:
Four small cleanups surfaced during a fresh-eyes audit. None change
behavior; all close consistency or hygiene gaps.

(1) cron.test.ts: replace inline buildEmptyModulesQuery() with
createMockQueryBuilder() from _helpers.ts. The shared helper already
supports the bare-then/await shape these tests need (no .single()),
and this matches every other sancio test in the file. Removes a
duplicate mock-builder pattern.

(2) cron.test.ts: consolidate process.env.CRON_SECRET cleanup into a
single file-level afterEach. Previously both an inner afterEach (in
the helper-describe) and an outer afterEach ran the same delete on
every test in that block. Harmless but ugly.

(3) dispatcher.test.ts: add afterAll to mockRestore the console.error
spy. Vitest isolates test files today, so the leaked spy is harmless
in the current setup, but it's a side effect that would silently
swallow console.error in an unrelated suite if a future shared-runtime
change ever changes that isolation guarantee.

(4) api/sancio/[...slug].ts: add a one-line note above SANCIO_PREFIX
spelling out that the constant is coupled to the file's location on
disk and to vercel.json rewrites — there is no runtime check that
would catch a drift if either ever moves. Future-proofs the next
reader against silently breaking routing by relocating the dispatcher.

166/166 tests still pass; typecheck clean.

commits.subject: fix(sancio): close cron auth fail-open; constant-time secret comparison
commits.body:
Both cron handlers (lib/routes/sancio/cron.ts) gated on
`if (cronSecret && authHeader !== "Bearer " + cronSecret) return 401`.
When CRON_SECRET was unset, the conditional short-circuited and the
handler ran unauthenticated — every HTTP caller could trigger real
notifications (nudge) and real module expirations (auto-expire). Vercel
auto-sets the env var for production, so the practical exposure was
limited to misconfigured deployments and previews, but the gate was
silently absent on those.

Extract isAuthorizedCron(authHeader) helper. Fail-closed on every
unauthorized shape:
- CRON_SECRET unset or empty
- Authorization header missing or empty
- Token does not match
- Prefix wrong-cased ("bearer ...")
- Trailing whitespace
- Any length mismatch

Comparison via crypto.timingSafeEqual to avoid leaking the secret
through string-comparison short-circuit timing. Length guard runs
before timingSafeEqual (which throws on length mismatch); the expected
length is not itself a secret (CRON_SECRET is a fixed-length value
provisioned by Vercel and visible to anyone with project access).

Helper stays local to cron.ts. The only callers are the two cron
handlers in this file; lifting it into lib/auth.ts would conflate
unrelated auth schemes (JWT for user routes vs shared-secret for cron).

Tests (api/__tests__/sancio/cron.test.ts, new — 22 tests):
- isAuthorizedCron helper across every input shape
- Per-endpoint integration: gate rejects before any Supabase call,
  OPTIONS preflight bypasses gate (CORS), non-GET returns 405, valid
  bearer reaches handler body. Both nudge and autoExpire covered.

Docs:
- AI_MISTAKES.md: new entry recording the fail-open pattern
  (`if (secret && doesNotMatch)` is one keyword off from correct and
  inverts under unset secret; auth checks must be fail-closed by
  construction).
- API.md: cron section spells out the fail-closed behavior + constant-
  time comparison.
- USER_ACTIONS.md: drop the now-obsolete "200 = fail-open" outcome row
  from the cron verification step (the gate now fail-closes regardless
  of CRON_SECRET state).
- SESSION_NOTES.md: updated to reflect the fix and the broader scope of
  this branch (routing + cron auth).

Tags: bugfix, security, sancio, cron, testing, documentation
Complexity: 2
Urgency: 4
Impact: api
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: sancio-api-stability
Semver: patch

commits.subject: docs(sancio): cover the CRON_SECRET fail-open in cron-routing verification
commits.body:
Step 3's "no auth → expect 401" guidance assumed CRON_SECRET is set on
the target deployment. lib/routes/sancio/cron.ts opens with
`if (cronSecret && authHeader !== ...)` — when the env var is unset the
auth check is bypassed entirely and the handler runs with full side
effects. Vercel auto-sets CRON_SECRET for production deployments but
not necessarily for preview/branch deployments, so the previous
guidance had a hole: a user verifying on a preview without that env
var would get 200 + real notifications/expirations instead of 401.

Add a fourth outcome row covering the 200 case: stop testing, set the
env var, retry. Reader can now diagnose every response shape from the
doc alone.

commits.subject: fix(sancio): percent-decode URL segments; correct cron verification guidance
commits.body:
Two issues found on a follow-up audit of the req.url-based dispatcher:

(1) Behavioral regression vs the previous implementation. The old code
read req.query.slug, which Node's querystring parser had already
percent-decoded — so a segment captured from "/api/sancio/projects/foo%20bar"
arrived at handlers as "foo bar". The new path-splitting approach skipped
that decode, leaving handlers to receive "foo%20bar" verbatim.

For the current Sancio routes (UUIDs + literal action words like "status",
"split") there is no observable difference, but the semantics change is
silent and would bite the moment any segment ever held a space, slash,
or other reserved char. Standard HTTP routing decodes path segments;
the dispatcher should too.

Fix: map decodeURIComponent over each segment (after split, so an encoded
"%2F" stays as one segment rather than being split into two), wrapped in
a try/catch so malformed encoding (e.g. lone "%") returns [] → 404. Two
new dispatcher tests pin both behaviors.

(2) Misleading cron verification in USER_ACTIONS.md. The previous
guidance suggested running the cron endpoints manually with CRON_SECRET
"to verify routing." That is unsafe: nudge sends real notifications and
auto-expire actually expires modules. Running them against production
fires real user-visible side effects.

Replace with side-effect-free verification: curl without Authorization,
expect 401 {"error":"Unauthorized"}. That response proves the dispatcher
matched the cron route and reached the handler's auth check, with no
notifications sent and no modules expired. The doc lists all three
distinguishable outcomes (401 = pass; sancio 404 = dispatch regression;
HTML 404 = Vercel routing broken) so the user can read the failure mode
off the response alone.

Tags: bugfix, sancio, dispatcher, testing, documentation, cron, safety
Complexity: 2
Urgency: 3
Impact: api
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: sancio-api-stability
Semver: patch
commits.title: percent-decode URL segments; correct cron verification guidance

commits.subject: docs(sancio): pin rename to Vercel build/edge layer; add cron verification curls
commits.body:
Tightening pass on the prior fix:

(1) Attribution: the catch-all key rename (slug → ...slug) does not come
from @vercel/node — that package only parses req.query from the URL it
receives. The rename is in Vercel's build/edge routing layer, which
rewrites incoming URLs onto the catch-all by appending a query string
whose key is the literal bracketed name from the filename. AI_MISTAKES
and the dispatcher header now name the correct subsystem; the lesson
("route from req.url, not req.query") is unchanged. Investigation
against @vercel/node's changelog and routing source did not pin the
rename to a specific commit, but the rename is observable end-to-end
via req.url, which is what the fix relies on.

(2) Cron verification: the cron endpoints (/api/sancio/cron/nudge,
/auto-expire) share the dispatcher and were also broken, but Vercel
only invokes them on schedule (08:00 / 09:00 UTC). USER_ACTIONS.md now
includes manual verification curls using CRON_SECRET so the user can
confirm cron routing without waiting for the next scheduled run. Both
handlers are idempotent (no-op on modules not yet stale/expired), so
running them outside the schedule is safe.

commits.subject: fix(timers): close out TIMER_LEAKS wrap-up — splice on fire, theme.js variant 4, tripwire, doc gaps
commits.body:
Resolves every item flagged in the prior wrap-up pass.

src/pwa.js + src/markdown.js — fired timer ids now splice themselves out of toastTimeouts and feedbackTimeouts so the arrays stay bounded by pending count rather than session lifetime.

src/pwa.js — explicit comment block on the registerSW call documenting why it is intentionally NOT wrapped in __pwaModuleAttached: vite-plugin-pwa creates a fresh Workbox instance per call with no public unregister API; guarding it would prevent HMR'd onNeedRefresh / onOfflineReady changes from taking effect (OLD Workbox would keep firing OLD callbacks against OLD module closures), breaking dev UX. The trade-off — one orphaned Workbox per HMR cycle — is benign because showUpdateBanner already has an existence-dedupe guard and prod has no HMR.

public/theme.js — full variant-4 refactor: every module-level addEventListener now flows through a trackListener(target, type, handler, options) helper that pushes a removal closure onto a cleanups[] array. Window storage, matchMedia change, document click/keydown, plus all per-element listeners (theme toggle, random toggle, burger trigger, menu) are now named handlers stored in refs. window.__theme.dispose() releases everything and restores body.style.overflow / scrollbarGutter in case the menu was open.

public/theme.js:370 — removed the `setTimeout(closeMenu, 100)` Key Lesson 7 timing-fix hack. The click event's default action (anchor navigation, button click) runs after the delegated handler returns regardless of subsequent CSS class changes; closing synchronously is functionally equivalent and removes the arbitrary delay. Replaced inline anonymous handlers with named ones (onTriggerClick, onDocumentClickForMenu, onDocumentKeydown, onMenuClick) for the dispose path.

scripts/verify-timer-cleanup.mjs (new) — static tripwire that fails when any src/ module gains a setTimeout / setInterval / addEventListener / .subscribe registration without a matching import.meta.hot.dispose() block, or when public/theme.js loses its window.__theme.dispose surface. Wired as `npm run verify:timer-cleanup`. No new test framework introduced — fits the existing scripts/ codegen + verify pattern.

CLAUDE.md AI Notes — pointer to the canonical examples (src/pwa.js variants 1+4+5, src/markdown.js variant 4, public/theme.js variant 4) plus the tripwire command, so future sessions implementing the pattern in another file have a local reference.

docs/TODO.md — verified the cross-fleet "TIMER_LEAKS subsection inheritance" claim by directly grepping each repo's CLAUDE.md via the GitHub API. Surprise findings: the subsection is MISSING in repo-tor and few-lap (both flagged "FULLY RESOLVED" elsewhere in the file but predate the 2026-04-22 upstream addition). Added a CLAUDE.md doc-alignment touch-up item to each. Repo classification adjusted: 6 repos fully clean (was 7), 2 repos with doc-alignment touch-ups only (repo-tor, few-lap). Cross-Cutting Gap #17 rewritten with verified per-repo presence list.

Build verified with `./node_modules/.bin/vite build` — green, 33 precache entries, no new warnings beyond the pre-existing theme.js bundling notice (intentional: theme.js is a static-asset script for synchronous flash prevention before module scripts load).

commits.subject: fix(sancio): route from req.url, not req.query, to survive Vercel param-key renames
commits.body:
@vercel/node silently renamed the catch-all key from `slug` to `...slug`
(literal three-dot prefix from the [...slug].ts filename), making
req.query.slug undefined for every Sancio request. Production logs
showed `url: '/api/sancio/projects?...slug=projects'` with
`rawSlug: undefined` — the value lived at req.query["...slug"]. Every
sancio endpoint 404ed across all consuming frontends; CI was green
because the dispatcher tests drove req.query.slug directly.

Switch routing to read req.url: strip the /api/sancio prefix, split the
remainder on /, drop empties. req.url is HTTP-level state Vercel cannot
rename out from under us. Drop the rawSlug field from the fallthrough
log; add queryKeys (key names only, not values, to avoid leaking auth
tokens or PII) so a future framework rename is immediately visible in
Vercel logs.

Rewrite dispatcher tests to drive req.url shaped like production
(including the `...slug=` rewrite query string) instead of synthetic
req.query.slug. Add a regression-guard test asserting that
req.query.slug alone does NOT dispatch — pinning the new contract so a
future "fallback to query" change surfaces here.

Update docs/AI_MISTAKES.md and CLAUDE.md with the lesson: for Vercel
catch-all routes, route from req.url, not req.query.

commits.subject: fix(timers): apply TIMER_LEAKS pattern to glow-props itself
commits.body:
src/pwa.js + src/markdown.js had raw setTimeout/setInterval/addEventListener calls without cleanup paths. Both modules sit in Vite's module graph, so HMR re-evaluation was layering fresh listeners on top of the old ones every cycle: a new SW update interval, a new beforeinstallprompt listener, a new appinstalled listener, a new 1s install-fallback timer, and unbounded toast/copy-feedback timers.

src/pwa.js:
- swUpdateIntervalId, manualInstallTimeoutId, toastTimeouts[], beforeInstallPromptListener, appInstalledListener, domContentLoadedListener tracked at module scope
- nested toast exit/remove timeouts pushed into the same array (variant 1)
- window-level beforeinstallprompt + appinstalled wrapped behind window.__pwaModuleAttached HMR guard (variant 5)
- import.meta.hot.dispose() drains every tracked id, removes every tracked listener, closes any open install modal, resets the HMR flag (variant 4)

src/markdown.js:
- feedbackTimeouts[] tracks the 1500ms button-text-reset timers in copyMarkdown + copyCodeBlock
- import.meta.hot.dispose() clears them

Closes the self-compliance gap surfaced by the 2026-04-25 fleet re-validation. TODO Cross-Cutting Gaps item 17 updated; glow-props moves from "trailing items" to "fully clean".

commits.subject: fix(sancio): unblock single-segment routes; harden multi-segment routing
commits.body:
Root cause: api/sancio/[...path].ts read `req.query.path as string[]`. The
cast lied — Vercel's runtime delivers the catch-all param as a bare string
for single-segment URLs, so segments became "projects" instead of
["projects"], no segments[0] branch matched, and every single-segment
sancio request fell through to the explicit 404. The per-handler tests
import resource handlers directly and bypass dispatch, so this never
failed a test.

Changes:
- Add normalizeSegments(raw) at the top of the dispatcher: handles
  string[] (documented shape), bare string (split on / and filter
  Boolean — covers single AND multi-segment string forms), and undefined.
- Add a `console.error("[sancio] fallthrough", { url, method, rawSlug,
  segments })` line before the final 404 so any future regression
  self-diagnoses in one Vercel-logs query.
- vercel.json: drop "framework": "vite". Vite is auto-detected from
  vite.config.ts; declaring it explicitly may invoke SPA-aware behavior
  that interferes with file-based API routing on the platform.
- Rename api/sancio/[...path].ts -> [...slug].ts (via git mv) and read
  req.query.slug. `path` is generic and overlaps with Node/URL
  conventions; `slug` rules out a Vercel-reserved query-key collision
  and reads more clearly. Doc references in CLAUDE.md and API.md updated.

Test coverage:
- New api/__tests__/sancio/dispatcher.test.ts (45 tests). Mocks every
  lib/routes/sancio/* module with named "probe" functions that record
  their name + post-mutation req.query, then drives every documented
  route shape through the dispatcher. Coverage:
    * Input shape normalization: string[], bare string, slash-separated
      string, leading/trailing slashes, missing slug, empty array.
    * Route table: all 26 dispatch branches, asserting both the right
      probe ran AND req.query.id was set for by-id routes.
    * 404 fallthrough: 11 unknown-path cases plus a console.error
      diagnostic assertion.
    * CORS short-circuit: dispatcher returns early without invoking any
      handler when cors() returns true.

Operational follow-up:
- docs/USER_ACTIONS.md: post-deploy verification curl set + Vercel
  support escalation procedure (capture diverging x-vercel-id values
  from a working single-segment call and a failing multi-segment call)
  in case the platform 404 on multi-segment routes persists despite
  the framework + rename changes.

commits.subject: docs: audit tool-till-tees backend, sharpen 404 diagnosis
commits.body:
User asked "did you check the backend repo's code?" — earlier audit
was shallow.  Did a proper read of tool-till-tees@f7b2f29 via the
GitHub API:

- api/sancio/[...path].ts (full catch-all dispatcher)
- lib/routes/sancio/{projects,agreements,notifications}.ts
- lib/cors.ts, lib/auth.ts
- tsconfig.json, package.json, vercel.json
- recent commit log (last 15 commits, 2026-04-22 to 2026-04-23)

Findings:

- Code on `main` is correct.  Catch-all dispatches segments[0]
  matches to the right handlers.  Named exports match imports.
  ESM resolution is fine.  No recent commit touched the dispatch.
- The string "Not found" (no prefix) emits in exactly one place:
  the catch-all final fallthrough.  Every resource handler uses
  prefixed messages ("Project not found", etc.).  The report's
  log line therefore points unambiguously at the fallthrough.
- Coverage gap: api/__tests__/sancio/*.test.ts imports handler
  functions directly and bypasses the dispatcher entirely.  A bug
  in the segment-matching cascade in [...path].ts would NOT be

commits.subject: docs(session): correct false firewall diagnosis, document 404 handoff
commits.body:
Earlier in this session I documented the API 404s as a Vercel platform
firewall on tool-till-tees, citing "403 x-deny-reason: host_not_allowed"
from curl probes.  That diagnosis was wrong: the 403s came from the
Claude Code sandbox's own network proxy, not from Vercel.  Every
external host returns the same response from inside the sandbox,
including google.com.

Corrected USER_ACTIONS.md entry:

- The user's actual symptom is 404 with body {"error": "Not found"}.
  That string only appears in tool-till-tees/api/sancio/[...path].ts's
  final fallthrough, so the request reached the function but no segment
  branch matched.
- Lists the four most-likely root causes (stale tool-till-tees deploy,
  rewrite mistranscription losing the path segment, trailing-slash
  redirect, deploy alias mismatch) and concrete diagnostic curl + browser
  steps that must be run from outside the sandbox.

SESSION_NOTES.md rewritten as a handoff for the next session:

- Flags the sandbox-network constraint up front so the next agent
  doesn't repeat the same diagnostic mistake.
- Pins the 404's source string to its single emit site for fast triage.
- Lists what "fix all" did and didn't cover (5 iOS-zoom fixes shipped;
  the 404 is unfixable from this repo alone; no specific viewport bug
  was described — ask before guessing).
- Documents three latent mobile concerns (iOS keyboard over fixed save
  bar, NotificationBell 100vw, DaisyUI modal-box vh-not-dvh) that
  weren't triggered by the current report but are worth eyes-on if more
  reports land.

No source changes.

commits.body:
iOS Safari auto-zooms into any <input> / <textarea> with computed
font-size < 16px on focus. Input.tsx / TextArea.tsx wrappers already
enforce text-base, but five bare elements bypassed that guard:

- ContractUploadPreview.tsx:294  text-sm -> text-base   (rename input)

- ToneSelector.tsx:128           +text-base on input-sm (same)

All five keep their compact visual variants (input-sm or text-sm
removed in favor of text-base on full-size); only rendered font-size
changes from 14px to 16px so iOS no longer zooms.  min-h-[44px]
already in place on each so the touch-target rule (CLAUDE.md
"User Experience (Non-Negotiable)") is unaffected.

Audit triggered by mobile debug report at 360x674 / Android Chrome 147.
Same report also surfaced GET /api/sancio/* -> 404; that's a
tool-till-tees Vercel platform firewall (now returning 403
x-deny-reason: host_not_allowed) and is recorded in
docs/USER_ACTIONS.md as a backend dashboard fix.  Frontend rewrites
+ client are correct (apiRewrites.test.ts green).

commits.body:
Vercel build was failing with ERESOLVE: @types/react-dom@19.2.3 peer-
requires @types/react@^19.2.0, but the root project pins @types/react
to ~19.1.10 (which resolves to 19.1.17). Vercel's npm enforces peer
deps strictly; local resolved more leniently and masked the conflict.

Root cause: I added @types/react-dom@^19.2.3 in commit 7f6cd39 (the
DEBUG_SYSTEM hardening that introduced `react-dom/client` createRoot
in utils/debugRootMount.ts) without realigning @types/react. The
right fix isn't to bump @types/react forward — runtime is pinned to
react@19.1.0 and react-dom@19.1.0, and the type definitions should
match the runtime, not float a minor ahead. Pinned @types/react-dom
to ~19.1.11 (latest 19.1.x) instead.

After change:
- @types/react-dom@19.1.11 peer-requires @types/react@^19.0.0
- @types/react resolves to 19.1.17 (~19.1.10 range)
- Peer requirement satisfied

commits.subject: fix(edges): context-aware update toast + document dark-mode contract
commits.body:
Two items from the `edges branch` trigger:

1. handleCheckForUpdates 'update-available' toast now branches on
   isInstalled. The header Update button only renders when
   `Platform.OS === 'web' && isInstalled && hasUpdate` (home.tsx:620),
   so a non-installed web user (browser-tab, not installed PWA) who
   triggers the manual check and gets `update-available` would
   previously see "tap Update in the header" — but the header has no
   such button to tap. Now:
     - isInstalled: "An update is available — tap Update in the header"
     - not installed: "An update is available — refresh the page to apply"
   Network-first SW caching means a hard reload reliably picks up the
   new bundle for browser users, so refresh is the correct affordance.
   Updated docs/TODO.md verification scenario #7 to cover both branches.

2. Documented the storage contract for `synctone_dark_mode` in the
   theme flash-prevention inline script comment. Only three valid
   states: absent, 'true', 'false'. Any other value silently maps to
   light mode because the comparison is `darkRaw === 'true'`. The app
   only ever writes 'true'/'false' via AsyncStorage's web backend, so
   the silent fallback is operationally safe — but if a future caller
   writes a different shape (e.g. 'TRUE' from a refactor or a
   third-party migration tool), the silent-light-fallback would mask a
   real bug. Comment instructs maintainers to fix the writer rather
   than relax the check.

Verification:

- npm run verify:inline-scripts: 47 passed, 0 failed

commits.subject: refactor(types): runtime-validate icon hashes + drop dead casts
commits.body:
Three type-hygiene items from the `types branch` trigger:

1. hooks/usePWAInstall.ts: removed two dead-weight casts.
   `(window as Window & { __pwaPromptReceived?: boolean })` and
   `(navigator as Navigator & { standalone?: boolean })` were both
   made redundant by the `types/rn-web-style.d.ts` module augmentations
   in commit d314346 — those ambient declarations already add the
   properties to the base Window/Navigator types. Pre-existing casts
   became dead weight once my own infrastructure landed; closing the
   loop.

2. app/+html.tsx: replaced the bare `JSON.parse(raw) as Record<string,
   string>` cast with a `parseIconHashes()` helper that validates the
   parsed shape at runtime. Top-level must be a JSON object (not
   null/array/primitive); every value must be a string. Throws with
   a specific "got <actualType>" error + remediation (`Regenerate
   with node scripts/inject-icon-hashes.mjs`) if either check fails.
   Catches the "someone hand-edited icon-hashes.json to an invalid
   shape" case that a bare type assertion would miss — the failure
   would otherwise surface as a cryptic downstream `iconUrl()` throw.

3. components/debug/DebugPill.tsx: extracted the per-source color
   override map out of the PALETTE object literal. The inline
   `{ ... } as Record<string, string>` inside `as const` was a
   self-contradicting cast (outer wants literal keys, inner wants
   widened-string keys). Moved to a dedicated top-level
   `SOURCE_PALETTE: Record<string, string> = { ... }` that uses
   variable-level type annotation instead of assertion. Callers
   updated; no semantic change.

Verification:
- npx tsc --noEmit: 0 errors
- npm run verify:inline-scripts: 47 passed, 0 failed
- Unit-tested parseIconHashes against 7 input variants:
  - null → throws "got null"
  - string → throws "got string"
  - number → throws "got number"
  - array → throws "got array"
  - object with number value → throws "value must be a string, got number"
  - object with null value → throws "value must be a string, got null"
  - valid {string: string} → parses correctly
- Expo export with malformed icon-hashes.json aborts the build
  (confirmed via `npx expo export -p web`)

commits.subject: fix(race): DebugPill removes fallback pill directly + sync gate on update check
commits.body:
Two concurrency fixes surfaced by the `race branch` trigger:

(1) DebugPill's React mount didn't directly remove the inline
pre-React fallback pill — it relied on the inline script's 250ms
setInterval poll to detect `__debugMounted = true` and remove the
DOM node. That poller is cleared at 30s. If the JS bundle loads
slower than that (throttled network, cold mobile CPU, dev-mode
source maps on a large bundle), the React pill mounts AFTER the
poller stopped, leaving the fallback pill attached forever. Both
pills then render at the same top-right position.

Fix: React pill's useEffect now removes `#__pre-react-debug-pill`
directly, once and idempotently, regardless of whether the poller
is still running. In the fast-mount happy path this is a no-op
(poller removed it first); in the slow-bundle case it's the only
thing that removes the fallback.

(2) SettingsModal → Check for Updates button could fire two
concurrent `checkForUpdates()` calls on a fast double-tap. Pressable
`disabled={isCheckingForUpdates}` + the JS-level `if
(isCheckingForUpdates) return` gate both read a React-state prop
propagated through (hook → home → modal → prop → Pressable). Within
one render tick, the second tap can pass both gates before
`setIsChecking(true)` has re-rendered. Result: two concurrent SW
update checks, two Promises, two "You're up to date" toasts.

Fix: added a `updateCheckInFlight = useRef(false)` gate that flips
synchronously inside `handleCheckForUpdates`. Two taps in the same
event-loop tick are serialized — the second reads the synchronous
ref value (true) and returns early. Ref is released in `finally` so
a failed check doesn't wedge the button. The existing state-based
gates remain as belt-and-braces at the Pressable layer.

Verification:
- npx tsc --noEmit: 0 errors
- npm run verify:inline-scripts: 47 passed, 0 failed

commits.subject: fix(edges): clamp storage bar + empty-state when all rows removed from review
commits.body:
Two edge cases from `edges branch`.

1. Storage progress bar/aria-label could read >100%.
   The WHATWG StorageManager spec allows `usage > quota` briefly — the
   browser can shrink an origin's quota (user clears site data, domain
   rebalancing between origins). Without a clamp, aria-label reads
   "Storage 105% used" until the next loadStorageUsage() catches up.
   Added Math.min(100, …).

2. ImportStepReview rendered blank when user removed every row.
   The new X-button delete flow (commit 561673a) lets users splice any
   row out of the review list. Removing all rows left a blank area
   with a disabled "Import 0 transactions" button and no path forward.
   Added an empty-state card with a "Go back to upload" button wired
   to the existing emit('back') contract.

Dropped from the audit:
- "CashflowGraph downloads for empty workspaces" — verified wrong.
  The chart is inside `<template v-else>` gated on
  `transactions.length > 0`; empty-state workspaces correctly skip
  the ApexCharts chunk download.
- "Tag-suggestion spinner is perpetual" — useTagSuggestions has a
  10s INFERENCE_TIMEOUT that resolves []; no infinite spin possible.
- "0% bar when quota is 0" — the `quota > 0` guard at
  loadStorageUsage prevents storageUsage being set, so the indicator
  is hidden entirely in that case.

Type-check clean. 145/145 tests pass. Production build succeeds.

commits.subject: fix(errors): surface silent background failures — 2 error-path gaps
commits.body:
Two error-path gaps found by the `errors branch` trigger. Both were
introduced on this branch; fixing them symmetrically with existing
error-surface patterns in the same files.

- hooks/usePWAUpdate.ts runPeriodicUpdateCheck: the hourly
  `_registration?.update().catch(() => {})` swallowed SW-update
  failures with no trace. Developers investigating "why isn't my
  update getting detected on the hourly poll?" had nothing to look
  at. Replaced with `.catch((err) => debugLog('pwa',
  'periodic-sw-update-error', String(err), 'warn'))` — symmetric with
  the manual-check path's 'manual-check-error-sw' log and the
  version.json path's 'version-check-error' log. Background polling
  still never throws or breaks the page.

- scripts/check-player-ids.ts top-level `checkPlayerIds()` call had
  no `.catch`. Inner Supabase errors are properly guarded via
  `if (error)` checks, but a rejected Promise from the client itself
  (network failure, TLS error, CORS from a misconfigured proxy)
  would propagate to Node 22's default UnhandledPromiseRejection
  handler — ugly stack, non-specific error. Wrapped with explicit
  catch that prints a single-line message + stack (if Error
  instance) and exits 1 so wrapper scripts / CI can detect failure.

Verification:
- npx tsc --noEmit: 0 errors
- npm run verify:inline-scripts: 47 passed, 0 failed
- EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY="" npx tsx scripts/check-player-ids.ts:
  still hits the existing early-return guard cleanly
- Simulated top-level throw: catch fires, prints "❌ Unexpected error:
  <message>", exit code 1

commits.subject: fix(race): sync-flush dark-mode watchers to honor syncing flag
commits.body:
Cross-tab dark-mode sync had a stale-flag race. handleStorageSync
sets syncing=true, mutates isDark + currentComboId, sets
syncing=false, all synchronously — then calls applyTheme(..., true).

Vue's default watch scheduling is post-flush: the isDark / combo
watchers fire on the next microtask. By then syncing is already
false, the `if (syncing) return` guard passes, and both watchers
run applyTheme with skipPersist=false + emit debug log entries.
Net: 3 applyTheme calls + 2 redundant localStorage writes +
2 duplicated debug entries per cross-tab sync event.

No ping-pong loop (localStorage setItem only fires storage events
in OTHER tabs when the value actually changes; the values match
what was just synced). Not corruption, just wasted work the
syncing flag was designed to prevent.

Fix: `{ flush: 'sync' }` on both watchers so they run synchronously
on ref mutation, while the syncing flag is still true. Single
applyTheme call per sync event; single localStorage write; single
debug entry.

Other findings from the race sweep were false alarms:
- Debounce timers on unmount: onUnmounted already clearTimeouts both
  cashSaveTimeout and runwayDebounceTimer.
- ML request on disposed worker: dispose() rejects pending requests.
- Radiogroup rapid keys: focus order is correct even with overlapping
  nextTick awaits (last-wins matches last-keypress).
- Print-theme fallback cancel: dataset.printTheme saved before swap,
  so cancel restores correctly.
- ConfirmDialog double-click: parent guard (`if (restoring.value) return`)
  handles it synchronously before second emit's handler runs.

Type-check clean. 145/145 tests pass.

Urgency: 1
Impact: internal

Debt: paid

commits.subject: fix(bugs): reversed-marker guard + clipboard textarea cleanup
commits.body:
Two small correctness fixes surfaced by the `bugs branch` trigger:

- scripts/inject-icon-hashes.mjs rewriteSwIconList: added
  `endIdx <= beginIdx` guard. Without it, if someone accidentally
  swaps the `@icon-cache-bust:begin` / `:end` markers in sw.js, both
  `indexOf` calls succeed but the slice logic (`before + newBlock +
  after`) produces silently-garbage output — no error, stale PWA
  icons ship. Verified: reversing the markers now throws "sw.js
  markers are in the wrong order" with specific offsets, aborting
  the build. Byte-identical output on the normal path is preserved
  (verified by post-fix run).

- components/debug/DebugPill.tsx copyToClipboardWithFallback: wrapped
  the textarea append→remove window in try/finally. Previously, if
  any call between appendChild and removeChild threw (most
  realistically `execCommand('copy')` in ancient browsers, or a
  detached document edge case), the outer catch returned false but
  left the textarea in the DOM. On repeated Copy failures under that
  condition the leak accumulates. Finally-block guards the remove
  with `textarea.parentNode === document.body` so appendChild-itself
  failures don't double-throw on the remove.

Verification:
- npx tsc --noEmit: 0 errors
- npm run verify:inline-scripts: 47 passed, 0 failed
- Reversed-marker simulation: throws with clear offset-annotated message
- Normal-case re-run: byte-identical output (idempotent)

commits.subject: fix(errors): clear pendingImportData on replace error + log cash-on-hand write failures
commits.body:
Two real error-handling gaps from `errors branch`.

1. useRestoreWorkspace.confirmReplace: catch branch previously left
   `pendingImportData` populated after a failed import. A subsequent
   file pick would show the confirm dialog with the OLD file's
   workspace name until the new file's validation overwrote it. Now
   cleared in the catch alongside showReplaceConfirm.

2. WorkspaceDashboard cashOnHand watcher previously had a silent
   catch marked "non-critical persistence". The value IS the user's
   entered financial data (not a UI pref); silent failure meant
   alpha reports would never surface DB-write issues on this path.
   Switched to debugLog('db', 'error', ...) so the failure shows in
   debug reports without disturbing the user (the value is still in
   the input ref — UI remains usable).

Dropped from the audit:
- CashflowGraph `runway.endBalance` optional-chain: type is
  non-optional; adding defensive chains defends against impossible
  state per CLAUDE.md "don't add error handling for scenarios that
  can't happen."
- useDarkMode storage-sync try/catch: agent itself flagged as
  low-risk; string comparisons are safe.

Deferred to TODO:
- TransactionEditModal save fire-and-forget: modal closes before
  DB write resolves; error surfaces on the dashboard rather than
  in context. Fixing requires restructuring the emit-based flow
  (child-waits-for-parent via callback or promise). Not
  data-loss — reopening shows the unsaved state and a dashboard
  banner appears — but feedback is loosely coupled.

Type-check clean. 145/145 tests pass.

commits.subject: fix(pwa): always toast Check-for-Updates outcome + exhaustive switch guard
commits.body:
Two issues surfaced by an audit of the prior race-fix commit:

(1) UX gap when triggered from SettingsModal.
The original design had 'update-available' silent on the assumption
that the Update button in the home header would signal the user. But
the manual Check-for-Updates trigger lives INSIDE SettingsModal,
which covers the header — so a user running the check from Settings
got zero feedback on the one branch that actually mattered.

Fix: every CheckForUpdatesResult branch now surfaces a toast.
'update-available' shows "An update is available — tap Update in the
header" (4s duration, so the text has time to be read before
auto-dismissing). Toasts render at Z.toast (70) which is above
Z.modal (60), so the feedback is visible on top of SettingsModal.

(2) Non-exhaustive switch in handleCheckForUpdates.
Because the function returns Promise<void>, falling off the end of
the switch without handling a new discriminator value would silently
no-op at runtime — TypeScript wouldn't flag it. Added
`default: { const _exhaustive: never = result; return _exhaustive }`
so adding a new CheckForUpdatesResult value without updating this
switch becomes a compile error.

Docs:
- hooks/usePWAUpdate.ts: TSDoc updated to show the new toast text for
  every discriminator (was still showing the pre-refactor "silent")
- docs/TODO.md live-browser scenario #7: updated to match new toast
  behavior (6 toasts, one per discriminator)
- docs/SESSION_NOTES.md: added a "Follow-up wrap pass on the undone
  commit" subsection documenting the race-fix commit AND this
  polish commit, so the session's final state is fully captured

Verification:
- npx tsc --noEmit: 0 errors
- npm run verify:inline-scripts: 47 passed, 0 failed

commits.subject: fix(pwa): dodge stale-closure race in Check-for-Updates toast feedback
commits.body:
The handleCheckForUpdates consumer I added in the prior commit had a
real bug: it read `hasUpdate` from React state AFTER awaiting
`checkForUpdates()`, but the closure captured the pre-check value.
During the await, a SW update event can fire → `_hasUpdate = true` →
listener calls `setHasUpdate(true)`, but React's re-render hasn't
flushed by the time the handler resumes. Result: user sees
"You're up to date" toast AT THE SAME TIME the Update button lights
up in the header. Confusing.

Fix: split the `'done'` discriminator into two values — `'up-to-date'`
and `'update-available'` — so the hook itself reports the outcome
instead of forcing the caller to infer from stale React state.
Read `_hasUpdate` (module-private var, always current) right before
the hook's return statement, not from the React view of it.

Changes:
- hooks/usePWAUpdate.ts:
  - CheckForUpdatesResult: `'done'` → `'up-to-date' | 'update-available'`
  - Updated TSDoc to explain the race-dodging rationale
  - checkForUpdates returns `_hasUpdate ? 'update-available' : 'up-to-date'`
- app/(app)/home.tsx handleCheckForUpdates:
  - Switch case renamed + `!hasUpdate` check removed (no longer needed)
  - Comment updated to reflect why 'update-available' is silent
- docs/TODO.md verification scenario: updated with new discriminator values

Verification:
- npx tsc --noEmit: 0 errors
- npm run verify:inline-scripts: 47 passed, 0 failed

commits.subject: feat(pwa-manifest): align theme_color with light-first default
commits.body:
Follow-up to 2ab4e0b. Previous commit flipped the runtime theme default
from OS-driven to light-first, but left the PWA manifest's install-time
`theme_color` at `#202020` (business base-100, dark). That created a
splash → app chrome mismatch for installed PWAs: dark chrome splash
followed by light app, which is the exact jarring flash the light-first
policy was introduced to avoid.

Updated vite.config.ts:
- `theme_color: '#202020'` → `'#0082ce'` (corporate `--color-primary`,
  matches the meta theme-color tag the app applies at mount).
- `background_color: '#ffffff'` unchanged — already light-aligned.

Behaviour on install:
- Majority path (user never toggles dark): splash shows blue
  status bar + white background; app opens in light mode with the
  same blue status bar. Consistent.
- Minority path (user toggles dark and reinstalls from icon): static
  light splash → dark app status bar transition. Trade-off accepted
  per the light-first policy.

CLAUDE.md "PWA manifest theme_color" paragraph under THEME_DARK_MODE
updated to record the new rationale + a restore-previous-color
instruction if the light-first policy ever reverts.

NOT changed:
- `public/icon-source.svg` — #1e293b background + white S is visual
  identity, not theme-coupled. Like most branded app icons (Slack,
  Gmail, etc.), it keeps its own palette against any splash/chrome.
  The dark icon now actually has more contrast against the white
  splash than it did against the previous dark splash — slight
  recognisability win.
- `background_color` — already white.
- Icon generation — no bytes changed; `npm run generate-icons`
  output identical.

Verified: dist/manifest.webmanifest emits "theme_color":"#0082ce"
+ "background_color":"#ffffff". Tests 155/155, tsc -b clean, eslint
clean, `npm run build` clean, iconCacheBust tripwires still pass
(manifest path + versioned URLs intact).

commits.subject: feat(theme): light-first default on first visit — ignore OS prefers-color-scheme
commits.body:
New policy: first-visit users always land on light mode regardless of
their OS theme preference. Dark mode requires an explicit toggle via
the burger menu, which is then persisted and wins on every subsequent
visit across all devices sharing the browser profile. Rationale: users
wanted a predictable landing experience — OS-driven theme flips were
jarring for people bouncing between devices with different OS settings,
especially since Sancio content (agreement titles, counterparty names)
doesn't adapt cleanly to being viewed at wildly different color
temperatures between sessions.

Four touchpoints in the theme resolution chain, all updated to agree:

1. src/index.css — DaisyUI plugin registration. Dropped `--prefersdark`
   from the `business` theme so DaisyUI's own first-paint fallback no
   longer swaps to dark on OS-dark systems when the JS bootstrap is
   blocked. `corporate --default` alone wins.

2. index.html — inline bootstrap script. Simplified the dark-state
   resolution from "stored ?? OS media query" to just `storedDark ===
   "true"`. Unset localStorage means light, always. Also collapsed the
   two `<meta name="theme-color">` tags (media-queried by OS prefer-
   ence) into a single non-queried `content="#0082ce"` tag so the
   pre-JS status bar is also light-first.

3. src/utils/theme.ts:
   - Removed `prefersDarkMedia()` helper entirely (no longer called).
   - `resolveInitial()` now returns `{ dark: false, source: 'default' }`
     when no stored preference exists (was `{ dark: prefersDarkMedia(),
     source: 'os' }`).
   - `setCombo()` and `toggleDark()` coalesce `null` storage to `false`
     (was `prefersDarkMedia()`).
   - `initTheme()` no longer attaches a `matchMedia('(prefers-color-
     scheme: dark)')` change listener — OS flips don't propagate.
   - Cross-tab sync's "both keys cleared elsewhere" branch now
     applies light instead of re-resolving from OS.
   - Added file-header note capturing the rationale + a clear comment
     at the removed-listener site explaining how to restore OS-sync
     if the policy ever reverses.

4. CLAUDE.md "Adopted Patterns → THEME_DARK_MODE" — added a
   "Light-first default (deliberate)" paragraph naming the four files
   that together enforce the policy. A future audit or contributor
   considering adding OS-sync back has a checklist to follow.

User-experience summary:
- First visit on any OS: light mode.
- User toggles dark via burger menu: `darkMode: "true"` persists;
  every future visit renders dark.
- User toggles back: `darkMode: "false"` persists.
- User clears site data: back to light-first on next visit.
- Cross-tab: toggling in one tab propagates to others via `storage`
  event; clearing keys in one tab reverts siblings to light-first.

Tests 155/155, tsc -b clean, eslint clean, `npm run build` clean.

commits.subject: feat(session-undone): address 5 items from session-end undone pass
commits.body:
Session trigger `undone` surfaced 5 items — each properly fixed rather
than skipped or deferred. The big one is a real new user-facing
feature (Check for Updates in Settings); the rest are adoptions,
corrections, and doc sync.

New user feature — Check for Updates (web only):
- SettingsModal: new "Check for Updates" menu item with cloud-download
  icon, loading spinner during the check, accessibility labels. Only
  renders when onCheckForUpdates is provided (the parent decides
  based on Platform.OS).
- home.tsx: wires `usePWAUpdate().checkForUpdates()` to the `useToast()`
  context. Each discriminator of CheckForUpdatesResult maps to an
  appropriate toast: 'no-sw' / 'done'+up-to-date / 'error-sw' /
  'error-version' / 'error'. The 'done'+hasUpdate branch is silent
  because the Update button in the home header already signals.
- This is the first real consumer of the typed CheckForUpdatesResult
  discriminator; previously the return was fire-and-forget.

HTTPS_PROXY pattern — adopted (adapted):
- scripts/check-player-ids.ts now installs an undici ProxyAgent as
  the global dispatcher when HTTPS_PROXY / HTTP_PROXY env vars are
  set, so Supabase's internal fetch calls route through the proxy
  transparently. Graceful fallback if env var unset OR undici can't
  be imported. Adapted from the glow-props reference (which
  hand-rolls https.get() with HTTP CONNECT) because Supabase owns its
  own fetch and can't be routed via the stdlib path.
- CLAUDE.md § Pattern Adoption Status: HTTPS_PROXY flipped from
  "Applicable but unimplemented" to "Adopted (adapted)".

TIMER_LEAKS triage — corrected:
- Discovered via `curl` against glow-props GitHub Pages that
  TIMER_LEAKS.md was ALREADY published upstream; the local triage
  record's "Action required: open upstream PR" was stale.
- Updated docs/TODO.md § Pattern Triage Record to reflect completed
  status.
- CLAUDE.md § Code Standards → Timer and Subscription Cleanup: fixed
  the broken `docs/implementations/TIMER_LEAKS.md` local reference
  (folder was deleted in a prior session) to point at the published
  https://devmade-ai.github.io/glow-props/patterns/TIMER_LEAKS.md URL.

TODO.md live-browser entry — reorganized:
- Split into "already covered by `npm run verify:inline-scripts`"
  (lists the specific headless-verified assertions) vs "requires
  real browser session" (true manual QA). Prevents verifiers from
  redoing headless-proven scenarios. Also added an item for the new
  SettingsModal → Check for Updates button so it gets manually
  exercised once.

Z.sheet tier — semantics clarified:
- constants/zIndex.ts: documented that Z.sheet (30) is for NON-modal
  persistent sheets/drawers — sits below Z.backdrop (40) by design
  so a modal opening over a sheet correctly covers it. Modal bottom
  sheets like SettingsModal continue to use Z.backdrop + Z.modal.
- CLAUDE.md § Z-Index Scale row updated to match.
- No current consumer, kept for glow-props fleet parity.

  window.__debugPushError so the pre-React inline pill's DOM fallback
  surfaces the failure visually. Without this, a mount-time React
  failure would only land in the in-memory buffer that the failed pill
  was supposed to display.
- hooks/usePWAUpdate.ts: documented why _lastPeriodicCheckAt is
  module-scope and intentionally survives HMR (matches the lifetime
  of the interval + visibilitychange/pagehide/pageshow listeners it
  throttles). Prevents a future reader from "fixing" it.

Verification + tooling:
- scripts/verify-inline-scripts.mjs: added 3 new assertions —
  @icon-cache-bust:begin marker present, :end marker present, block
  contains a parseable PWA_ICON_PATHS array. Catches future edits
  that would orphan inject-icon-hashes.mjs before it throws on the
  next build. Suite now 47/47 (was 44/44).
- scripts/inject-icon-hashes.mjs: added --help flag describing
  inputs / side-effects / fail-loud conditions, with pointer to
  CLAUDE.md § Icon Cache Busting.

Docs:
- docs/SESSION_NOTES.md: rewritten with a fresh snapshot covering the
  seven fleet-alignment tasks landed on this branch (was stale —
  only described the first task).
- docs/TODO.md: added a delete-on-verify reminder to the High Priority
  live-browser entry. Each subsection's bullets get deleted (not
  struck-through) as they're verified; the parent entry only goes once
  every subsection is empty.
- CLAUDE.md: tightened the BURGER_MENU row in § Pattern Adoption Status
  so the "ModalBackdrop helper IS adopted" caveat is co-equal with the
  not-applicable verdict, not buried in a parenthetical.

Verification:

- npm run build:web (after Metro cache clear): clean, dist has zero
  favicon.png references
- npm run verify:inline-scripts: 47 passed, 0 failed
- node scripts/inject-icon-hashes.mjs --help: prints help and exits

  output on unchanged icons (idempotent)

commits.subject: i18n(chart): browser-locale axis + tooltip dates in CashflowGraph
commits.body:
Replaces ApexCharts' built-in format strings ('dd MMM' and
'dd MMM yyyy') with custom formatters backed by
toLocaleDateString(undefined, …). ApexCharts' internal format string
always emits English month abbreviations regardless of browser
locale; a custom formatter defers to the OS/browser preference.

Matches the formatting scheme already used elsewhere:
- useFormat.formatDateForDisplay uses toLocaleDateString(undefined, …)
- CashflowGraph tooltip Y values + SR chart summary use
  toLocaleString(undefined, …)
So the chart axes are now the last place picking up the locale.

Effect today: zero visible change — the entire UI is still English,
so English month abbreviations were consistent. The value is
forward-looking: the moment any user-facing copy is translated,
the chart axes track the locale automatically without a separate
change.

section).

commits.subject: perf(dashboard): debounce cashOnHand for runway recompute
commits.body:
Typing in the Cash on hand input previously fired a keystroke-level
reactivity cascade: cashOnHand → runway computed → CashflowGraph
series prop → ApexCharts cash-balance series re-render. While
individual steps are fast (runway walks ~90-365 daily points;
ApexCharts updates series in place), the per-keystroke visible
flicker in the chart is jarring mid-type.

Added a 300ms debounced mirror `cashOnHandForRunway` wired via
watch(cashOnHand, …) → setTimeout. Runway reads the debounced ref
for both its gating check and the calculation. Timer is cleared on
unmount alongside the existing 500ms DB-save timer.

DB persistence path is unchanged (still debounced 500ms). The new
300ms affects only the reactive recompute → chart redraw.

Type-check clean. 116/116 tests pass.

commits.subject: feat: storage-usage indicator, code-split ApexCharts, delete in review
commits.body:
Three S-sized feature items from docs/TODO.md.

1. Storage usage indicator on workspace list.
   Small footer on WorkspaceListView showing quota usage via
   navigator.storage.estimate() — icon + "N MB of M MB used" + progress
   bar. Hidden when the API isn't supported (older Safari / some Android)
   or while the list is still loading. Read once on mount; pull-to-refresh
   re-triggers loadWorkspaces but storage itself stays static — users can
   hard-reload for a fresh estimate if they just imported a large batch.

2. Code-split CashflowGraph / ApexCharts.
   CashflowGraph is the sole consumer of the ~500 KB ApexCharts bundle.
   Wrapped in defineAsyncComponent so ApexCharts loads on demand instead
   of on initial page paint. Workspace list, import wizard, and settings
   pages no longer download the chart library.

   Bundle impact (verified in production build):
     WorkspaceDetailView chunk: 560 KB → 40 KB
     CashflowGraph chunk (new, async): 523 KB (+ apexcharts 517 KB shared)

   Loading fallback is a chart-sized inline skeleton (matches breakpoint
   heights 280/350/420) with role="status" + aria-label so AT users hear
   "Loading chart…" instead of silence. 100ms delay prevents a flash
   when the chunk is cached.

3. Remove-row affordance in import review.
   ImportStepReview previously had only "Ignore" which dims a row and
   excludes it from import but keeps it on screen. Added a trash X
   button per row that splices the transaction out of the review list
   entirely — for users who've marked a bunch as ignored and want the
   screen quieter. tagInputIndex is reconciled on splice so a mid-edit
   index on a later row still points at the same row. 28×28 focus-ring
   touch target, aria-label includes the description.

Type-check clean. 116/116 tests pass. Production build succeeds.

commits.subject: i18n: fix pluralization consistency + swap directional classes to logical
commits.body:
Two of three audit findings applied. formatRelativeTime rewrite
skipped — pushed back because Intl.RelativeTimeFormat changes visible
text ("1 min ago" → "1 min. ago" in short style, or "1 minute ago" in
long) and none of the styles match the current UX exactly. The audit
itself said "leave unless you want it"; I followed that judgement.

MEDIUM (1) — pluralization inconsistency / latent zero-count bug:

Six sites in src/ use `!== 1 ? 's' : ''` (correct English — handles
0, 1, 2+ properly). Two sites in AgreementDetailPage (line 228 +
line 249) used `> 1 ? 's' : ''` which would render "0 module"
instead of "0 modules" if ever called with an empty count. In
practice both upstream paths (SplitAgreementForm refuses 0
selections at line 36, batchToggle only fires from non-empty
multi-select UI) validate 1+, so the bug couldn't fire. Still fixed
for consistency — same pattern everywhere, defence against future
validation changes.

LOW (1) — directional → logical classes:

11 uses of `ml-*` / `mr-*` / `pl-*` / `pr-*` / `left-*` / `right-*`
across 10 files, all logical spacing/positioning (not icon-
directionality). Swapped to Tailwind logical-property variants:

- ml-* → ms- (margin-inline-start): NudgeExpiryConfig helper text
  indent, SigningStatus timestamp, SequenceSignOff timestamp,
  SplitAgreementForm "Signed" pill, ContractUploadPreview oversized
  warning
- mr-* → me- (margin-inline-end): GoogleLoginButton icon-before-text
- pl-* → ps- (padding-inline-start): ContractUploadPreview action
  button indentation
- pr-* → pe- (padding-inline-end): ContractUploadPreview scroll
  container
- right-0 → end-0 (inset-inline-end): BurgerMenu dropdown anchor,
  NotificationBell dropdown anchor (both should anchor to the
  screen edge of the trigger in either LTR or RTL)
- left-0 right-0 → inset-x-0: ModuleEditor sticky save bar (full-
  width horizontal span)

Identical rendering in LTR; automatically mirrors under dir="rtl" if
the app ever adopts an RTL locale. Final grep confirms zero
directional classes remain.

Hardcoded English strings + RTL layout beyond what logical-class
swaps cover + `<html lang="en-ZA">` specificity: marked N/A per the
audit (sun-sea-o is intentionally English-only; adding an i18n
framework is an epic, not a trigger fix).

Tests 155/155, tsc -b clean, eslint clean.

commits.subject: refactor(pwa-icons): wrap-pass cleanups + docs + Metro quirk note
commits.body:
All 6 items from the icon cache-busting wrap pass. Nothing deferred.

## Fix 1 — Dead filter removed

`scripts/inject-icon-hashes.mjs` had
`.filter((name) => name !== 'icon.svg' || true)` — leftover from a
draft where I considered excluding SVGs from sw.js precaching. The
`|| true` made the predicate always true, so the filter was a no-op
that obscured the intent. Removed.

## Fix 2 — Preserve VERSIONED_ICONS declaration order

Script previously sorted PWA_ICON_PATHS alphabetically when
rewriting sw.js, producing spurious diff noise (favicon.ico kept
moving to the top of the list on every re-run even when contents
were unchanged). Now the rewrite follows the same order as
VERSIONED_ICONS in the script — human-readable, matches source
intent, byte-identical output on unchanged inputs.

Verified: re-running `node scripts/inject-icon-hashes.mjs` on
clean state produces zero git diff.

## Fix 3 — Legacy `assets/images/favicon.png` documented

Investigated whether `assets/images/favicon.png` (48×48 Expo web
legacy) is reachable. It's referenced by `app.json` as `web.favicon`
but that field is honored only by the legacy Expo Web toolchain;
synctone's Expo Router static-export pipeline emits icon `<link>`s
from `app/+html.tsx` pointing at `public/favicon.ico?v=<hash>`.
Left file alone (harmless, unused at runtime); existing CLAUDE.md
Icon Assets table row already labels it "Browser tab fallback
(Expo web legacy)".

## Fix 4 — docs/TODO.md live-browser entry expanded

7 new icon-cache-bust verification scenarios appended to the
existing High Priority live-browser entry:
 - Cold visit → every icon URL on the wire has `?v=<hash>`
 - Offline hard-reload → icons still display (precache hit)
 - Icon-change roundtrip: edit SVG → `gen:icons` → only the
   changed icon's hash diffs
 - Post-rebuild reload → changed icon fetched fresh, others
   cached
 - Android Chrome WebAPK regenerator picks up manifest URL
   change within ~24h
 - iOS Safari home-screen icon stays cached until
   Remove-App + re-Add-to-Home-Screen
 - `npm run build:web && npm run verify:inline-scripts`
   → 44/44 passing

## Fix 5+6+7 — CLAUDE.md docs

Added three notes to the Icon Cache Busting subsection:

- **Depends on Vercel Cache-Control headers.** The whole chain
  requires `manifest.json` being served with `max-age=0,
  must-revalidate`. Without that header the browser caches the
  manifest, so an updated `icons[].src` wouldn't reach the client.
  Same for `sw.js` and `version.json`.

- **Metro quirk: `process.cwd()`, NOT `import.meta.url`, for
  filesystem reads in `+html.tsx`.** Discovered the hard way —
  my initial implementation used `fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)`
  which resolves to a Metro-internal bundled-chunk path and
  breaks the build with a very unhelpful "Failed to statically
  export route: _sitemap" error. `path.join(process.cwd(),
  'public', 'icon-hashes.json')` works because Expo's static
  export runs from the project root. Documented so the next
  person doesn't repeat the investigation.

- **Dev-mode caveat.** `npm run web` doesn't re-run
  inject-icon-hashes; hashes come from the committed
  `icon-hashes.json`. Mid-session icon edits without `gen:icons`
  leave dev serving old hashes. Non-issue for prod (Vercel's
  `buildCommand` always runs the full chain).

## Fix 8 — TESTING_GUIDE.md § PWA icon verification

New section matching the § Theme verification style, with five
step-by-step tables:
 - Build-time verification (automated via verify:inline-scripts)
 - Runtime cache behavior (Network/Application panels + offline)
 - Icon-change roundtrip (most important correctness test)
 - Install + OS-level cache (physical Android + iOS)
 - Manifest integrity (loud-failure contract)

## Verification

`tsc --noEmit` → 0 errors.
`npm run build:web` → succeeds.
`npm run verify:inline-scripts` → 44 passed, 0 failed.
Idempotency: re-running `inject-icon-hashes.mjs` on clean state
produces byte-identical sw.js / manifest.json / icon-hashes.json.

Urgency: 2

Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: fleet-alignment
Semver: patch

rather than fresh triggers.

Code
1. useDialogA11y: removed SSR guards in lockBodyScroll /
   unlockBodyScroll. The composable only runs inside Vue components
   mounted in the browser (lockBodyScroll is reachable only via
   onMounted); `typeof document === 'undefined'` can't fire. Kept
   the empty-focusable Tab guard (legitimate edge case) and the
   focus-yank-back branch (legitimate stacked-modal edge case where
   a click on an input in the outer modal moves focus out of the
   top dialog).
2. CashflowGraph: simplified `reducedMotion` from a reactive ref +
   matchMedia change listener + onMounted/onUnmounted wiring down
   to a single const read at init. Mid-session OS-level reduce-motion
   toggles are vanishingly rare; users can reload to pick up a
   preference change. Save 14 lines for coverage that costs more
   than it buys. Also removed `reducedMotion` from the chart :key

commits.subject: fix(proposals): counterparty-accept now replaces module content (parity with creator-accept)
commits.body:
Bug surfaced by the `copy` trigger audit. Left unfixed in the copy
commit because the symptom was a toast inconsistency but the root
cause was a missing state mutation — a behaviour bug, not a copy
issue.

The proposal flow:

1. Counterparty creates Proposal A (proposed_by: counterparty.id,
   proposed_content: their change request).
2. Creator reviews. Three resolutions available:
   - Accept → updateModule({ content: A.proposed_content }) +
     resolveProposal(A, 'accepted'). Module becomes A's body. ✓
   - Reject → resolveProposal(A, 'rejected'). No content change.
   - Counter → resolveProposal(A, 'countered', <creator's text>) AND
     createProposal(…) as Proposal B (proposed_by: creator.id,
     proposed_content: creator's counter text).
3. Counterparty reviews Proposal B. Can accept / reject / counter.

The creator's accept-path (ModuleEditorPage:169-172) correctly does
updateModule + resolveProposal before the toast. The counterparty's
accept-path (lines 312-315 pre-fix) ONLY resolved the proposal —
never called updateModule. Accepting the creator's counter was a
no-op on module content.

ProposalCard gates the Accept button with `status === 'pending' &&
!isOwnProposal`, so this path only fires when the counterparty is
accepting a proposal they didn't author. In practice that means
they're accepting a creator-authored counter, and
`proposal.proposed_content` is the target body — same semantics as
the creator branch accepting a counterparty-authored original.

Fix: mirror the creator branch — updateModule +
resolveProposal + matching toast. Added a brief in-code comment
explaining the symmetry so a future reviewer sees why both branches
write updateModule before resolveProposal.

Removed the corresponding `## Behaviour Asymmetry` entry from
docs/TODO.md since it's fixed.

No new test: ModuleEditorPage doesn't have a component test yet (both
creator and counterparty branches share ProposalCard + useProposals +
useModules, which would all need mocking for an end-to-end accept
assertion). The fix is one line mirroring an existing working
pattern; verified against the creator branch's contract in the
audit above.

Tests 155/155 passing, tsc -b clean, eslint clean.

Tags: bug-fix, proposals, domain-logic
Complexity: 1
Urgency: 3
Impact: user-facing
Risk: medium
Debt: paid
Epic: frontend-audit-sweep
Semver: patch

commits.subject: copy: compliance with CLAUDE.md "what went wrong AND what to do next" rule
commits.body:
Closes 5 out of 5 cases where failure toasts stopped at "what went
wrong" without naming the recovery path — a direct breach of the
"User Experience (Non-Negotiable)" rule in CLAUDE.md. Also tidies
two toast labels to match their companion buttons + rewrites one
stilted error string into natural voice.

Toasts now compliant (all 6 sites):
- ProjectDetailPage: "Failed to save changes" → "...— try again."
- AgreementHeader: "Failed to save changes" → "...— try again."
- AgreementDetailPage: 3 sites — update agreement status, generate
  verification code, split agreement — all get "— try again."
- TemplateSelector parse-error fallback: the fallback only fires when
  parseDocument surfaced no message of its own (the library-supplied
  message is already user-readable). Extended copy: "Failed to read
  this file. Try a different file, or convert it to .txt / .docx /
  .pdf under 5 MB." Comment explains why the fallback is generic —
  we can only guess the root cause at that point.

Button/toast parity (2 sites):
- ModuleEditorPage: "Ready status removed" → "Ready status cleared".
  The button it refers to is already labelled "Clear ready status"
  (from the earlier ux pass); the toast now uses the same verb.

Stilted-voice rewrite (1):
- ProposeChangeForm: "Please make changes to the content before
  submitting" → "No changes yet — edit the content to submit your
  proposal." Reads like a peer prompt rather than a form-validator
  string; names the same constraint more directly.

NOT a copy fix — a behaviour asymmetry surfaced by the audit:

The audit flagged "Proposal accepted" vs "Proposal accepted —
module content updated" as inconsistent. On inspection, the two
toasts accurately describe two DIFFERENT behaviours:

- ModuleEditorPage:169-172 (creator accepts a counterparty proposal)
  calls updateModule(mod.id, { content: proposal.proposed_content })
  THEN resolveProposal(...). Module content updates. Toast says so.
- ModuleEditorPage:312-314 (counterparty accepts a creator's
  counter-proposal) calls resolveProposal(...) only. No updateModule.
  Toast correctly says just "Proposal accepted".

The toasts are right. The missing updateModule call on the
counterparty branch is almost certainly a bug — accepting a
counter-proposal should replace module content with the counter's

commits.subject: fix(export): wrap-pass follow-up — callback safety, sunset signal, regression doc
commits.body:
Addresses every wrap-pass finding (13 items) including the items the wrap
itself flagged as deferred or out-of-scope.

Callback safety (#2):
- New safeWarn() helper wraps every onWarning?.(...) call in try/catch.
  Without it, a buggy caller-supplied callback throwing during partial-weight
  failure handling would cascade into the per-font .catch (which calls
  onWarning again), producing an unhandled rejection that aborts the entire
  export. Two tests cover the path: throwing onWarning during per-font
  failure and during partial-weight failure — both must NOT break the
  export and surviving weights must still inline.

Test coverage (#3):
- New pwaCleanup.test.js (3 tests): happy path calls delete for both
  pre-rename names; missing globalThis.caches is a no-op (non-PWA env);
  rejected delete is swallowed (idempotent on missing cache).

Test fixture cleanup (#8, #9):
- Replaced the leaky `_text` field on the Blob mock with a
  WeakMap<Blob, string> lookup. Mock now uses real `new Blob([text])`;
  source recovered by reference, not by faking a field that doesn't exist
  on real Blobs.
- Removed the dead `_resetFontEmbedCache` export from fontEmbed.js.
  jest.resetModules() in beforeEach already gives a fresh module instance
  with empty caches; the explicit reset call was redundant. Per CLAUDE.md
  "Don't add features beyond what the task requires."

Assumption documentation (#5, #6):
- Code comment in ExportButtons near activeFontIds derivation flags the
  title+body assumption: if a future feature adds per-element font
  selection, this derivation must collect those font IDs too — otherwise
  exports silently fall back to system fonts.
- Code comment in fontEmbed flags the Google Fonts ACAO assumption:
  if they ever stop returning Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, every fetch
  rejects with CORS error and the warn-and-degrade path produces system-
  font output. Mitigation: the SW runtime cache buffers existing entries.

Bundle tripwire (#10):
- New src/__tests__/bundleSize.test.js (4 tests). Reads dist/assets/,
  asserts: each vendor-* chunk exists, app `index-*.js` stays under 500KB
  (well above current 349KB but well below pre-split 1099KB), pdf-lib
  stays in vendor-pdf (largest dep — must stay isolated from the app
  chunk). If a future dep is added without updating manualChunks in
  vite.config.js, this test fails before the regression ships.

Sunset verification (#13):
- DebugPill PWA Diagnostics tab now shows an `SW Caches` row enumerating
  caches.keys() and flagging stale pre-rename names with status `warn`.
  Concrete signal: when no debug report shows that warning for ~30 days
  (~Oct 2026), pwaCleanup.js is safe to remove.
- TODO.md sunset entry rewritten with that mechanism + step-by-step
  removal sequence (5 steps).

Regression scenario (#4):
- TESTING_GUIDE.md new test E8 — exact steps to verify the exported PNG/
  JPG/PDF renders with the chosen Google Fonts (not system fallback).
  Includes DebugPill log + cache row checks. Specifies the diagnostic
  drill-down if step 7 (visual font check) ever fails.

In-environment verification (#1):
- Started dev server, fetched index.html (crossorigin attribute
  confirmed), fontEmbed.js + exportHelpers.js + ExportButtons.jsx via
  Vite's module graph (full wiring confirmed). Production build's
  minified `index-*.js` confirmed to contain `font-embed-warning`
  (Is bridge), the getEmbeddedFontCSS({onWarning:...}) call (Ds), and
  `fontEmbedCSS:o` in the toCanvas options. Browser-visual verification
  (open exported file, check rendered font) is the user's step,
  documented as TESTING_GUIDE.md E8.

Test count: 153 → 164 (added: 2 onWarning-throw, 3 pwaCleanup, 4 bundle,
plus 2 fontEmbed restructured for callback-safety coverage minus 0).

commits.subject: feat(pwa): icon cache busting — content-hashed URLs across HTML,
commits.body:
manifest, service worker

Adopts the glow-props PWA_ICON_CACHE_BUST pattern, adapted to
synctone's Expo/Metro build chain (vs the pattern's Vite plugin
reference). All 5 URL surfaces now carry synchronized `?v=<hash>`
query suffixes so a new icon deploy invalidates browser, CDN, and
service-worker caches on first page load.

## Part A — scripts/inject-icon-hashes.mjs (new)

SHA-256 8-char content hash per icon in public/. Writes three
artifacts:

- `public/icon-hashes.json`: source-of-truth map
  `{ filename: "8-char-sha256" }`.
- `public/manifest.json`: rewritten in place — every icons[].src
  becomes `/<filename>?v=<hash>`.
- `public/sw.js`: rewrites the PWA_ICON_PATHS array between the
  `@icon-cache-bust:begin` and `:end` marker comments with
  versioned URLs.

Idempotent: re-running produces a byte-identical output when the
icon bytes haven't changed. Fails loudly (throws) on:
 - any listed icon missing from public/
 - manifest.json containing an icon src not in VERSIONED_ICONS
 - sw.js missing the icon-cache-bust markers
 - manifest.json JSON parse failure

## Part B — app/+html.tsx reads hashes, emits versioned URLs

Reads `public/icon-hashes.json` at build time via Node `fs.readFileSync`
(Metro's static-render pass runs +html.tsx in Node with `process.cwd()`
at the project root — that's the reliable way to locate the file;
`import.meta.url` resolves to a Metro-bundled chunk path). New
`iconUrl(filename)` helper returns `/<filename>?v=<hash>`, throws
if the filename isn't in the hashes (prevents silent "leaked
unversioned URL" regressions).

Every `<link rel="icon">` / `<link rel="apple-touch-icon">` now uses
`iconUrl(...)` instead of a hardcoded path. The manifest `<link>`
still points at the stable `/manifest.json` — the manifest file
itself is rewritten with versioned internal URLs, and Vercel
serves it with Cache-Control: max-age=0, must-revalidate so the
updated contents reach the browser on every page load.

## Part C — sw.js precache + cache lookup

Three changes:
 1. CACHE_VERSION: v9 → v10. Old DYNAMIC_CACHE entries with stale
    versioned URLs get cleaned up on activate.
 2. PWA_ICON_PATHS array between markers is rewritten by the
    injection script with versioned URLs. Install precaches these
    versioned URLs directly (not the unversioned ones) so a
    request for `/icon-192.png?v=NEW` hits the precache on the
    new SW's first activation.
 3. cacheFirst regex allows optional `(?:\?.*)?$` tail — without
    this, `/icon-192.png?v=abc` wouldn't match the `.png$`
    anchor and would fall through to networkFirst.
 4. caches.match uses { ignoreSearch: true } as belt-and-braces
    so a slightly-off URL (e.g. during a partial rollout)
    still finds the precached entry.

## Part D — InstallInstructionsModal "icon looks outdated" disclosure

New `IconCacheNote` component in the modal — collapsible via
useState (React Native equivalent of HTML `<details>/<summary>`).
Closed by default to avoid cluttering the Install modal for the
95% case.

When expanded, explains that the OS-level installed-app icon
cache (iOS Springboard, Android launcher, Windows/macOS Dock)
keys off installed-app identity and isn't touched by the web
cache-busting layer. Platform-specific uninstall/reinstall steps
keyed off `navigator.userAgent` + `BrowserType`:

 - iOS (Safari, or Chrome-on-iOS): long-press → Remove App →
   re-add from Safari Share menu.
 - Android (Chrome/Edge/Brave): long-press → Uninstall → re-add
   via browser menu → Install app.
 - Chromium desktop: browser menu → Apps → Uninstall SyncTone →
   reload page → Install icon in address bar.
 - Safari macOS: Launchpad long-press → X → Safari File → Add
   to Dock.

## Build chain

`package.json` `build:web` + `vercel.json` `buildCommand` both
now chain:

  inject-icon-hashes → write-build-version → expo export

`gen:icons` additionally chains inject-icon-hashes so the
hashes stay in sync when icons regenerate.

## Verification

`scripts/verify-inline-scripts.mjs` extended by 15 assertions
covering the new icon-cache-bust surface (total 44 up from 23):

 ✓ every built-HTML icon <link> carries ?v=<8-hex>
 ✓ zero unversioned icon URLs leaked through
 ✓ every manifest.json icon.src has matching hash
 ✓ sw.js PWA_ICON_PATHS entries are versioned
 ✓ sw.js cacheFirst regex allows optional query string
 ✓ sw.js caches.match uses { ignoreSearch: true }
 ✓ all hashes match icon-hashes.json (no drift between surfaces)

Full build chain (`npm run build:web`) succeeds end-to-end.
`npm run verify:inline-scripts` (post-build): 44 passed, 0 failed.
`tsc --noEmit`: 0 errors.

Tags: pwa, caching, infrastructure, fleet-alignment
Complexity: 3
Urgency: 2
Impact: user-facing
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: fleet-alignment
Semver: minor
commits.title: icon cache busting — content-hashed URLs across HTML,

commits.subject: forms: apply all 8 audit findings — error handling, a11y labels, autofill hints
commits.body:
Forms-trigger pass. Every touched form now has proper async error surfacing, screen-reader-announced live counters, and accessibility-hinted autofill behaviour. HIGH (3) - ProposeChangeForm: onSubmit prop widened to `void | Promise`; handleSubmit awaits it inside try/catch/finally. On failure: error surfaces above the submit button ("Failed to submit — try again in a moment"). `submitting` state gates the button + flips label to "Submitting…" so double-clicks can't fire duplicate proposals. - AddNoteForm: same pattern. Label flips to "Adding…" during flight. - NudgeExpiryConfig: added `inputMode="numeric"` so iOS/Android show the numeric keypad instead of QWERTY for the 1-365 nudge-days field. MEDIUM (3) - Character counters on ProposeChangeForm, AddNoteForm, ModuleEditor, ModuleSummaryEditor now carry `role="status" aria-atomic="true"`. `role="status"` implies polite aria-live; `aria-atomic="true"` forces the full "N / MAX" text to be announced on every update rather than just the changed characters. Screen readers throttle polite announcements so keystroke-level updates don't become chatter — but users near the cap get the feedback they need. - SplitAgreementForm: the checkbox group is now a proper
with , and the error

gets `id="split-agreement-error"` + `role="alert"`. The fieldset adds `aria-describedby` pointing at the error id when an error is present. Assistive tech now associates "Please select at least one module" with the group that failed. - ContractUploadPreview rename input: explicit `aria-label={`Rename section (currently "${mod.title}")`}` on the input itself. Previously users who reached the input via direct keyboard focus (not through the preceding rename button) lost the context the button's aria-label had provided. LOW (2) - InviteCounterpartyForm email field: `autoComplete="email"`, `inputMode="email"`, `autoCapitalize="none"`, `autoCorrect="off"`, `spellCheck={false}`. Mobile autofill + browser-level email recognition now work; typing stops trying to sentence-case the first letter. Passphrase field: `autoComplete="off"` (this is a side-channel shared secret, not a real password — we don't want password managers saving it), plus the same no-capitalize/no-correct/ no-spellcheck companions. Comment explains the intent so a future reviewer doesn't add `autoComplete="current-password"` by default. - ModuleEditor empty-content validation: left unvalidated per the audit's intent question. Added a comment in handleSave explaining why content emptiness is acceptable (skeleton modules / reverted drafts) and naming the guards that do exist (title-required + disabled-unless-hasChanges). Tests 155/155 passing — vi.fn() in ProposeChangeForm + AddNoteForm tests awaits as undefined, so the async widening is backward- compatible with existing mocks. tsc -b clean, eslint clean. commits.subject: fix(dark-mode): replace invalid 'black' print-theme fallback with 'night'
commits.body:
AppLayout's onAfterPrint restored data-theme via
`dataset.printTheme || 'black'`. 'black' is not among the themes
registered in src/index.css (cmyk, night) — if onAfterPrint ever
fires without a matching onBeforePrint (or if printTheme stored an
empty string because data-theme was absent at print time), DaisyUI
silently falls back to its default theme instead of the user's
original dark theme. Replaced with 'night' — the correct dark-theme
fallback, reachable only when printWasDark was 'true'.

Narrow edge case but easy to get right. Single-line change.

Other findings from the dark-mode sweep were either intentional
design (pre-framework inline debug pill renders dark by design —
per glow-props DEBUG_SYSTEM pattern; the diagnostic surface must
be CSS-independent and visually distinct from app theme), require
browser-level contrast measurement rather than code changes, or
the agent itself flagged as "no action needed".

Type-check clean. 116/116 tests pass. Production build succeeds.
