commits.subject: fix(debug): 4 bugs from ship/bugs trigger
commits.body:
B1 — redactUrls step 2 was over-redacting hash-router URLs with
query-style fragment params. `https://app.com/#/settings?tab=profile`
was getting redacted to `#[redacted]` because the fragment contained
`=`. Hash-router convention always starts with `/` after the `#`;
OAuth implicit-flow callbacks never do. Added a `(?!\/)` negative
lookahead to step 2 so hash-routes are preserved while
`#access_token=…` is still redacted. Three new assertions in the
redactUrls test block cover the new behaviour + prove the OAuth
path still works.

B2 — RECOVERY_TIMEOUT_MS (15000) was a literal inside the
recoveryScript template, while BOOT_PILL_TIMEOUT_MS was a named
constant. The comment on BOOT_PILL_TIMEOUT_MS documented a
must-stay-greater-than invariant with RECOVERY_TIMEOUT_MS — but
RECOVERY_TIMEOUT_MS didn't exist as a symbol so the invariant could
silently break. Extracted RECOVERY_TIMEOUT_MS alongside
BOOT_PILL_TIMEOUT_MS, interpolated into the recoveryScript template,
and made the relationship explicit in both comments.

B7 — DebugRootBoundary.componentDidCatch was discarding React's
errorInfo.componentStack. React's componentStack is more useful than
the raw JS stack for locating the crashing component (JS stack shows
internal React frames; componentStack shows the React tree path).
Updated the signature to accept errorInfo and include componentStack
in the structured details. Test updated to pass errorInfo and assert
componentStack lands in details; also added null-componentStack
coverage for React cases where the stack isn't available.

B10 — installConsoleCapture() ran during Expo's static rendering
(Node process, Platform.OS === 'web'), which patched Node's console
for the duration of the SSR process. Harmless but wasteful (leaked
debug entries nobody reads). Added a `typeof document === 'undefined'`
gate to skip SSR. Both jest test files now stub a minimal `document`
object before importing debugLog so the tests still exercise the
web-only install path; commented on why.

and BOOT_PILL_TIMEOUT_MS = 20000 interpolated correctly.

commits.subject: test(debug): extract DebugRootBoundary + unit-test its three paths
commits.body:
The ErrorBoundary class that wraps DebugPill in the separate web root
was inlined in debugRootMount.web.tsx. Moving the setup-side effect
(createRoot, DOMContentLoaded listener) into the same file as the
testable class made it un-unit-testable — importing the file runs the
mount code.

- New: src/debug/DebugRootBoundary.tsx — the class component +
  plain-DOM fallback, exported. Unchanged behaviour.
- debugRootMount.web.tsx imports DebugRootBoundary instead of defining
  it inline.

New test: src/debug/__tests__/DebugRootBoundary.test.tsx (5 cases).
Tests the class methods directly (getDerivedStateFromError,
componentDidCatch, render-with-error, render-without-error) rather
than routing through react-test-renderer — that package is deprecated
on React 19 and returns null without act() plumbing that would be
noise for a pure class-behaviour test. Class lifecycle methods are
pure functions of state/props; calling them directly exercises the
full contract.

Test coverage:
- getDerivedStateFromError returns { error } shape.
- render() passes children through when state.error is null.
- render() returns the plain-<div> fallback (critical: not a RN
  View, since the boundary has to survive a broken RN-Web stack)
  with the error message in both the title attribute and children.
  Positioning verified against DebugPill's bottom-right z-80 slot.
- componentDidCatch writes a 'DebugPill crashed' entry with source
  'global', severity 'error', and details { message, stack }.
- componentDidCatch handles non-Error throw values via errDetails,
  preserving the uniform { message } shape.

Jest plumbing (minor):
- testMatch now matches .test.tsx as well as .test.ts.
- Added a transform override so ts-jest compiles .tsx with
  jsx: 'react-jsx'. The main tsconfig uses jsx: 'react-native'
  (Metro-friendly, preserves JSX) which ts-jest can't execute;
  the override is test-only and doesn't touch the main build.

commits.subject: fix(debug): notifyAll re-entry guard + cascade bound
commits.body:
Previously, a subscriber that synchronously called debugAdd inside its
own callback would re-enter notifyAll mid-iteration. Best case: each
re-entry iterates all subscribers again, stack depth grows with each
nested call. Worst case: a subscriber that always calls debugAdd
infinite-recurses to stack overflow. None of the current subscribers
do this, but the latent concern has been noted in-session for several
refactors.

Fix:
- `notifying` flag tracks whether notifyAll is mid-iteration. If
  re-entered, sets pendingNotify and returns — the entry is still
  added (debugAdd adds to the buffer before calling notifyAll), and
  the outer do-while picks up pendingNotify and runs another pass so
  every subscriber still sees the new snapshot. Single-pass iteration
  semantics preserved.
- NOTIFY_CASCADE_MAX = 10 bounds pathological subscribers that ALWAYS
  call debugAdd (which would otherwise spin forever in the do-while).
  10 rounds is way beyond any realistic cascade — Fast-Refresh errors,
  PropType warnings, etc. rarely stack more than 2–3 deep.

New tests (2):
- A subscriber that cascades 3 times: all 3 'cascade-N' entries land
  in the buffer, no RangeError, no hang.
- A subscriber that infinitely re-triggers: entry count is finite
  (bounded by the cascade guard) and control returns normally.

tsc 0, jest 83/83 (up from 81), expo web build clean.

commits.subject: fix(debug): sentinel-guard global error listeners against Fast Refresh duplication
commits.body:
The console-patch block has had a __debugPatched sentinel from day one
to avoid re-wrapping console.error/warn on repeat module execution.
The two window-level listeners right above it did not. On Fast Refresh
(dev) or any other repeat module evaluation, each re-execute would
addEventListener again — silently multiplying every captured error by
the number of re-executes.

Fix: mirror the console-patch pattern with a __debugGlobalListenersInstalled
sentinel on globalThis. Registration runs only when the flag is unset;
re-executions see it set and skip.

New tests (2):
- Asserts that on first module load, addEventListener was called once
  each for 'error' and 'unhandledrejection'.
- Under jest.isolateModules + re-require, asserts zero new
  addEventListener calls for those types.

Jest plumbing: Node's globalThis has no addEventListener (web-only API),
so the production `typeof globalThis.addEventListener !== 'undefined'`
guard would have made the branch a dead no-op under jest. Install a
jest.fn stub BEFORE the first require so the branch actually executes
and idempotency is observable.

tsc 0, jest 81/81 (up from 79), expo web build clean.

commits.subject: feat(debug): redact OAuth-style fragment tokens + mention redaction in CLAUDE.md
commits.body:
Two small follow-ups on the URL-redaction pass:

1. Redact fragments that contain `=`. OAuth implicit-flow callbacks
   deliver access tokens via the fragment (`#access_token=…`); the
   previous pass preserved fragments wholesale, which meant the one
   well-known fragment-carried-secret shape was still leaking.

   Tight heuristic: `=` is the discriminator. Section anchors
   (`#chapter`) and hash-routes (`#/settings`) don't contain `=` and
   remain readable — they're useful for debugging and not secret.

2. CLAUDE.md's debug-system project-status bullet now mentions
   redaction, so a reader scanning project state knows about it
   without having to grep the code.

Implementation:
- Second regex pass in redactUrls: `/(https?:\/\/[^\s#'"<>]*)#[^\s'"<>]*=[^\s'"<>]*/g` → `$1#[redacted]`. Runs after the query-string pass so
  a URL with both a query and a fragment (`?q=1#t=abc`) gets both
  redacted (`?[redacted]#[redacted]`).
- URL portion in step 2 allows `?` so a previously-redacted query
  (`?[redacted]`) is included in the URL match and not broken.

3 new tests: OAuth fragment redacted, anchor + hash-route preserved,
query-and-fragment both redacted. 79 tests total (up from 76).
tsc 0.

commits.subject: feat(debug): redact URL query strings in debug reports + env output
commits.body:
Users paste debug reports into support chats, and screenshot the pill
when the format is more convenient. Query strings routinely carry auth
tokens, session ids, and PII — both output paths need redaction before
leaving the device.

- New redactUrls(text) helper exported from debugLog.ts. Replaces
  everything from `?` up to the next whitespace / `#` / quote / angle
  bracket with `?[redacted]`. The fragment is preserved (client-side,
  usually non-sensitive).
- Regex intentionally excludes quotes and angle brackets from the
  query-match character class: without that, URLs embedded in
  JSON-stringified details (`{"url":"https://x/?t=1"}`) would have the
  regex greedily consume the closing `"}` as part of the "query" and
  produce malformed output.
- Applied in TWO places:
  - debugGetEnvironment: redacts env.url at construction time so the
    pill's Env tab can't leak the query in a screenshot. A caller that
    needs the raw URL reads globalThis.location.href directly.
  - debugGenerateReport: final sweep over the joined string so URLs
    that appear in log entry events / details get redacted too.

Tightening from the task snippet's regex (`[^\s#]*` for the query
chars): expanded to `[^\s#'"<>]*` to fix the JSON-embedded URL case
noted above. Fragment-preservation behaviour of the original is kept.

7 new tests — straight redaction / fragment preservation / no-query
passthrough / multi-URL / JSON-embedded / structured-detail via
report / env.url via report (location mocked).

76 tests total (up from 69). tsc 0, jest green, expo web build clean.

commits.subject: refactor(debug): errDetails helper unifies the 10 structured-error callsites
commits.body:
The previous commit migrated 10 structure-losing callsites to inline
ternaries — { message, stack } for Error, { reason: String(e) } for
non-Error. Two different shapes meant a future filter doing
`entry.details.message.includes('...')` would break silently on the
non-Error branch.

Extracted `errDetails(err: unknown): { message: string; stack?: string }`
in debugLog.ts. Same top-level shape for both paths: non-Error values
coerce to String() and land in `message`; stack is simply absent. All
10 callsites collapse from 3-line ternaries to single-line helper calls.

Also applied to the unhandled-rejection global handler, which had
the same two-shape split. Global `error` event handler keeps its
ErrorEvent-specific fields (filename, lineno, colno) — those don't
fit the error-ish shape and errDetails isn't the right abstraction
for them.

New tests:
- `errDetails normalises Error and non-Error to a uniform { message,
  stack? } shape` — covers primitives, strings, null/undefined, Error.
- `debugGenerateReport formats both string and structured details` —
  covers the previously-unexercised branching in the clipboard report.

commits.subject: refactor(debug): details accepts string | Record<string, unknown>
commits.body:
The 70+ debugAdd callsites in this repo split roughly two ways:
- ~10 flatten real structure into a string (err instanceof Error ?
  err.message : String(err); JSON.stringify({...}); ErrorEvent.message
  dropping filename/lineno/colno).
- ~50 just pass a plain message string — no structure to preserve.

Wrapping the latter group as { message: str } would be mechanical
noise across ~20 unrelated files for no queryability gain. So instead
of migrating the literal "details: string → Record<string, unknown>"
signature the task proposed, I made the field a union type and
migrated only the structure-losing callsites.

debugLog.ts:
- DebugEntry.details: string → string | Record<string, unknown>
  (aliased as DebugDetails).
- Global error handler: { message, filename, lineno, colno } instead
  of discarding everything but message.
- Unhandled rejection: split by whether reason is an Error — Error
  gets { message, stack }; non-Error gets { reason }.
- Boot entry ('Debug log initialized'): unchanged, no details.
- console.error / console.warn patches: remaining args stored as
  { args: unknownArgs } — raw values preserved, no pre-flattening
  to "extra 42" strings. First arg is still safeStringify'd for the
  event field (truncated at 160 chars as before).
- safeStringify (formerly local formatArg) exported so the renderer
  and report generator can share the same Error / circular-ref /
  React-element handling.

Migrated structure-losing callsites (10 files):
- app/_layout.tsx: Early SW registration failure
- src/debug/debugRootMount.web.tsx: DebugPill crash
- src/hooks/useTheme.ts: Theme apply failure
- src/hooks/usePWAUpdate.ts: 2× (init + manual check failure)
- src/hooks/useFuelTypes.ts: Fuel types fetch failure
- src/hooks/useAuth.ts: Auth init failure
- src/hooks/usePWAInstall.ts: Install prompt error + the diagnostic
  JSON.stringify({...}) wrapper which now passes the object directly.
- src/components/ui/BurgerMenu.tsx: Menu action failure
- src/components/map/FuelMapWeb.tsx: Map init failure

Left unchanged (50+ callsites in FilterContext, StationList, useTheme
toggle, useSearchModal, useLocation success path, usePWAUpdate info
messages, useAuth sign-in/out, useFuelTypes success, useStations
success, FuelMapWeb layer/style updates, LocationSearch, admin bulk
ops success/failure-with-msg-already-extracted, etc.) — these pass
plain human-readable strings and wrapping them as { message: str }
would add noise for zero structural benefit. The union type is what
makes "migrate only where it matters" viable.

Renderer:
- DebugPill LogRow: displays typeof details === 'string' ? details :
  safeStringify(details). Same single-line truncated view as before.
- debugGenerateReport: same branching for the clipboard output.

Tests:
- Updated 2 existing assertions: "remaining args in details" now
  checks { args: ['extra', 42] } (types preserved, number stayed a
  number); "Error instances preserved" checks err is still an Error
  instance in args[0] rather than a pre-flattened string.
- Added 'accepts both string and Record<string, unknown> details'
  covering the union type.
- Added 'safeStringify handles Error / circular / React element /
  primitives' covering the newly-exported helper.

67 tests (up from 65). tsc --noEmit: 0. expo export --platform web:
clean.

commits.subject: refactor(debug): debugSubscribe replays entries + isolates subscriber errors
commits.body:
Architectural tightening of the pub/sub surface.

Problem: every subscriber had to do two things on mount — call
debugGetEntries() for the initial snapshot, then debugSubscribe() for
live updates. Easy to forget; left a race window where an entry added
between the two calls was visible to neither path. Separately, a
throwing subscriber would break subsequent subscribers in the same
notification loop (listeners.forEach with no try/catch).

Changes:
- Subscriber type: () => void → (entries: DebugEntry[]) => void.
- debugSubscribe now invokes the callback synchronously with the
  current snapshot before returning. Wrapped in try/catch.
- notifyAll() helper used by debugAdd/debugClear; iterates a
  snapshot of subscribers (so a self-unsubscribing callback doesn't
  disrupt iteration) and wraps each call in try/catch.
- Error isolation: a throwing subscriber never prevents others from
  receiving a notification, and never propagates out of debugAdd
  or debugSubscribe.
- DebugPill.useEffect collapses to a single debugSubscribe((latest)
  => { setEntries([...latest]); setCounts(debugGetCounts()); }).
  Removed the manual pre-subscribe sync and the now-unused
  debugGetEntries import.

Tests:
- Updated 'does not recurse when subscriber calls console.error' —
  immediate replay means the subscriber fires once during subscribe
  too, producing an additional 'subscriber log' entry. Adjusted
  expectations.
- New 'invokes the callback synchronously with the current snapshot
  (immediate replay)'.
- New 'isolates subscriber errors: one throwing subscriber does not
  block others'.

tsc --noEmit: 0 errors. jest: 65/65 (up from 63). expo export --platform
web: clean.

Deviation from task snippet: task noted "wrap each subscribers.forEach
in try/catch" — I extracted a single notifyAll helper used by both
debugAdd and debugClear so the isolation policy lives in one place.

commits.subject: fix(debug): boot pill clear also removes error listeners + guards null reason
commits.body:
Two small tightenings to the pre-React boot pill:

1. __debugClearLoadTimer now also removes the window.error and
   unhandledrejection listeners it registered. Previously they stayed
   live post-mount, so any runtime error would re-trigger the red
   banner alongside DebugPill's own error-count badge (double-
   surfacing the same event, with inconsistent UX). Boot pill is for
   *boot* failure visibility — DebugPill handles runtime.
2. unhandledrejection handler falls back to 'Unhandled rejection'
   when e.reason is null/undefined, instead of showing the literal
   string "undefined" / "null".

Handlers extracted to named function expressions so removeEventListener
can target them.

commits.subject: docs(claude): add skipped + assumed + approach reflective triggers; split meta table
commits.body:
Three new reflective passes, each individually invokable:
- `skipped` / `skp` — what was skipped (including issues noticed outside
  the current changes that were left alone)
- `assumed` / `asm` — what was assumed (including things treated as
  out of scope)
- `approach` / `apr` — honest self-review: was the fix the best / most
  proper way? What shortcuts were taken, what would a senior reviewer
  flag, what does the proper version look like

Also split the old "Meta sweeps" table into two:
- **Meta sweeps** — multi-trigger sweeps (`quick`, `ship`, `all`)
- **Reflective passes** — single-pass reflections (`risk`, `surface`,
  `wrap`, `skipped`, `assumed`, `approach`, `cold`)

The distinction is behavioral: sweeps fan out to other triggers and
pause between each; reflective passes answer one question without
fan-out.

commits.subject: feat(debug): pre-React inline boot pill for silent-boot-failure visibility
commits.body:
A broken JS bundle, parse error, or unhandled rejection during boot
previously produced a silent white screen. This adds a classic inline
IIFE in +html.tsx <head>, registered before the Metro bundle, that
shows a small red bottom-right banner in those cases.

Pill shows on:
- window.error (native script errors, React render crashes that bubble)
- unhandledrejection (async boot failures)
- 20s fallback timer — last-resort "App failed to load" indicator
  (preempted by the existing 15s recovery reload on first load; kicks
  in once the recovery counter caps at 2 reload attempts)

Exposes two hooks on window:
- __debugPushError(msg): lets app code surface errors to the pre-React
  pill if it wants to
- __debugClearLoadTimer(): called by RootLayoutNav's mount effect
  alongside setting __appHealthy. Cancels the 20s timer AND removes
  any pill that already appeared during boot — otherwise a transient
  early error that resolves on mount would leave a stale red banner.

Deviation from task snippet: the task only cleared the timer. I also
remove the DOM node on clear, because "app loaded but a banner says
it didn't" is strictly worse than showing nothing. Kept the 20s
timeout per the task despite knowing the 15s recovery preempts it
first-load — it's useful on the final reload attempt.

Structural verification: in the built dist/index.html, the boot IIFE
sits at byte 1570 (first inline script), before earlyInstallCapture
(1885), recoveryScript (2081), and the bundle <script src> (31906).

tsc --noEmit: 0 errors. jest: 63/63. expo export --platform web:
clean.

commits.subject: feat(debug): capture console.error / console.warn in the debug log
commits.body:
Patches console.error / console.warn at module load (web-only) so the
Log tab reflects everything React and app code log at error/warn
severity — PropType failures, hydration mismatches, key warnings,
caught exceptions, etc. — alongside manual debugAdd calls.

- Add 'console' to the DebugSource union; add a zinc-gray hex to the
  SOURCE palette in DebugPill.
- describeConsoleArgs() maps the first arg to the entry's `event`
  (truncated at 160 chars) and subsequent args to `details`.
- formatArg() serialises args safely: Error → stack/message, primitive
  → String(), object → JSON.stringify with a circular-ref replacer
  that also collapses React elements to '[ReactElement]' (would
  otherwise dump huge objects). Unserialisable values fall back to
  '[Unserializable]'.
- reentrant flag prevents infinite loops (e.g. a React subscriber
  logs a console.warn during render, which would retrigger the patch).
- __debugPatched sentinel makes the patch idempotent under Fast
  Refresh; without it, a module re-execute would wrap the already-
  wrapped console.error as the "original" and eventually recurse.
- Native skipped: RN's LogBox patches console itself at RN boot and
  module order is unreliable — whoever patches last wins, so cross-
  platform capture would silently lose entries mid-session.
- Original console.error / console.warn are still called afterwards,
  so DevTools' red/yellow message rendering is preserved.

Deviation from task snippet: task used debugAdd({ ... }) object form
and details: { args } (raw object). Existing debugAdd is positional
with details: string, used at 10 callsites. Reconciled by writing a
small helper that respects the existing API instead of migrating it.

commits.subject: refactor(debug): replace className with inline styles + hex literals in DebugPill
commits.body:
The pill is a diagnostic tool — it's most needed when *other* things
are broken, and a stylesheet load failure is exactly one of those
things. Previously every structural element used DaisyUI className
tokens, which silently no-op if Tailwind/Uniwind CSS fails to load.

Changes:
- Remove all 40 className usages on View / Text / TouchableOpacity /
  ScrollView in DebugPill.tsx.
- Remove the SEVERITY_BG / SEVERITY_TEXT / SOURCE_TEXT className token
  maps; replace with C (surface/text/primary), SEVERITY, and SOURCE
  hex-literal maps.
- Expand StyleSheet.create to cover every reusable element (collapsed
  pill, tabs, log rows, env/pwa tabs, footer). Colour and layout are
  co-located so each element is independent of any stylesheet.
- Single dark palette chosen to read against both dark and light app
  backgrounds. Deliberate tradeoff: the pill no longer auto-themes,
  but it renders correctly when the app stylesheet is blocked/missing.

Verification added: TESTING_GUIDE.md step 47 — DevTools block the
`_expo/static/css/global-*.css` request and reload; main app loses
its theme (expected), but the pill still renders correctly.

clean; CSS bundle size dropped 26.3 → 25.3 kB as a side effect.

commits.subject: chore(types): wire Uniwind className augmentation + fix 2 lingering casts
commits.body:
tsc --noEmit went from 317 errors to 0.

Root cause: Uniwind ships its className augmentation in
node_modules/uniwind/types.d.ts but its package "types" export points
only at dist/module/index.d.ts, which re-exports runtime types and
skips the augmentation. TS therefore never saw className on ViewProps,
TextProps, TouchableOpacityProps, etc. — producing ~315 cascading
TS2769/TS2322 errors across the codebase.

Fix: add uniwind-env.d.ts at the project root with
  /// <reference types="uniwind/types" />
which is the standard pattern Uniwind's quickstart assumes and mirrors
the next-env.d.ts / expo-env.d.ts convention.

Remaining 2 errors were real latent bugs: direct casts from Window /
Navigator to Record<string, unknown>, which TS 5 rejects. Replaced
with the shape-cast pattern already used elsewhere in _layout.tsx.

unmounted the root layout took it with it — including the very case
where it's most useful. Mount it into <div id="debug-root"> via its

separate-DOM-root concept doesn't apply on iOS/Android.

components into any DOM node once the style registry is initialised)
rather than duplicating ~450 lines as a DOM-native variant.

commits.subject: feat(a11y): extract useArrowNavigation + complete search modal wiring
commits.body:
Three related improvements, one commit:

1. Extract useArrowNavigation hook
   Arrow navigation in BurgerMenu was inline. HeaderFilters now needs
   the same behavior, so extract into a shared hook alongside
   useDisclosureFocus and useFocusTrap. Reuses FOCUSABLE_SELECTOR for
   "what counts as focusable". BurgerMenu refactored to use it.

2. Thread search button ref through SearchModalProvider
   The SearchButton lives in HeaderFilters; the search modal body
   lives in the home screen. A shared triggerRef on the context lets
   useDisclosureFocus return focus to the button on close instead of
   dropping to document.body. useDisclosureFocus gained an optional
   overrides argument for this cross-component case.

3. Search modal dialog semantics + aria-controls
   Panel gets role="dialog", aria-modal="true", id="search-panel",
   and accessibilityLabel="Search location". SearchButton gets
   aria-controls + aria-expanded (via accessibilityState.expanded).
   Parallels the BurgerMenu aria wiring.

HeaderFilters dropdown also gains arrow navigation via the new hook.

Remaining: TutorialModal + InstallInstructionsModal — open from
inside BurgerMenu, focus-return semantics need discussion.

commits.subject: feat(a11y): arrow keys + aria-controls on burger menu
commits.body:
Arrow/Home/End cycle between focusable menu items while the menu is
open. Home jumps to the first, End to the last; ArrowUp wraps past the
top, ArrowDown wraps past the bottom. Handler attaches to the menu
container (via containerRef) and short-circuits on native.

Selector-based query (same as useFocusTrap) instead of role="menuitem"
— this menu is a WAI-ARIA disclosure, not a role="menu" application
menu (see file header for rationale). role="menuitem" without a parent
role="menu" would be invalid ARIA and contradict the documented design.

aria-controls wires the trigger to the panel. Panel id is spread
web-only; on native, the Pressable doesn't receive unknown props.
aria-expanded continues to come from accessibilityState.expanded.
The panel is conditionally mounted — aria-controls resolves at
activation time per WAI-ARIA, so the stale reference is fine.

Theme toggle task was already implemented (lines 266-286) — no
changes needed there.

commits.subject: feat(a11y): keyboard focus for BurgerMenu disclosure
commits.body:
Adds two reusable hooks for the standard disclosure pattern:
- useDisclosureFocus: moves focus to the first item on open, returns it
  to the trigger on close.
- useFocusTrap: cycles Tab / Shift+Tab within the container while open.

Hooks follow the project convention of a plain .ts native stub plus a
.web.ts implementation — DOM-only APIs are isolated from native bundles
and the consumer sees a single import path on both platforms.

Wired into BurgerMenu: triggerRef on the hamburger Pressable, firstItemRef
on the "How to use" item, containerRef on the menu card. Other disclosures
(header dropdowns, filter panels) can adopt the same hooks later.

Public ref types are React.RefObject<any> — RN Web forwards refs to the
underlying DOM node on web and to the RN component instance on native,
so a permissive type is honest rather than requiring every call site
to cast.

commits.subject: feat(pwa): detect icon-only deploys, strengthen tripwire, dedupe precache
commits.body:
Follow-up on cross-repo cleanup sweep — address every self-review item:

- OS icon cache mitigation: new useIconRefresh composable watches iconsHash
  in version.json and surfaces a reinstall banner when (a) the app is
  running standalone AND (b) the current hash differs from the
  last-acknowledged hash. "Show how" opens InstallInstructionsModal with
  the reinstall collapsible pre-expanded (new expand-reinstall prop);
  "Dismiss" acknowledges the current hash so the banner stops reappearing
  until icons change again. First run after a clean install records the
  current hash as acknowledged so users aren't flagged for icons they've
  never seen another version of.
- Version manifest: versionJsonPlugin now takes iconsHash and emits
  { buildTime, iconsHash } so icon-only deploys become detectable without
  relying on sw.js revision bumps.
- Workbox precache dedup: dropped redundant includeAssets since
  globPatterns already sweeps ico/png/svg from /public. Precache count
  34 → 31 entries — favicon.ico, favicon-48x48.png, apple-touch-icon.png
  no longer listed twice in sw.js.
- Tripwire strengthened (iconCacheBust.test.ts, 6 → 9 tests):
  • Source-level: scans the manifest.icons block and fails on any bare
    string src (earlier version only checked 3 hardcoded paths, so a new
    icon added without versioned() slipped through).
  • Source-level: asserts versionJsonPlugin(ICONS_HASH) wiring.
  • Dist-level: verifies sw.js precache has no duplicate URLs.
  • Dist-level: verifies version.json carries iconsHash.
  Verified by breaking the bare-string guard (test fails) then restoring
  (test passes).
- Save-as-PDF rationale: strengthened handlePrint() comment to explain
  why the per-workspace actions menu beats the burger-menu alternative
  — printing only makes sense on a workspace page, and a global burger
  entry would either print nothing off-workspace or need route-aware
  visibility. CLAUDE.md Project Status updated to match.
- Docs: CLAUDE.md Project Status gained a PWA icon cache-busting bullet;
  README.md tree lists the new composable; SESSION_NOTES rewritten.

All 115 tests pass, vue-tsc clean, npm run build emits versioned hrefs
in dist/index.html, dist/manifest.webmanifest icon srcs, dist/sw.js
precache with zero duplicate URLs, and dist/version.json with iconsHash.

Tags: pwa, ux, infra, test, docs
Complexity: 3
Urgency: 2
Impact: user-facing
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: cross-repo-cleanup
Semver: minor

- Content-hash every PWA icon URL: iconCacheBustHtml() Vite plugin rewrites

  same ?v=<sha256-8> into the manifest. Workbox now strips the v param on

  surfaced. Vue reactivity + Dexie liveQuery + postMessage cover the
  current demand.

commits.subject: refactor(pwa): use UA for reinstall platform detection; refresh session notes
commits.body:
The reinstall collapsible previously classified platform from
instructions.browser (display string). That string includes (iOS)/
(Mobile)/(Desktop) for Safari and Firefox but NOT for Chrome/Edge/Brave/
Opera — bare "Chrome" covers both desktop and Android. Android Chromium
users who hit the manual-install fallback (5-second diagnostic timeout
when beforeinstallprompt doesn't fire) would see desktop reinstall
steps ("click ⋮ menu → Uninstall") instead of the Android flow.

Switch getReinstallSteps() to navigator.userAgent directly. No new

commits.subject: feat(pwa): add icon cache busting + tripwire + reinstall guidance
commits.body:
Stable icon filenames (pwa-192x192.png, apple-touch-icon.png, favicon.png,
icon.svg) survive browser cache, CDN, Workbox precache, and Chrome WebAPK
caches for weeks after a deploy — users see stale icons until each layer
decides to refresh. Append ?v=<sha256-prefix> to every icon URL so a new
icon is a new URL at every cache layer; hash-derived means the version
only bumps when bytes change (no spurious WebAPK regens).

Implementation (vite.config.js):
- iconVersion(path): sha256 prefix of public/<path>, warns-not-throws on
  missing file so first-time clones before generate-icons don't break dev.
- iconCacheBustHtml(): Vite plugin that rewrites the four icon <link>
  literals in index.html. Throws on missing literal so a tag-formatting
  change can't silently ship un-versioned URLs.
- Manifest icons: use versioned() helper for all four entries.
- Workbox: add cleanupOutdatedCaches: true and /^v$/ to
  ignoreURLParametersMatching — without /^v$/, precache misses versioned
  requests and offline breaks.
- Plugin order: iconCacheBustHtml() runs before VitePWA() to lock the
  contract against future plugin behavior changes.

Tripwire (src/__tests__/iconCacheBust.test.js):
- Source-level: plugin defined, wired before VitePWA, ignore param wired,
  cleanupOutdatedCaches wired, index.html still has the four literals.
- Dist-level (skipped if dist/ absent): manifest icons versioned,
  index.html links versioned, sw.js has both workbox config pieces.
- Verified: breaking /^v$/ → test fails. Restoring → test passes.

User communication (InstallInstructionsModal.jsx):
OS icon cache is the one layer no web-side change refreshes. Added
a DaisyUI collapse (closed by default) with platform-tailored reinstall
steps — iOS long-press → Remove App, Android long-press → Uninstall,
Desktop → app menu → Uninstall. Platform classified from the existing
instructions.browser display string (no duplicate UA detection).

Pattern source: glow-props/docs/implementations/PWA_ICON_CACHE_BUST.md.

commits.subject: fix(pwa): strengthen icon cache-bust — assertions, warning, tripwire, user note
commits.body:
Self-review pass on the cache-bust fix. Five changes addressing real bugs,
overstated rationale, missing test coverage, and missing user comms.

vite.config.js — `iconCacheBustHtml` no longer fails silently:
  Original used `String.replace` on literal hrefs. When the literal isn't
  found, replace returns the original string with no error — a future
  reformat of dashboard/index.html (single-quoted attrs, attribute
  reorder, query already present, leading slash dropped) would have
  shipped a manifest with versioned icons but a <head> with un-versioned
  ones, surfacing only on the next icon change after deploy. Plugin now
  walks an explicit REPLACEMENTS table and throws with a clear message
  pointing at the affected literal.

vite.config.js — `iconVersion` warns on missing icons:
  Previously returned '0' silently if a file was missing. A deleted-by-
  accident icon would have shipped with `?v=0` and 404'd at runtime. Now
  console.warn'd at config-load time pointing at `npm run generate-icons`.
  Doesn't throw — fresh-clone dev runs legitimately have no icons before
  the first generate, and CI builds always run generate first.

vite.config.js — corrected `cleanupOutdatedCaches` rationale:
  Original commit message and comment claimed it "drops the previous
  build's precache entries on activation." That's wrong per Workbox
  source — it only deletes cache stores whose names use a different
  `workbox-precache-*` prefix (cross-major-version cleanup). Same-prefix
  entries with stale revisions are already replaced by Workbox's normal
  install flow. Option is still kept (defends against future Workbox
  major bumps); comment now describes what it actually does.

scripts/__tests__/icon-cache-bust.test.mjs — new tripwire (9 tests):
  Two layers, mirroring the daisyui-surfaces pattern. Source assertions
  always run: iconCacheBustHtml plugin defined, wired before VitePWA in
  plugins[], workbox.cleanupOutdatedCaches:true, /^v$/ in
  ignoreURLParametersMatching, dashboard/index.html still contains the
  exact literal hrefs the plugin replaces. Built-output assertions skip
  with a logged warning when dist/ is absent, otherwise verify every
  manifest icon URL has ?v=<8-hex>, every <link rel="icon|apple-touch-icon">
  href has ?v=<8-hex>, no un-versioned icon URLs leaked through, and
  dist/sw.js contains both cleanupOutdatedCaches() and /^v$/ inside
  ignoreURLParametersMatching. Test count 65 -> 74.

InstallInstructionsModal.jsx — OS-cache user-facing note:
  Added a collapsed <details> block ("Already installed and the icon
  looks outdated?") explaining in plain language that the OS keeps app
  icons cached separately from the browser, so clearing site data alone
  won't refresh them — the user must remove the app first. Collapsed by
  default to avoid confusing first-time installers; discoverable for the
  small subset who hit the issue.

docs — HISTORY.md gets a strengthening entry AND the original cache-bust
narrative is corrected in place (cleanupOutdatedCaches rationale).
SESSION_NOTES.md updated with current state.

Verified: `vite build` clean; `npm test` 74/74 pass against fresh dist;
manifest, HTML link tags, and SW all carry the expected versioned URLs +
workbox config.

Tags: pwa, caching, icons, workbox, testing, ux, refactor
Complexity: 3
Urgency: 2
Impact: user-facing
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: pwa-resilience
Semver: patch
commits.title: strengthen icon cache-bust — assertions, warning, tripwire, user note

commits.subject: fix(pwa): cache-bust icon URLs via content hash so re-install shows new icon
commits.body:
Re-installing the PWA (or clearing site data) was still showing the old
icon because the icon URLs (`assets/images/icon-192.png`,
`apple-touch-icon.png`, `favicon.ico`, ...) never changed — the browser
HTTP cache, Vercel CDN, Workbox precache, and Chrome's WebAPK shadow all
kept serving the previous bytes.

vite.config.js:
- SHA-256 each icon in `dashboard/public/` at config-load time, take the
  first 8 chars, and append `?v=<hash>` to the three manifest icon
  entries and the three static `<link>` tags in `index.html` (via a new
  `iconCacheBustHtml` transformIndexHtml plugin).
- Add `workbox.cleanupOutdatedCaches: true` so the SW drops the previous
  build's precache entries on activation. This was missing entirely —
  caught during a glow-props PWA_SYSTEM.md pattern review.
- Add `workbox.ignoreURLParametersMatching: [/^utm_/, /^v$/]` so the
  versioned URLs still match the precached `icon-192.png` / etc. entries
  (Workbox would otherwise miss `?v=...` and fall through to network).

Hash is content-derived → bumps automatically when icons regenerate, no
manual version constant to forget. New URL → fresh fetch at every cache
layer the web app controls; new manifest icon URL → Chrome WebAPK regen
on next update check. OS-level icon caches (Springboard, Android
launcher, Windows icon cache, macOS Icon Services) are platform-
controlled and still require a full PWA uninstall — documented inline.

Verified: `dist/manifest.webmanifest` icons render as
`icon-192.png?v=de08cd78`, `dist/index.html` link tags render as
`favicon.png?v=05809e07`, `dist/sw.js` contains
`cleanupOutdatedCaches()` and
`ignoreURLParametersMatching:[/^utm_/,/^v$/]`. 65/65 tests pass.

Tags: pwa, caching, icons, workbox, vite-config
Complexity: 2
Urgency: 3
Impact: user-facing
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: pwa-resilience
Semver: patch

commits.subject: refactor(hamburger): drop max-w bracket value — use JS-measured maxWidth
commits.body:
Removes the `max-w-[calc(100vw-2rem)]` arbitrary bracket value
exception from CLAUDE.md. The dropdown's max-width is now computed
at runtime from `window.innerWidth - 32` in the same
`useLayoutEffect` measurement pass that computes `top`/`left` portal
positioning — applied as an inline `style={{ maxWidth }}` alongside
the existing portal position props.

Before:
  className="... max-w-[calc(100vw-2rem)] ..."
  style={{ top, left }}

After:
  className="... (no max-w bracket value) ..."
  style={{ top, left, maxWidth }}

Why this is strictly better than the CSS approach:

1. `window.innerWidth` excludes scrollbar width on browsers where
   the scrollbar overlaps the viewport (mobile Safari, Chrome
   on iOS/Android). CSS `100vw` includes the scrollbar, so the
   previous approach left a slightly wider-than-intended max on
   those browsers. The JS version is tighter and correct.

2. The `maxWidth` is already being computed in the same
   `useLayoutEffect` that measures the trigger button's
   `getBoundingClientRect` — zero additional DOM reads. The
   measurement runs on open, on resize, and on scroll (with
   passive + capture for nested scroll containers), so viewport
   changes are always reflected.

3. Eliminates the fourth (and last removable) arbitrary bracket
   value exception from CLAUDE.md. The remaining 3 exceptions
   are genuine capability gaps where stock Tailwind has no
   equivalent: z-21 (between z-20 and z-30), z-70 (above
   Tailwind's z-50 ceiling), and grid-cols-[auto_repeat(7,1fr)]
   (mixed auto + repeat() grid template). None can be eliminated
   by moving to JS without losing the utility-class benefit.

CLAUDE.md bracket-value exception list trimmed from 4 items to 3.
The removed entry carries a one-line note explaining the
replacement for traceability.

Tripwire test `ALLOWED_BRACKETS` set updated from 4 items to 3.
Comment added explaining the removal.

Files touched:
  - dashboard/js/components/HamburgerMenu.jsx — updatePosition
    now computes maxWidth; className drops max-w-[calc(...)]
  - CLAUDE.md — exception list entry replaced with removal note
  - scripts/__tests__/daisyui-surfaces.test.mjs — allowlist trimmed

Build clean, 63/63 tests pass.

Tags: refactor, vanilla-daisyui, hamburger-menu
Complexity: 2
Urgency: 2
Impact: user-facing
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: post-audit-cleanup
Semver: patch

out of scope. The file contained ~700 lines of pure aggregation

Split into two new library modules under `scripts/lib/`:

  scripts/lib/aggregateCalcs.js (289 lines, NEW)

    calcComplexityBreakdown, calcUrgencyBreakdown,
    calcImpactBreakdown, calcRiskBreakdown, calcDebtBreakdown,
    calcEpicBreakdown, calcRepoCommitCounts, calcHourlyHeatmap,
    calcSemverBreakdown, calcCodeStats, calcAverage, calcDateRange,
    calcFilterOptions. All take a `commits` array and return a
    summary object with no dependency on module-level state.

  scripts/lib/aggregateTimeWindows.js (231 lines, NEW)
    9 time-bucketing functions: getISOWeekKey, getUTCDateKey,
    getUTCMonthKey, accumulateBucket, createEmptyBucket,
    finalizeBucket, calcWeeklyAggregations, calcDailyAggregations,
    calcMonthlyAggregations. Internal helpers (bucket system) are
    module-private; time-key generators and the three windowed
    aggregators are exported.

  scripts/aggregate-processed.js (556 lines, trimmed from 1042)
    Keeps: config, author mapping (loadAuthorMap, resolveAuthorId,
    getAuthorInfo — these depend on module-level emailToCanonical
    state), data loading (validateCommit, loadRepoCommits,
    loadAllRepos), calcContributorAggregations (depends on
    resolveAuthorId/getAuthorInfo), generateAggregation

commits.subject: docs(claude): scope note — index.html pre-React hex/inline-style is out of policy
commits.body:
Retrospective on the 3-audit-pass cleanup noticed a clarity gap: the
CLAUDE.md hex-literal and inline-style rules say "in JSX or JS data
constants" and "in JSX" respectively, but a future reviewer looking
at `dashboard/index.html` would see inline `style="..."` blocks with
hex literals (`#333`, `#2D68FF`, `#767676`, `#e5e7eb`, `#f59e0b`)
and a `@keyframes spin` block, and might think the policy was being
violated.

Those inline styles are INTENTIONAL:

  - Pre-React loading spinner (lines ~108-117) renders before
    `createRoot()` executes, before the main CSS bundle loads.
    A Tailwind-class-based spinner would be invisible in that
    pre-React loading window because the class names wouldn't
    resolve yet.
  - `<noscript>` fallback (~line 116) renders only when JavaScript
    is disabled entirely — the CSS bundle also depends on JS (via
    Vite's module loading), so Tailwind classes wouldn't work.
  - `@keyframes spin` inline `<style>` block provides the spinner
    animation without needing the external CSS file to load.
  - PWA early-capture warnings (~line 321) in the flash-prevention
    script use inline hex for the same reason.

Added a "Scope note" sub-bullet to the hex-literal exception list in
CLAUDE.md explaining that `dashboard/index.html` is a separate layer
with its own pre-React rules. Future reviewers now have explicit
documentation saying "yes, that's intentional, don't refactor it."

This is a pure documentation clarification — no code change, no test
change. Build and tests unaffected (63/63 pass).

Tags: documentation, audit, policy, scope
Complexity: 1
Urgency: 2
Impact: internal
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: post-audit-cleanup
Semver: patch
commits.title: scope note — index.html pre-React hex/inline-style is out of policy

commits.subject: test(tripwire): enforce CLAUDE.md exception lists at source level
commits.body:
Retrospective on the 3-audit-pass cleanup caught a gap: the tripwire
verifies DaisyUI component-class usage and the custom-CSS allowlist,
but does NOT enforce the CLAUDE.md "Frontend: Styles and Scripts"
exception lists I just spent three passes locking down. Without
machine-verified invariants, a future contributor can drift right
past the rules.

Added three regression guards to
`scripts/__tests__/daisyui-surfaces.test.mjs` — one per high-value
exception category. Each test:
  - Scans every `.js` / `.jsx` file under `dashboard/js/`
  - Extracts matching patterns from stripped source (comments removed
    so rationale blocks mentioning forbidden patterns don't false-fire)
  - Fails with a specific list of offenders and instructions for how
    to think about adding a new exception vs removing the offender

### Test 1: arbitrary bracket value allowlist

Allowlist (set in the test):
  - `z-[var(--z-sticky-header)]`
  - `z-[var(--z-toast)]`
  - `grid-cols-[auto_repeat(7,1fr)]`
  - `max-w-[calc(100vw-2rem)]`

Matches any Tailwind-style `utility-[...]` in a JSX className. Uses
the classname attribute extractor to avoid false-positives from JS
array subscript syntax (`foo[i]`). A new bracket value triggers the
test and requires either rounding to stock or adding a 5th documented
exception with its own rationale in CLAUDE.md.

### Test 2: hex literal scope

Allowlist (set in the test):
  - `dashboard/js/components/DebugPill.jsx` (isolated React root,
    survives CSS load failure)
  - `dashboard/js/components/debug/` (entire subtree — same
    resilience concern as DebugPill.jsx)
  - `dashboard/js/generated/themeMeta.js` (auto-generated PWA meta
    colours — browser parses `<meta content="">` as literal hex)

Matches `#[0-9a-fA-F]{6}` in any file outside the allowlist. Stripped
source excludes comments, so the comment block describing the
2026-04-15 `themes.js #808080` removal doesn't trigger. Would have
caught the gap I noticed during the retrospective: if the DebugPill
subsystem expanded to a new file outside `components/debug/`, the
test would flag it as an unapproved hex location.

### Test 3: JSX heading font-mono requirement

Matches `<h[123]\b[^>]*>` and fails if the opening tag doesn't
contain `font-mono` in its classNames. Enforces the contract that
replaced the `h1, h2, h3 { font-family: mono }` element-selector rule
removed in commit 5bcc2c2. `<h4>` through `<h6>` are intentionally
excluded — the old rule targeted h1-h3 only, and h4+ uses inherited
body font.

A future new heading without font-mono will fail the test with
`file:line: <h2 className="text-lg">` offender format, prompting the
contributor to add the class or promote to h4+ if monospace is
undesired in that spot.

### Implementation notes

- Pure Node — no external deps (we're in `node:test`-land, not
  vitest-land). Added a `walkFiles()` recursive walker that uses
  `readdirSync({withFileTypes: true})` for efficient directory
  traversal.
- Uses `createRequire(import.meta.url)` from `node:module` to get
  at `require` for sync fs operations — the existing builtCss loader
  at the top of the file does the same thing via top-level await.
- Stripped-source comment removal is the same regex pair used in the
  existing "styles.css allowlist" test. Keeps behaviour consistent
  with how other regression guards handle comments.
- Sanity-checked the regexes against synthetic violations outside the
  test suite before committing — all three catch planted bad
  patterns while allowing the current 4-bracket / 2-hex-location /
  4-heading-with-font-mono state.

Verified:
  - All 63 tests pass (was 60, now +3)
  - Build clean
  - No new false positives on the current tree

Tags: testing, tripwire, audit, regression-guard
Complexity: 3
Urgency: 2
Impact: internal
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: post-audit-cleanup
Semver: patch
commits.title: enforce CLAUDE.md exception lists at source level

commits.subject: docs(claude): consolidate exception lists after third-pass cleanup
commits.body:
Third audit-pass summary commit. The previous 5 commits in this
series removed four of the documented CLAUDE.md exceptions by
changing the code itself — this commit rewrites the exception list
blocks to reflect the final state, plus rolls up the session notes
and history for the whole pass.

CLAUDE.md "Frontend: Styles and Scripts" changes:

  - **Element-selector exceptions** collapsed from 3 entries to 1.
    The `* { font-family }` reset (commit 12cd65d) and the
    `h1, h2, h3 { font-mono }` heading rule (commit 5bcc2c2) are
    both gone. `body::before` decorative grid is the only remaining
    element-selector rule — and it's a pseudo-element, so it
    genuinely cannot be expressed as a Tailwind utility.

  - **Hex literal exceptions** collapsed from 3 entries to 2. The
    `themes.js #808080` fallback (commit 0cc5d6e) was replaced with
    `META_COLORS[DEFAULT_LIGHT_THEME]`, so `themes.js` source code
    contains zero hex literals now. DebugPill subsystem and the
    auto-generated `themeMeta.js` remain as documented exceptions,
    both with capability-gap rationales (isolated React root
    resilience, browser meta-tag spec limitation respectively).

  - **Static inline-style exceptions** collapsed from 2 entries to
    1. The root ErrorBoundary in main.jsx (commit aea7c43) is now
    pure Tailwind utilities; DebugPill subsystem remains as the
    only static-inline-style exception because the resilience
    argument there is genuinely load-bearing — the pill's whole
    purpose is to be a diagnostic surface when CSS has failed.

  - **Arbitrary bracket value exceptions: 4, unchanged.** All four
    remaining bracket values are genuine capability gaps:
      * `z-[var(--z-sticky-header)]` (z-21) / `z-[var(--z-toast)]`
        (z-70) — design-token z-scale values that stock Tailwind's
        integer 10 scale doesn't cover.
      * `grid-cols-[auto_repeat(7,1fr)]` — functional grid row
        alignment that can't be expressed with stock grid-cols-N.
      * `max-w-[calc(100vw-2rem)]` — Tailwind has no max-width
        utility that takes a calc() input.

    None of these are removable without losing a capability or
    collapsing the z-scale in a way that sacrifices real stacking
    distinctions.

Each removed exception has a one-line reference to the removal
commit in the updated list blocks so a future reader can trace
the history without checking git log.

Documentation rollup:

  - **docs/SESSION_NOTES.md**: third-pass section added with 6
    commit summaries + an "after the third pass, the exception
    list is:" subsection with before/after counts per category.
    File-size posture updated (styles.css 177 → 175 after the
    second element-selector removal).

  - **docs/HISTORY.md**: new "Third audit-cleanup pass" entry at
    the top of the 2026-04-15 section with paragraph-per-commit
    summaries for all 6 commits. Keeps the first-pass and
    second-pass entries unchanged below.

  - **docs/TODO.md** footer: rewritten to describe all three
    passes (13 + 8 + 6 commits) and the final exception count.
    Notes that no pure design-preference exceptions remain — every
    remaining exception has a stated capability-gap or resilience
    rationale, so a future "remove exception X" ask should be
    approached as a design change, not a cleanup.

Verified:
  - Build clean (879.86 kB)
  - 60/60 tests pass
  - `dashboard/styles.css` is 175 lines (down from the 177 line
    count in the second-pass posture note — two element-selector
    blocks trimmed)

commits.subject: chore(dashboard): delete 5 stale aggregate artefacts at dashboard/ root
commits.body:
Resolves the `scripts/aggregate.js` data-flow investigation that was
parked in TODO.md after the first audit pass.

Investigation findings:

  1. **scripts/aggregate.js is NOT dead code.** It's the
     multi-repository aggregation script for the @data / admin
     persona, documented in 4 places:
       - docs/ADMIN_GUIDE.md (primary workflow guide, 4 references)
       - docs/DATA_OPERATIONS.md (1 reference)
       - docs/DISCOVERY_SESSION.md (persona mapping)
       - docs/CODE_REVIEW.md (review notes)
     It's a hand-run CLI tool, not part of any npm script or CI
     workflow. No repository has an automated pipeline around it.

  2. **aggregate.js does NOT write to dashboard/.** Its default
     output directory is `aggregated/` (hardcoded at line 21:
     `let outputDir = 'aggregated'`). The docs show examples using
     `--output=combined` and similar — none target `dashboard/`.

  3. **The 5 stale JSON files at dashboard/ root are artefacts of a
     deprecated workflow.** Specifically:
       - dashboard/commits.json       (870 kB)
       - dashboard/contributors.json  (2 kB)
       - dashboard/files.json         (87 kB)
       - dashboard/metadata.json      (1 kB)
       - dashboard/summary.json       (2 kB)
     First committed 2026-02-11 (`546f487` merge PR #61), last
     touched 2026-04-02 (`6fc20cf` chatty-chart purge). Not gitignored.
     Zero consumers anywhere in the tree:
       - `grep -rn '/commits\.json\|/contributors\.json|...' dashboard/
         scripts/ vite*.js package.json | grep -v public/data` finds
         only self-references (the JSON files contain old commit
         messages that quote these filenames as text, not as imports).
       - `dashboard/js/` grep for any runtime loader: zero matches.
     These files were the output of an OLDER aggregate.js invocation
     (probably `--output=dashboard`) back when the dashboard loaded
     from `dashboard/commits.json` at runtime, before the 2026-02
     restructure to `dashboard/public/data/` served by Vite.
     `scripts/aggregate-processed.js` now writes to
     `dashboard/public/` (verified — line 28:
     `DEFAULT_OUTPUT = path.join(__dirname, '..', 'dashboard', 'public')`),
     which is the ONLY data-flow feeding the runtime dashboard.

Action taken:

  - Deleted the 5 stale JSON files. Total reclaimed: ~960 kB.
  - `aggregate.js` kept as-is — it's a documented tool with a real
    consumer (the admin/maintainer persona). Its header comment
    block still describes its intended use and doesn't need
    "consumer documentation" since the docs already have it.
  - `aggregate-processed.js` kept as-is — it's the active runtime
    data pipeline.
  - TODO.md investigation entry removed (resolved).

Verified after deletion:
  - Build clean (879.95 kB → 879.86 kB — the PWA precache dropped
    0.09 kB because the 5 files were being precached by the service
    worker's default glob)
  - 60/60 tests pass
  - Dashboard still loads data from `dashboard/public/data/*.json`
    via the existing data-loading path in App.jsx (unchanged)

commits.subject: refactor(themes): getMetaColor fallback uses default theme's hex, not #808080
commits.body:
Removes the `themes.js #808080` entry from the hex-literal exception
list in CLAUDE.md. The fallback existed because getMetaColor() needed
to return *some* hex string when a theme name wasn't in the META_COLORS
map — but the hardcoded neutral gray violated the vanilla-DaisyUI
"no hex literals in JS" rule, even though it was documented as an
exception.

Before:
    export function getMetaColor(themeName) {
        return META_COLORS[themeName] || '#808080';
    }

After: primary lookup against META_COLORS, debug-log on miss, then
fall back to `META_COLORS[DEFAULT_LIGHT_THEME]`. The default-theme
lookup is guaranteed by the generator's own build-time validation —
`scripts/generate-theme-meta.mjs` refuses to regenerate the file if
DEFAULT_LIGHT_THEME isn't in the generated META_COLORS map, so this
code path is safe by construction.

The new fallback has three properties the old gray didn't:

  1. The returned hex matches an actual theme's `--color-base-100`
     value (lofi's `#ebebeb` today), so the PWA status bar looks
     coherent instead of "stuck at #808080 grey". Matters during the
     window when a theme is added to scripts/theme-config.js but the
     prebuild regenerator hasn't run yet — the meta tag shows the
     default theme's colour, not a jarring neutral.

  2. The source file dashboard/js/themes.js now contains ZERO hex
     literals. Verified via grep — the only remaining `#` match is in
     a comment describing the removal. The CLAUDE.md exception can be
     dropped in the consolidation commit.

  3. The debug-pill "log" tab now surfaces any unreachable-branch hit
     via `debugAdd('theme', 'warn', ...)`, so if the generator ever
     produces an inconsistent META_COLORS map the anomaly appears in
     the diagnostic surface instead of silently rendering grey.

The Approach/Alternatives comment block above the function enumerates
all four approaches that were considered (throw, gray literal, null,
default lookup) so a future reader sees the reasoning without having
to re-derive it from the commit history.

Verified:
  - Build clean (879.90 kB → 880.02 kB, +0.12 kB for the expanded
    comment + debug-add call)
  - 60/60 tests pass
  - `grep '#[0-9a-fA-F]\{6\}' dashboard/js/themes.js` now matches only
    the comment that describes the removal — no hex literals in code

Removes the second element-selector exception in styles.css. The
`h1, h2, h3 { font-family: var(--font-mono) }` rule existed because
adding `font-mono` to every heading individually would be repetitive
boilerplate — but a repo-wide grep found only 4 JSX headings in the
entire dashboard tree, well within the "do it by hand" threshold.

Heading inventory (4 sites total):

  dashboard/js/components/Header.jsx             h1  "Git Analytics"
  dashboard/js/components/QuickGuide.jsx         h2  step titles
  dashboard/js/components/InstallInstructionsModal.jsx  h2  "Install App"
  dashboard/js/components/DropZone.jsx           h1  "Git Analytics Dashboard"

DropZone.jsx already had `font-mono` on its h1 (pre-existing). The
other three were relying on the styles.css rule. Added explicit
`font-mono` utility class to each. CollapsibleSection's title prop
renders as a `<span>`, not a heading element — section titles were
not affected by the old rule and don't need a change.

The single `<h4>` in sections/Projects.jsx is intentionally left
without font-mono because the old styles.css rule targeted h1/h2/h3
only. No behavioural change.

    (`body::before` decorative grid — pseudo-elements can't be
    Tailwind utilities)

    a single entry in the consolidation commit at the end of this

    Tailwind's JIT for each JSX consumer of the utility)

commits.subject: refactor(errorboundary): root fallback uses Tailwind utilities, not inline style
commits.body:
Removes the "Root ErrorBoundary in main.jsx" exception from the
inline-style allowlist in CLAUDE.md. The exception existed as
defensive scaffolding for the case where the main CSS bundle failed
to load entirely — but in practice, when CSS fails to load the
browser falls back to its user-agent stylesheet, which still renders
the ErrorBoundary content in a legible way (default font, top-of-
page, left-aligned). The error text is still readable, the Reload
button still works as a native button element.

The inline-style exception was buying us "centered, padded, spaced
fallback UI in the CSS-load-failure mode" at the cost of a permanent
documented exception. The new code uses pure Tailwind utility classes
for layout (`min-h-screen flex flex-col items-center justify-center
gap-4 p-6 text-center`) plus the existing DaisyUI semantic tokens
for text colour. The Reload button keeps `btn btn-primary`.

Trade-off analysis spelled out in the Approach / Alternatives
comment block above the render() method, so a future reader
considering re-adding the inline style for resilience sees the
reasoning without having to re-derive it.

After this commit:
  - DebugPill subsystem is the ONLY inline-style exception in
    CLAUDE.md (it has a stronger resilience argument — the pill's
    entire purpose is to be a diagnostic surface when things are
    broken, including the CSS-load failure mode)
  - The "Root ErrorBoundary" sub-bullet can be removed from
    CLAUDE.md's inline-style exception list in the consolidation
    commit at the end of this batch.

Verified:
  - Build clean (879.97 kB → 879.91 kB, -0.06 kB from the removed
    inline style object literal)
  - 60/60 tests pass
  - Error fallback still renders correctly (structural test — the
    render() output is still the same div + 3 p + button tree, just
    with className instead of style)

commits.subject: refactor(styles): drop * font-family reset — use body class="font-sans"
commits.body:
Removes one of the three element-selector exceptions in styles.css.
The `*` font-family reset existed because adding `font-sans` to every
JSX element individually would be repetitive boilerplate — but the
same effect is achievable by setting `font-sans` on a single ancestor
element (body) and letting CSS inheritance do the propagation.

Before:
  dashboard/styles.css:
    * { font-family: var(--font-sans); }
  dashboard/index.html:
    <body>

After:
  dashboard/styles.css:  (rule removed)
  dashboard/index.html:
    <body class="font-sans">

Why this works:
  - Tailwind v4's `.font-sans` utility emits
    `font-family: var(--font-sans)` (verified by grepping the built
    CSS bundle). The variable is defined in `:root` at line 95 of
    styles.css and Tailwind v4 auto-detects `--font-sans` /
    `--font-mono` as theme tokens by naming convention.
  - CSS inheritance propagates font-family from body to every
    descendant element by default. The previous `*` universal
    selector was doing explicit per-element application of the same
    value inheritance would have done anyway.
  - The `h1, h2, h3 { font-family: var(--font-mono) }` rule at line
    141 still overrides the inherited sans-serif for headings, so
    heading typography is unchanged. (That rule is a separate
    exception that will be addressed in a follow-on commit.)

Verified:
  - Build clean (879.95 kB → 879.97 kB, +0.02 kB for the one added
    class string in the HTML)
  - `.font-sans{font-family:var(--font-sans)}` rule present in the
    built CSS bundle
  - 60/60 tests pass

CLAUDE.md element-selector exception list updated — the numbered
list dropped from 3 entries to 2, with a note explaining where the
old `*` exception went. The 4th-pass exception-list consolidation
commit will tidy up the list further once the h1/h2/h3 removal and
ErrorBoundary conversion land.

commits.subject: refactor(context): extract reducer + filter logic to appReducer.js
commits.body:
Audit finding #14 — dashboard/js/AppContext.jsx was 579 lines, over
the 500-line soft-limit from CLAUDE.md "Code Organization" and the
largest single file in dashboard/js/. The audit pass deferred the
split because the previous round prioritized lower-risk line-count
wins (TimingHeatmap, DebugPill, Discover, Timeline). With those done,
AppContext was the only remaining over-soft-limit offender.

Split cleanly into two files at the pure-data / React boundary:

  dashboard/js/appReducer.js (285 lines, NEW)
    - DEFAULT_FILTERS (single source of truth for filter shape)
    - loadInitialState() (reads localStorage + matchMedia, validates
      theme names against themes.js allowlists, returns the reducer's
      initial state object)
    - reducer(state, action) (the full switch — every case preserved
      verbatim, including the no-op short-circuits for SET_DARK_MODE /
      SET_LIGHT_THEME / SET_DARK_THEME / SET_THEME_COLORS that skip
      re-renders when the incoming value equals the stored value)
    - filterCommits(commits, filters) (the predicate applied in
      AppProvider's filteredCommits useMemo, including the hoisted
      fromBoundMs/toBoundMs optimization from commit 4a8bd00)

  dashboard/js/AppContext.jsx (338 lines, trimmed from 579)
    - createContext + DispatchContext / AppStateContext
    - AppProvider component with every useEffect / useMemo /
      useCallback hook (persistence, dark-mode effect, cross-tab
      storage listener, system-preference fallback, active filter
      count, openDetailPane, setTheme, isMobile resize tracking)
    - useApp() + useAppDispatch() consumer hooks
    - Imports { loadInitialState, reducer, filterCommits } from
      ./appReducer.js

Why this split and not alternatives:
  - Three files (reducer / initial state / filter): Rejected —
    they're tightly coupled (the reducer references DEFAULT_FILTERS,
    loadInitialState seeds filters from it) and three files for ~280
    lines fragments logic pointlessly.
  - Move filterCommits to utils.js: Rejected — it's tied to the
    filters shape defined in this module, not a generic helper.
  - Pure split at the reducer function only (leaving loadInitialState
    and filterCommits in AppContext.jsx): Rejected — would leave
    AppContext at ~450 lines, just under the soft-limit, with no
    headroom for future growth. All three helpers are pure data with
    no React dependencies, so they belong together.

The reducer module has zero React imports — it only pulls from
utils.js (pure helpers), themes.js (validators), and chartColors.js
(runtime resolvers). This means the reducer can be unit-tested
without mounting a React tree — a future test layer could import
reducer + loadInitialState directly and assert (state, action) ->
state' behaviour, including the theme cross-tab handlers and the
no-op short-circuits.

`DEFAULT_FILTERS` is exported from appReducer.js but not re-exported
from AppContext.jsx — a repo-wide grep confirmed no component
imports DEFAULT_FILTERS from anywhere (the only reference was an
old git-history commit message in the JSON data files). If a
future component needs it for a "reset filters" action, it can
import directly from `./appReducer.js`.

Test fixup:
  scripts/__tests__/daisyui-surfaces.test.mjs "AppContext dispatches
  SET_THEME_COLORS after applyTheme()" test now reads both files:
  `case 'SET_THEME_COLORS'` in appReducer.js, dispatch call +
  resolveRuntimeAccent/Muted calls in AppContext.jsx. Added a
  sanity assertion on the import wiring between the two files so a
  future rename of the extracted module gets caught.

CLAUDE.md "Key Components" list updated with the new module + a
one-line note on AppContext's delegation.

Build clean (879.95 kB, identical post-tree-shake — the split is a
pure code-organization refactor, no behavioural change). 60/60
tests pass.

commits.subject: perf(timeline): handleCardClick builds only the count it needs
commits.body:
Audit finding #15 — `Timeline.handleCardClick('contributors')` was
building a `{ email: { name, count } }` object map by iterating
every filtered commit, then sorting the values, then reading
`sorted.length` off the sorted array to pass as a subtitle to
`openDetailPane`. The full `filteredCommits` array is passed through
as the detail-pane payload anyway — the detail pane does its own
per-author aggregation downstream — so the intermediate map and the
sort were allocating + mutating work whose only output was a
single integer (unique-author count).

Replaced with:

    const uniqueAuthors = new Set(filteredCommits.map(getAuthorEmail)).size;

One pass over the email stream, one Set, zero intermediate objects,
zero sort. Behaviour is identical (same subtitle string, same
payload). On a 10k-commit repo this drops the allocation profile
from O(n) objects + O(k log k) sort to O(n) Set inserts.

`getAuthorName` import still used at line 187 (commit-row author
name rendering in the commit list) — not touched.

commits.subject: refactor(charts): replace chartHeight inline style with utility classes
commits.body:
Audit finding #17 — every chart container in the sections/ tree used
the inline-style pattern:

    const chartHeight = isMobile ? 'NNNpx' : 'MMMpx';
    ...
    <div style={{ height: chartHeight }}>

CLAUDE.md explicitly forbids inline style={} unless values are
runtime-computed from data, and a breakpoint-based switch is not
data — it's a responsive class. Eleven call sites across five files
converted to `className={chartHeightClasses}` using stock Tailwind
h-N utilities.

Height mapping (CLAUDE.md rule: "round to nearest stock, redesign,
or drop the feature"):

  sections/Timing.jsx:   200px / 250px  → h-50 sm:h-62  (200 exact, 248 ≈ 250, -2px)
  sections/Progress.jsx: 220px / 300px  → h-55 sm:h-75  (220 exact, 300 exact)
  sections/Timeline.jsx: 220px / 300px  → h-55 sm:h-75  (220 exact, 300 exact)
  sections/Tags.jsx:     250px / 350px  → h-62 sm:h-87  (248 ≈ 250, 348 ≈ 350, -2px)

The 2px deltas at Timing (250→248) and Tags (250/350 → 248/348) are
visually imperceptible — less than a pixel on retina at default zoom.
Rounding down rather than up was chosen so there's no risk of
touching the next breakpoint transition. Timeline/Progress are exact
matches since the original design already snapped to Tailwind's
default spacing scale.

Why not add custom spacing tokens? CLAUDE.md forbids @theme tokens
— the existing policy is "use Tailwind's stock scale as-is". The
stock dynamic spacing scale (each unit = 0.25rem = 4px) covers every
height we need after snapping to the nearest integer multiple.

Verified the JIT content scanner picked up every new class — grep
over `dist/assets/index-*.css` confirms `.h-50`, `.h-55`, `.h-62`,
`.h-75`, `.h-87` rules all emitted correctly by Tailwind v4.

Bundle size impact: 879.92 KiB → 880.14 KiB (+0.22 KiB = 5 new
utility rules + their sm: variants). Inline style object
allocations per render also eliminated for all 11 call sites.

Files touched:
  - dashboard/js/sections/Timing.jsx    (2 call sites)
  - dashboard/js/sections/Progress.jsx  (2 call sites)
  - dashboard/js/sections/Timeline.jsx  (5 call sites)
  - dashboard/js/sections/Tags.jsx      (1 call site)

Contributors.jsx line 266 is DELIBERATELY left as inline style —
its height is `Math.max(200, aggregated.slice(0, 8).length *
(isMobile ? 35 : 40))px` which is runtime-computed from the number
of top contributors (1 to 8), not a breakpoint switch. CLAUDE.md
explicitly lists "chart container heights [runtime-derived]" as
an allowed inline-style use case. Not touched.

Build clean, 60/60 tests pass.

Tags: refactor, ui, performance, audit
Complexity: 2
Urgency: 2
Impact: user-facing
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: post-audit-cleanup
Semver: patch
commits.title: replace chartHeight inline style with utility classes

commits.subject: chore(hygiene): session notes shas, hamburger comment, header dep array
commits.body:
Audit findings #18, #19, #20 — three small hygiene fixes batched
together since each is a one-spot edit.

#18 SESSION_NOTES.md commit shas — the "Last Session's Work" list
had the first 6 commit shas written out but commits 7-13 were
collapsed to "7-13. —" because the shas hadn't been minted yet when
SESSION_NOTES was rewritten in commit 51d16d2. Backfilled the
remaining 7 shas (51d16d2, 2036661, 4f38b72, feb7129, f0bd185,
2f815d1, fd0985b) with one-line summaries matching their commit
messages. Also added a new "Second pass" subsection listing the
follow-on cleanup commits (09ca333 exceptions expansion, 3520a6f
dead-code sweep round 2, plus a forward reference to the remaining
commits in this batch). A future session can trace the full
branch history by reading SESSION_NOTES + git log together.

#19 HamburgerMenu.jsx stale z-[40]/z-[50] comment — the backdrop
comment still referenced the arbitrary-bracket `z-[40]` and
`z-[50]` forms, but the code migrated to stock `z-40` / `z-50`
utilities months ago (2026-04-14 vanilla-DaisyUI sweep). Rewrote
the comment to describe the actual current code: stock Tailwind
utilities that happen to line up exactly with the :root design
token scale (--z-menu-backdrop: 40, --z-menu: 50). Explicit note
that no arbitrary value is needed because the two token values
match Tailwind's stock scale exactly — this is different from
Header.jsx / Toast.jsx where z-21 / z-70 require the arbitrary
`z-[var(...)]` form because stock Tailwind has no such utility.

#20 Header.jsx menuItems useMemo dep array — was listing
`setGuideOpen` in the deps. useState setters have guaranteed stable
identity across renders (documented React guarantee), so including
them in dep arrays is noise that falsely suggests they could
change. Dropped from deps, added an inline comment explaining why
it's deliberately omitted so a future reader doesn't add it back
thinking it was an oversight.

Build clean, 60/60 tests pass.

commits.subject: refactor(cleanup): delete 4 dead exports, unexport 4 internals, drop dead hook
commits.body:
Audit findings #4, #5, #6, #7, #8, #9 — a repo-wide grep across
dashboard/js/ revealed 9 exported symbols with zero consumers. Four
were genuinely dead (delete them + the matching file), four were
internal helpers that only their own file consumes (drop the export
keyword, keep the function body).

Deleted entirely (zero consumers in dashboard/js, tests, or
scripts/__tests__):

  - debugLog.js debugGetEntries() — shallow-copy of entries array.
    No subscriber ever called this; DebugPill consumes entries via
    the debugSubscribe() replay mechanism instead.
  - debugLog.js diagnoseFailure(url) — async no-cors HEAD probe for
    CORS vs network failure differentiation. Was written for a
    diagnostic panel that never shipped; the DebugPill PWA tab runs
    its own probes via DebugTabs.jsx runDiagnostics() which is
    unrelated. Pre-existing dead code from an earlier session.
  - utils.js formatNumber(num) — 1.5K / 2.3M suffix formatter.
    Zero consumers; Chart.js handles axis-label formatting via its
    own `callback` ticks config, and the few stat cards that
    display large numbers use .toLocaleString() directly.
  - utils.js aggregateForDrilldown(commits, context) — detail-pane
    drilldown helper. Zero consumers; detail-pane aggregation
    happens in the section components directly via
    aggregateContributors() + inline filtering.
  - utils.js DAY_NAMES — full-name day array. Zero consumers; the
    only consumer (Discover peak-day metric) defines its own local
    array because it needs Sunday-first ordering. DAY_NAMES_SHORT
    is kept — Timing.jsx uses it.
  - dashboard/js/hooks/useClickOutside.js — default-exported hook.
    Zero imports anywhere. The components that originally needed
    click-outside (HamburgerMenu, dropdowns) migrated to inline
    backdrop overlays during the 2026-04-13/14 vanilla-DaisyUI
    sweep and never re-added the hook. File deleted; CLAUDE.md
    hooks list trimmed to match.

Demoted from `export` to module-private (only used in own file):

  - utils.js SA_HOLIDAYS — the holiday object literal. Only
    buildHolidaySet() (now module-private after commit 4e4f672)
    reads it. Removed `export`, added comment explaining the
    module-private status.
  - utils.js aggregateByTag(commits) — tag-count helper. Only
    aggregateContributors() (in the same file) calls it.
    aggregateForDrilldown deleted above was the other consumer.
    Dropped `export`, added comment.
  - chartColors.js getRepoColor(repoName, activeIndex) — per-repo
    chart colour. Only buildRepoColorMap() (in the same file) calls
    it. Dropped `export`, added module-private note.
  - components/debug/debugStyles.js SOURCE_COLORS + SEVERITY_COLORS
    — the two colour lookup maps exported by my own extraction in
    commit feb7129. They're consumed only inside the `styles`
    object closure in the same file (styles.sourceTag reads
    SOURCE_COLORS, styles.logEntry reads SEVERITY_COLORS). Removed
    `export` from both; `export const styles` is unchanged.

CLAUDE.md also updated:
  - Hooks list: removed `useClickOutside`.
  - js/debugLog.js one-line description: dropped "failure diagnosis"
    since diagnoseFailure was deleted.

Close call averted: utils.js getCommitDateRange was briefly
considered dead (it was only used inside the now-deleted
aggregateForDrilldown) but a repo-wide grep turned up
sections/Health.jsx line 217 as a legitimate external consumer.
Kept with its existing export.

Build clean, 60/60 tests pass.

commits.subject: docs(claude): expand exceptions for debug subsystem + ErrorBoundary + paths
commits.body:
Fresh-eyes audit findings #1, #2, #3, #11, #12 — the CLAUDE.md
exception lists had drifted out of sync with both the filesystem
and the actual code in three distinct ways.

1. Hex-literal exception list only named `DebugPill.jsx`, but the
   2026-04-15 DebugPill split (commit feb7129) moved most hex
   literals into `components/debug/debugStyles.js` and
   `components/debug/DebugTabs.jsx`. Both new files inherit the
   "isolated React root must survive CSS load failure" rationale,
   but the rationale was tied to a specific path that no longer
   holds all the hex. Expanded the exception to cover the entire
   DebugPill subsystem: `DebugPill.jsx` PLUS every file under
   `components/debug/`. Any future file added to that subdirectory
   inherits the exception automatically — stated explicitly in the
   expanded wording so a future reader doesn't re-litigate.

2. Bracket-value exception for `grid-cols-[auto_repeat(7,1fr)]`
   referenced `Timing.jsx full heatmap`, but the 2026-04-15
   TimingHeatmap split (commit 4f38b72) moved the grid into
   `components/TimingHeatmap.jsx` `HourlyHeatmap`. Same exception,
   new path. Fixed the file reference and added a parenthetical
   note about when the move happened so the path change isn't
   confusing to a reader cross-referencing git history.

3. Inline-style rule said "No inline `style={}` objects in JSX
   unless values are runtime-computed from data" with ONE list of
   data-derived allowances. But the root ErrorBoundary in main.jsx
   has a small static-literal inline style block (min-h, flex
   centering, padding, gap) that's defensively inlined so the
   error fallback renders even if the Tailwind CSS bundle failed
   to load — structurally the same resilience concern as the
   DebugPill exception. Added a new "Exceptions for static inline
   styles" sub-list with two entries: the root ErrorBoundary and
   the DebugPill subsystem. The ErrorBoundary entry explicitly
   notes that text colours still use DaisyUI utilities so they
   apply when CSS does load, and that this is the ONLY such
   exception outside the debug subsystem.

4. Docs table was missing `docs/ADMIN_GUIDE.md` despite the
   "After Each Significant Task" checklist (line 341) saying
   "Update docs/ADMIN_GUIDE.md if setup, extraction, or
   configuration changed". Added a row between USER_GUIDE.md and
   TESTING_GUIDE.md.

5. Key Components list (js/components/, js/sections/, js/hooks/)
   was stale after the 2026-04-15 extraction pass — missing
   `TimingHeatmap`, the `components/debug/` subdirectory, the
   `sections/discover/` subdirectory, and `useTimelineCharts`.
   Rewrote the three bullets to mention each new location with a
   one-line summary of what's in it, so the list matches a `ls`
   of the tree. `useClickOutside` is still listed because commit
   2 hasn't removed it yet — will be taken out in the next commit.

Build clean, 60/60 tests pass (no code touched, docs only).

Tags: documentation, audit, policy
Complexity: 2
Urgency: 2
Impact: internal
Risk: low
Debt: paid
Epic: post-audit-cleanup
Semver: patch
