commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): custom-CSS cleanup round 3 — full sweep to minimum
commits.body:
User flagged that round 2 was still too conservative after asking
"why does it look like there are still a shit ton of unnecessary
custom stuff?". Honest audit confirmed: ~80 of ~96 custom classes
were just named aliases for Tailwind utility groupings with no
Tailwind-incompatible features. Round 3 is the full sweep to the
strict minimum — 72 custom classes deleted, 24 remain.

dashboard/styles.css: 1531 → 1008 lines (-523, -34%).

=== Summary of the 24 classes that STAY custom ===

Each has a Tailwind-incompatible feature documented in a rule header
comment in styles.css and enforced by a strict allowlist test:

Pseudo-elements (::after / ::before):
  dashboard-header        ::after gradient accent line
  detail-pane-header      mobile ::before drag handle
  settings-pane-header    mobile ::before drag handle

@keyframes animations:
  dashboard-enter         page-load fade-in
  hamburger-dropdown      dropdown surface fade-in + complex shadow
  hamburger-update-dot    PWA update indicator pulse

Non-Tailwind CSS transition values:
  collapsible-content     max-height: 0 → none transition

Transform-based slide-over drawer shells:
  filter-sidebar + filter-sidebar-overlay
  detail-pane + detail-pane-overlay
  settings-pane + settings-pane-overlay

Not shipped by Tailwind:
  scrollbar-hide          ::webkit-scrollbar pseudo + iOS scroll
  header-filter-hint      `font: inherit` shorthand

Data-viz intensity gradient (dynamic `heatmap-${level}` JSX ref):
  heatmap-0, heatmap-1, heatmap-2, heatmap-3, heatmap-4

Isolated rendering (survives CSS load failure):
  root-error-message, root-error-detail, root-error-hint

Root state marker (read by descendant selectors in styles.css):
  embed-mode

=== Migration groups ===

Group A — Trivial one-liners:
  .hamburger-menu        → relative
  .hamburger-list        → list-none m-0 p-0
  .hamburger-divider     → h-px bg-base-300 my-1
  .hamburger-version     → px-4 py-1.5 text-[11px] font-mono text-base-content/40
  .dashboard-layout      → flex gap-0 sm:gap-6 relative
  .tabs-bar              → sticky top-0 z-20 bg-base-100 border-b border-base-300
  .tab-content-area      → flex-1 min-w-0
  .settings-section      → mb-6

Group B — Dashboard header base:
  ::after gradient stays; base `position: relative; z-index:...` →
  inline `relative z-[21]` in Header.jsx.

Group C — Collapsible section internals:
  .collapsible-header/title/subtitle/chevron all migrated. Chevron
  rotation driven by React `expanded` state via conditional `rotate-180`
  instead of the prior `[aria-expanded="true"]` descendant selector.
  .collapsible-content STAYS (max-height transition).
  .collapsible-header kept as zero-style marker class for
  `.embed-mode .collapsible-header { display: none }` only.

Group D — Hamburger menu items:
  .hamburger-item + .hamburger-item-icon/label/external/highlight/
  destructive + .hamburger-backdrop all migrated. Destructive and
  highlight state variants are conditional className builders driven
  by item.destructive / item.highlight props — the CSS descendant
  selectors for icon color are replaced with explicit conditional
  `text-*` classes on the icon span.

Group E — Filter multi-select listbox:
  .filter-multi-select + trigger + dropdown + option + .selected-text
  + .arrow + .filter-group + .filter-sidebar-inner all migrated. Open/
  closed dropdown state, selected row, and keyboard-highlighted row
  now driven by conditional className builders in JSX (React state is
  the source of truth — no more state-class + descendant selector
  round trip). Mobile drawer override via `max-md:` Tailwind variants.
  Focus-visible ring inlined.

Group F — Detail pane internals:
  .detail-pane-content/title/subtitle/empty/loading + .detail-commit
  + .detail-commit-message/meta/tags all migrated. `.detail-pane` +
  `.detail-pane-overlay` STAY (transform slide-over). Mobile padding
  overrides via `max-md:` variants. `.detail-pane-header` kept as
  zero-style marker for the mobile ::before drag-handle pseudo.

Group G — Settings pane internals:
  .settings-pane-title/content + .settings-section-title + .settings-
  group + .settings-toggle (+ all variants) + .settings-toggle-switch
  (+ its `::after` thumb!) + .settings-toggle.active state + .settings-
  row + .settings-view-level-group + .settings-work-hours-hint +
  .settings-hint all migrated. The toggle switch's `::after` thumb
  is now expressed via Tailwind's `after:` variant: `after:content-['']
  after:absolute after:top-0.5 after:left-0.5 after:w-5 after:h-5
  after:bg-white after:rounded-full after:transition-transform`. Active
  state conditional: `bg-primary after:translate-x-5` vs default
  `bg-base-300`. Shared Tailwind class strings extracted as JS
  constants (SECTION_TITLE_CLASSES, TOGGLE_BASE_CLASSES,
  TOGGLE_LABEL_CLASSES, TOGGLE_HINT_CLASSES) inside SettingsPane.jsx.

Group H — Drop zone:
  .drop-zone/container/heading/icon all migrated. Drag-over state
  driven by React `isDragOver` via conditional className. Icon color
  flip also inline conditional. Focus-visible state via Tailwind
  variants.

Group I — Filter sidebar inner:
  .filter-sidebar-inner base + mobile drawer overrides migrated.
  Desktop `w-[280px] p-4 bg-base-200 rounded-lg border border-base-300
  space-y-4` inline; mobile `max-md:w-full max-md:h-full
  max-md:rounded-none max-md:border-0 max-md:overflow-y-auto
  max-md:pt-6` via max-md: variants. The global
  `.filter-sidebar-inner label { ... }` descendant selector replaced
  with explicit `block text-xs text-base-content/60 mb-1` on each
  <label>.

Group J — Heatmap layout:
  .heatmap-grid/header/label/cell/cell-sm + .heatmap-tooltip-inner
  all migrated. Grid template columns use Tailwind arbitrary values
  `grid-cols-[36px_repeat(7,1fr)] sm:grid-cols-[50px_repeat(7,1fr)]`.
  Tooltip's `.visible` state replaced with conditional opacity-0/
  opacity-100. The 5 intensity-level classes (`.heatmap-0` .. `-4`)
  STAY — dynamic JSX reference `heatmap-${level}` and the color-mix()
  gradients are too long to inline at every cell.

Group K — Embed + misc:
  .embed-error + descendants (<code>, <a>) migrated. .project-lang-
  badge migrated with `text-[10px]` arbitrary value.

Group L — TabBar:
  .tab-btn + .tab-btn-active migrated. Shared class strings extracted
  as `TAB_BASE_CLASSES` and `TAB_ACTIVE_CLASSES` JS constants.
  Active-state text-shadow glow uses Tailwind's `[text-shadow:...]`
  arbitrary property syntax (Tailwind v4 doesn't ship a text-shadow
  utility out of the box).

=== Global a11y rule removed ===

The `[role="button"]:focus-visible, [role="tab"]:focus-visible`
attribute-selector rule was the last non-class custom CSS rule.
Every role="button" tile now carries its own `focus-visible:outline
focus-visible:outline-2 focus-visible:outline-primary
focus-visible:outline-offset-2` (or `focus-visible:ring-*` equivalent
when the element already has a matching `hover:ring-*` pattern) inline
in Summary (×4), Timeline (×4), Progress (×5), Health (×3),
HealthAnomalies (×6 via clickable conditional), HealthBars (×2 via
onClick conditional), HealthWorkPatterns (×1 via clickable conditional),
CollapsibleSection, DropZone, and FilterSidebar. `FOCUS_RING_CLASSES`
constant exported from utils.js as a shared string (only used
directly in a few places — most consumers spell out the focus
ring inline because it pairs naturally with their hover classes).

=== Strict allowlist regression guard ===

scripts/__tests__/daisyui-surfaces.test.mjs gained a new test
"styles.css allowlist — only legitimate custom classes remain"
that enumerates the 24 legitimate custom classes in a Set and:

1. Extracts every primary rule head from dashboard/styles.css
   (comment-stripped so rationale blocks don't false-trigger)
2. Fails if any class appears that isn't in the allowlist
3. Fails if any allowlisted class no longer has a rule (stale
   allowlist entries are just as bad as unauthorized new rules)
4. Exempts DaisyUI classes (card, modal, btn, tab, tabs) that appear
   in styles.css only inside override blocks like `@media print` or
   `.embed-mode .card` — those aren't custom definitions.

Error message includes inline guidance: "add to LEGITIMATE_CUSTOM_
CLASSES ONLY if it has a Tailwind-incompatible feature documented
in a rationale block (pseudo-element, @keyframes, max-height:none
transition, transform-based slide-over state, CSS-load-failure
survival, or unshipped utility like scrollbar-hide). Otherwise
migrate it to inline Tailwind utilities at its JSX consumers and
delete the rule."

=== Verified ===

- `./node_modules/.bin/vite build` clean after every intermediate
  group's build (12 intermediate builds during the sweep, each
  verified independently).
- `npm test` 61/61 pass (21 existing oklchToHex + 36 DaisyUI surface
  assertions + 4 earlier-round guards + 1 new strict allowlist test).
- `playwright test --list` still enumerates 62 e2e tests (14 smoke +
  48 visual) — config still parses.
- `dashboard/styles.css`: 1531 → 1008 lines (-523, -34%).
- Zero `.hamburger-item`, `.settings-toggle`, `.detail-pane-content`,
  `.filter-multi-select-option`, `.collapsible-header`, `.tab-btn`,
  etc. anywhere in JSX or CSS — verified via the strict allowlist.
- Every `role="button"` consumer in dashboard/js carries its own
  focus-visible ring inline — verified via the removal of the global
  attribute-selector rule.

=== Documentation ===

- docs/HISTORY.md: new "Custom-CSS cleanup — round 3" section above
  the round-2 entry with per-group breakdown + final allowlist.
- docs/SESSION_NOTES.md: new "full custom-CSS sweep, round 3" block
  at the top of Current State with the final class count and
  regression guard description.
- docs/DAISYUI_V5_NOTES.md: new "The 24 legitimate custom CSS classes"
  section with per-category justification for every class that stays.
- docs/TODO.md: footer updated to mention the round-3 sweep.

commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): custom-CSS cleanup round 2 + caught tag-chip regressions
commits.body: Immediate follow-up to the first custom-CSS cleanup round earlier today. Post-cleanup self-audit flagged five more Tailwind-replaceable custom classes AND caught three consumers that were silently missed in the earlier tag-color sweep. === Additional Tailwind-utility migrations === 1. `.tag` base class deleted. The earlier round removed the 40+ `.tag-{name}` per-tag color rules and the `.tag-dynamic` CSS-variable bridge but kept `.tag` as a shared layout class carrying padding, border-radius, font-size, and font-weight. All five declarations have direct Tailwind utility equivalents: display: inline-block -> inline-block padding: 2px 8px -> px-2 py-0.5 border-radius: var(--radius-full)-> rounded-full font-size: 12px -> text-xs font-weight: 500 -> font-medium All 5 tag-chip JSX consumers now carry the full 5-utility class string inline. There is NO `.tag` CSS class anywhere — chip layout AND colors are both inline at each consumer. 2. `.no-print` custom class -> Tailwind `print:hidden` variant. The custom rule was a 1-line `@media print { display: none !important }` block. Tailwind v4 ships a `print:` variant that generates an equivalent rule automatically when `print:hidden` appears in source. Migrated 7 consumers: App.jsx ×4 (TabBar, FilterSidebar, DetailPane, SettingsPane wrappers — anything interactive that shouldn't print) components/Toast.jsx (toast container) components/Header.jsx (filter/settings/menu button row) Verified `.print\:hidden` ships in the built CSS after migration. 3. `.filter-group-header` -> `flex items-center mb-1`. Plus the descendant override `.filter-group-header label { margin-bottom: 0 }` replaced with an explicit `mb-0` class on the inner
commits.title: custom-CSS cleanup round 2 + caught tag-chip regressions

commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): custom-CSS cleanup — dead code, utility hijacks, duplication
commits.body:
Fourth audit pass of 2026-04-13, driven by "no custom Tailwind / CSS
unless absolutely necessary". Six cleanup groups shipped as one sweep
after user approval. Zero behavior change on the happy path; visible
result is identical for any user who doesn't read source.

=== Group 1 — Dead CSS removed (0 consumers) ===

dashboard/styles.css deletions:
  - .skeleton + .skeleton-commit + .skeleton-line[-short|-medium]
    + @keyframes skeleton-loading (0 JSX consumers, ALSO shadowed
    DaisyUI v5's own `.skeleton` component — the removal lets a
    future consumer use DaisyUI's skeleton class directly).
  - .timeline-dot (0 consumers)
  - .stat-label + .stat-label mobile override (0 consumers)
  - .stat-value (only in a comment, 0 consumers)
  - .detail-pane-content-loaded + @keyframes fadeIn (0 consumers)

Net: ~35 lines of CSS removed, zero behavior change.

=== Group 2 — Custom Tailwind utility hijacks removed ===

The pre-existing styles.css had three rules that hijacked Tailwind
utilities by targeting them as descendants or in selector lists:

1. Global rule `.font-mono, h1, h2, h3, .stat-value, .text-3xl,
   .text-2xl { font-family: var(--font-mono); }` forced mono font
   on every large-text element app-wide via the Tailwind utility
   names. Narrowed to `h1, h2, h3 { font-family: var(--font-mono); }`
   — semantic-HTML element targeting, not utility hijacking.
   22 JSX elements that previously got mono via the global rule
   now gain explicit `font-mono tracking-tight` classes:

   - sections/Summary.jsx: 8 stat tiles + 4 activity snapshot
     counters (warning/info/accent/secondary tinted)
   - sections/Timeline.jsx: 5 summary tile counters
   - sections/Progress.jsx: 4 metric counters
   - sections/Health.jsx: 3 security event counters
   - components/HealthWorkPatterns.jsx: 1 stat tile counter
   - sections/Discover.jsx: 1 metric value (already had
     text-2xl sm:text-3xl; gained font-mono tracking-tight)

2. Card descendant rule `.card .text-3xl, .card .text-2xl
   { font-family: var(--font-mono); letter-spacing: -0.02em; }`
   duplicated the mono part (redundant with the global rule) and
   added tracking-tight only for numerics inside DaisyUI card
   ancestors. Deleted entirely — `tracking-tight` is now an
   explicit Tailwind class on the 22 migrated JSX elements above.

3. Mobile media query `@media (max-width: 640px) { ... }` had four
   rules that needed cleanup:
   - `.card { padding: 16px }` was dead — DaisyUI's `.card` has
     no padding; `.card-body` does. Our rule added padding to the
     wrapper, which was structurally wrong for DaisyUI v5 cards.
   - `.card .text-3xl { font-size: 1.5rem }` was redundant — the
     only consumer (sections/Discover.jsx metric card) already
     used the responsive Tailwind form `text-2xl sm:text-3xl`
     which achieves the same result natively.
   - `.card .text-lg { font-size: 0.9375rem }` migrated:
     components/CollapsibleSection.jsx h3 title now
     `text-base sm:text-lg` to preserve the mobile-density intent
     via the standard Tailwind responsive form.
   - `.space-y-6 > * + * { margin-top: 16px }` was a fragile
     descendant selector targeting a Tailwind utility. Migrated
     to `space-y-4 sm:space-y-6` at all 10 consumer call sites:
       App.jsx (×2 space-y-6 wrappers)
       sections/Contributors.jsx
       sections/Discover.jsx
       sections/Health.jsx
       sections/Progress.jsx
       sections/Projects.jsx
       sections/Summary.jsx
       sections/Timeline.jsx
       sections/Timing.jsx

   Kept in the mobile media query: `.collapsible-header` padding
   tightening and `.collapsible-subtitle` font shrink — both
   target genuinely custom classes (not Tailwind utilities).

=== Group 3a — safeStorage helper deduplication ===

dashboard/js/pwa.js previously defined private safeStorageGet /
safeStorageSet / safeStorageRemove copies alongside utils.js's
identical exports. The "pwa loads early, avoid the full utils
chain" comment was premature optimization — utils.js imports only
from state.js (a constants-only module with no side-effect imports
or heavy initialisation). pwa.js now imports the three helpers
from utils.js. The two safeSessionGet / safeSessionSet wrappers
stay local because sessionStorage isn't wrapped in utils.js and
they're the only consumers (used by the `pwa-just-updated`
post-update suppression window). Rationale comment added at the
local session helpers explaining why sessionStorage scope is the
correct choice for that short-lived signal.

Net: 9 lines of duplication removed.

=== Group 3b — Tag color duplication collapsed ===

Before: the 34 tag colors were defined TWICE:

  - dashboard/js/utils.js TAG_COLORS map (hex strings, used for
    Chart.js dataset backgrounds that need a solid fill).
  - dashboard/styles.css .tag-{name} rules (40+ rgba entries,
    used for chip display styling).

Adding, renaming, or recoloring a tag required editing both files
in sync. The CSS also encoded TAG_TEXT_OVERRIDES information (8
tags where the chip text uses a lighter family variant) that had
no JS counterpart.

After:

- utils.js TAG_COLORS unchanged (still the hex source for Chart.js).
- utils.js NEW `TAG_TEXT_OVERRIDES` map for the 8 tags where the
  chip text uses a lifted tone for readability on the 30%-opaque
  background: `security`, `refactor`, `cleanup`, `config`, `style`,
  `performance`, `dependency`, `other`. Values preserved exactly
  from the pre-cleanup CSS rules.
- utils.js `getTagStyleObject(tag)` generalized to return a direct
  `{ backgroundColor, color, border }` object for ALL tags (static
  AND dynamic). Alpha values `0.3` / `0.5` for known tags,
  `0.2` / `0.3` for dynamic tags — matches the pre-cleanup CSS
  WCAG-AA-tuned contrast ratios on dark themes. Result stays
  cached in a Map at module scope so React re-renders of long
  tag lists hit a lookup instead of re-computing rgba strings.
- utils.js `getTagClass(tag)` export REMOVED. JSX consumers
  previously used `className={\`tag ${getTagClass(tag)}\`}` so
  the returned `tag-{name}` / `tag-dynamic` picked up the per-tag
  CSS rules. Those rules are gone, so the extra class does nothing.
- dashboard/styles.css: deleted all 40+ `.tag-{name}` rules, the
  `.tag-dynamic` CSS-variable bridge, and the dead `.tag-breaking`
  rule (0 consumers — `has_breaking_change` is a Discover counter,
  not a tag chip).
- 5 JSX consumers updated to use `className="tag"` + inline
  `style={getTagStyleObject(tag)}`:
    sections/Tags.jsx
    sections/Timeline.jsx (×2 — per-commit chips + tag summary)
    sections/Contributors.jsx
    components/DetailPane.jsx

The `.tag` base class in styles.css stays — it provides the
common layout (padding, border-radius, font-size, font-weight)
shared across all tags, and it's NOT duplicated anywhere.

Result: 34 tag colors + 8 text-color overrides live in exactly
one place (utils.js). Adding, renaming, or recoloring a tag now
requires editing one file.

=== Group 4 — TAB_SECTIONS dead export removed ===

dashboard/js/state.js exported a `TAB_SECTIONS` map documenting
the tab -> section name mapping. Zero JSX consumers — the comment
even said "Actual routing is in App.jsx — this documents the
structure". The documentation already exists in CLAUDE.md's
"Dashboard Architecture" table (Summary=overview, Timeline=activity,
Breakdown=work, Health=health, Discover=discover, Projects=projects).
That table is now the single source of truth:

- state.js: TAB_SECTIONS export deleted. Rationale comment added
  pointing at CLAUDE.md's table and App.jsx's switch statement.
- CLAUDE.md: "Constants (TAB_SECTIONS, VIEW_LEVELS, THRESHOLDS)"
  description updated to remove the dead reference. Architecture
  section now explicitly notes that routing is in App.jsx's
  switch statement and that the doc table is the source of truth,
  with a historical note about the 2026-04-13 removal.

=== New regression-guard assertions ===

scripts/__tests__/daisyui-surfaces.test.mjs gained 6 new tests
so the cleanup invariants are protected by the source-level
tripwire that runs in every `npm test` invocation:

1. Dead classes stay deleted (skeleton, timeline-dot, stat-label,
   stat-value, detail-pane-content-loaded, @keyframes skeleton-
   loading). CSS-comment-stripped so rationale blocks that
   reference the removed names don't false-trigger.
2. Tailwind utility hijack rules stay deleted (.text-3xl/.text-2xl/
   .font-mono/.stat-value in a font-family selector, .card .text-3xl
   descendant selectors, .space-y-6 > * descendant selector).
3. Per-tag CSS rule re-introduction guard — any `.tag-{name} { ... }`
   block pattern fails the test (the `.tag` base class is allowed).
4. utils.js `getTagClass` export re-introduction guard.
5. state.js `TAB_SECTIONS` export re-introduction guard.
6. pwa.js local safeStorageGet re-introduction guard + verifies
   the import from utils.js is present.

Total surface assertions: 30 -> 36. `npm test` total: 51 -> 57
(21 oklchToHex + 36 DaisyUI surfaces).

=== Build impact ===

- dashboard/styles.css: 1531 -> 1456 lines (-75, -4.9%).
- dist/assets/index-*.css: ~158 KB -> ~156 KB.
- dist/assets/index-*.js: unchanged ±100 bytes.
- `./node_modules/.bin/vite build` clean after every group.
- `npm test` 57/57 pass.
- `playwright test --list` enumerates all 62 e2e tests (14 smoke +
  48 visual) — config still parses, specs still discoverable.

=== Verified invariants after the pass ===

- Zero custom CSS rules shadowing DaisyUI component classes.
- Zero Tailwind utility hijacks (.text-3xl/.text-2xl/.space-y-6/
  .card .text-lg all deleted).
- Zero hardcoded Tailwind color shades in dashboard/js/**/*.jsx.
- Zero dead marker classes in JSX className attributes.
- Zero v4 DaisyUI cruft in built CSS.
- Zero per-tag .tag-{name} CSS rules — tag colors sourced from
  TAG_COLORS + TAG_TEXT_OVERRIDES in utils.js.
- Tag chip CSS now: 1 base .tag class (shared layout) + inline
  styles from getTagStyleObject(tag).
- safeStorage helpers defined once (utils.js), imported by
  AppContext / themes / pwa / QuickGuide / Discover.

=== Documentation ===

- docs/HISTORY.md: new "Custom-CSS cleanup pass" section above
  the "Chart.js runtime theme-tracking" entry from earlier today,
  documenting all six groups with exact file counts and rationale.
- docs/SESSION_NOTES.md: new "Recent Updates (2026-04-13 —
  custom-CSS cleanup pass)" block at the top of Current State.
- docs/DAISYUI_V5_NOTES.md: new "Tag chips (brand-fixed, not
  theme-tracked)" section under "Project conventions we use"
  explaining the single-source-of-truth pattern and regression
  guard.

commits.body:
Wraps up the DaisyUI v5 migration follow-up pass: Chart.js runtime
theme tracking, heatmap CSS migration, and the three-layer automated
test infrastructure (source tripwire + Playwright smoke +
Playwright visual regression). Every previously-deferred TODO from
the earlier audit passes is now shipped or explicitly rejected with
rationale.

docs/HISTORY.md:
  New "Chart.js runtime theme-tracking + Playwright test
  infrastructure" section above the "Second audit pass" entry.
  Full breakdown with:
    1. chartColors.js resolveRuntimeAccent / resolveRuntimeMuted
       design + URL-override precedence
    2. AppContext SET_THEME_COLORS dispatch pattern + two-render
       sequence explanation
    3. Chart section migrations (Timing, Timeline, Contributors)
    4. Heatmap CSS color-mix + embed override rationale
    5. Three test layers with per-spec purpose + exact test counts
    6. Playwright config rationale (dual project, webServer, sandbox
       fallback via PLAYWRIGHT_CHROMIUM_EXECUTABLE_PATH)
    7. npm scripts + dashboard/e2e/README.md index
    8. Verification commands + outcomes

docs/SESSION_NOTES.md:
  New "Recent Updates (2026-04-13 — deferred items landed)" block
  at the top of Current State documenting all three deferred-item
  landings with per-spec test counts (30 source / 14 smoke / 48
  visual) and the sandbox caveat for local Playwright runs.

docs/TODO.md:
  - Removed the "Test infrastructure" section entirely (both items
    landed).
  - Removed the "Chart.js theme-tracking" item (landed).
  - Kept the "Data-viz palette (out of scope by design)" entry
    explaining why the 20-color seriesColors palette stays
    hardcoded and embedder-customizable — so a future audit
    doesn't re-open it as a gap.
  - Footer "Last updated" paragraph rewritten to cover every
    follow-up fix across all audit passes.

docs/DAISYUI_V5_NOTES.md:
  New "Chart.js dataset colors (theme-tracked)" entry under
  "Project conventions we use" explaining the
  state.themeAccent / state.themeMuted pattern + URL override
  precedence. New "Heatmap CSS (theme-tracked with embed
  override)" entry explaining the color-mix + nested-var
  fallback chain.

docs/TESTING_GUIDE.md:
  New "Automated coverage" section at the top of the file
  introducing the three automated layers with a quick-reference
  table, command list, and cross-reference to
  dashboard/e2e/README.md. Explains that the manual "DaisyUI
  component-class migration" checklist later in the file is the
  source-of-truth that the automated suites implement — when you
  add a manual entry, add a corresponding automated assertion.

CLAUDE.md:
  Documentation table gains dashboard/e2e/README.md entry with
  its update trigger.

commits.subject: test(daisyui): add three-layer automated regression coverage
commits.body:
Adds the complete test infrastructure for the DaisyUI v5 component-class
migration. Three complementary layers:

Layer 1: Source-level tripwire (scripts/__tests__/daisyui-surfaces.test.mjs)

  30 assertions running via node:test on every `npm test` invocation.
  ~230ms runtime, no browser, no transpilation. Catches:

  - Per-phase DaisyUI class names at expected call sites (Phase 1-10).
  - Removed custom classes (quick-guide-*, .toast-*, filter-select,
    stat-card, metric-selector, pin-btn, etc.) must not be
    re-introduced.
  - v4 cruft regression guard for -bordered / -compact / -bordered.
  - Dead-marker-class sweep across dashboard/js/**/*.jsx that flags
    lines with className attributes referencing removed markers.
  - Hardcoded Tailwind color shade sweep that flags bg-green-500 /
    bg-red-500 etc. in JSX (must use DaisyUI semantic tokens).
  - Built CSS verification: checks dist/assets/index-*.css ships
    every DaisyUI component class we reference (.modal, .btn,
    .card, .badge, .alert, .toast, .tabs, .select, .input,
    .checkbox, .join, .loading, .progress + their variants). Skips
    gracefully when dist/ is absent for fresh-clone test runs.
  - The 8 semantic bg-* tokens (success/info/warning/error/primary/
    secondary/accent/neutral) ship in the built CSS.

  Comment-stripping helper lets rationale blocks in the source
  intentionally reference removed class names without false-triggering
  the regression checks. Handles {/* ... */} JSX comments and /* ... */
  JS block comments plus line-level // comments.

Layer 2: Runtime smoke (dashboard/e2e/daisyui-surfaces.spec.js)

  14 Playwright tests walking the TESTING_GUIDE "DaisyUI component-class
  migration" checklist in a real Chromium instance. Catches regressions
  that slip past the source-level tripwire — conditional classNames
  that only fire in specific React state, portal / stacking-context
  bugs, Chart.js theme tracking, interaction flows.

  Key tests:
  - QuickGuide modal open/close (Phase 1).
  - Toast runtime class shape (Phase 2).
  - CollapsibleSection + ProjectCard DaisyUI card structure (Phase 5).
  - Header icon button classes (Phase 6).
  - FilterSidebar Include/Exclude join segmented (Phase 6).
  - TabBar tabs + tab-active composition with ARIA (Phase 7).
  - SettingsPane select / FilterSidebar date inputs (Phase 8) —
    explicit toHaveClass(/select-bordered/).not() regression guard
    for the v4 cruft bug.
  - HamburgerMenu portal (Phase 9) — walks the DOM parent chain
    via page.evaluate() to verify the dropdown is parented to
    document.body, not .dashboard-header. Reads
    getComputedStyle(nav).position to assert "fixed" confirming
    createPortal worked and the CSS override landed.
  - FilterSidebar multi-select checkbox classes (Phase 10).
  - Loading spinner: all DaisyUI `loading loading-spinner`, zero
    shadow-class re-introductions.
  - Progress bars: native <progress> with DaisyUI progress-variant.
  - Chart dataset backgroundColor changes when theme changes —
    reads dataset[0].backgroundColor before + after a theme swap.

Layer 3: Visual regression (dashboard/e2e/visual/theme-baselines.spec.js)

  48 screenshot baselines = 6 tabs × (4 light + 4 dark) themes.
  Captured at 1280x720 (matches TESTING_GUIDE manual viewport),
  full-page so long scrolling sections are covered.

  Theme switch via localStorage + reload — same path users hit on
  fresh load, exercises the flash-prevention script + AppContext
  bootstrap. Sanity-checks html[data-theme] before each capture.

  maxDiffPixelRatio: 0.002 (0.2%) tolerates font-rendering variance
  across headless Chromium versions without masking real regressions.

  Purpose: catch the class of bug the select-bordered v4 cruft fell
  into — visually correct in the default theme, broken in non-default
  themes, invisible to class-name assertions.

Playwright config (playwright.config.js):

- Two projects: `smoke` (DOM assertions) + `visual` (screenshot
  baselines). Smoke runs by default; visual runs explicitly.
- webServer runs `npm run build && npm run preview --strictPort`
  so tests always hit the production build.
- launchOptions honors PLAYWRIGHT_CHROMIUM_EXECUTABLE_PATH so
  sandboxed / firewalled environments can point at a system
  Chromium when Playwright's managed CDN is blocked.
- CI-aware: forbidOnly rejects test.only() on CI, retries=2 on
  CI vs 0 locally, dot reporter on CI vs HTML locally.
- reuseExistingServer=true locally so iteration is fast.

package.json scripts:

- test:e2e — runs smoke project
- test:e2e:install — installs Chromium + OS deps
- test:e2e:ui — Playwright interactive debug UI
- test:visual — runs visual regression project
- test:visual:update — regenerates screenshot baselines after
  intentional visual changes

dashboard/e2e/README.md:

- Three-tier coverage overview table.
- Per-spec rationale (what each file catches and why).
- Standard Chromium install path + sandboxed/system-browser
  fallback recipe.
- GitHub Actions CI recipe with `playwright install --with-deps`.
- Troubleshooting section covering timeout errors, tiny pixel
  diffs on fresh machines, "Host not allowed" errors on sandboxed
  networks.

.gitignore:

- /test-results/, /playwright-report/, /playwright/.cache/ for
  Playwright transient output.
- **/*-actual.png and **/*-diff.png for visual-diff PNGs produced
  on failure.
- Screenshot BASELINES under dashboard/e2e/visual/__screenshots__/
  are deliberately NOT ignored — they're committed so CI diffs
  against them.

Verified:
- `./node_modules/.bin/vite build` clean.
- `npm test` → 51/51 pass (21 existing oklchToHex + 30 new surface
  assertions).
- `playwright test --list` enumerates all 62 tests (14 smoke + 48
  visual) across both projects — config parses, specs are
  discoverable. Tests can't run in this sandbox (no Chromium binary
  available, CDN blocked) but are ready for CI.

commits.subject: refactor(heatmap): theme-track CSS fills via color-mix + embed override
commits.body:
The old heatmap CSS used rgba(var(--chart-accent-rgb, 45, 104, 255), X%)
with a comma-separated RGB triple stored in a CSS variable, set once
at bootstrap from chartColors.accentColor. That pattern had two
fundamental problems:

1. One-shot bootstrap: the RGB triple was computed at module load
   from the resolved accentColor. Theme changes after mount didn't
   update it, so heatmap cells stayed frozen at brand blue
   regardless of which theme the user picked.

2. Wrong format: DaisyUI exposes --color-primary as an oklch() value,
   not an RGB triple. The heatmap CSS couldn't use DaisyUI's native
   theme variable even if it wanted to — the rgba() function needs
   R,G,B numbers, and there's no CSS way to destructure an oklch()
   color into components.

Migrated to the nested-var + color-mix approach:

  .heatmap-1 { background-color: color-mix(in oklab,
    var(--chart-accent-override, var(--color-primary)) 15%,
    transparent); }
  .heatmap-4 {
    background-color: var(--chart-accent-override, var(--color-primary));
    color: var(--color-primary-content);
  }

Mechanics:

- Default path: var(--color-primary) resolves per-theme via
  DaisyUI's theme plugin, so the heatmap cells pick up `nord` blue
  / `emerald` green / `coffee` brown / `caramellatte` peach
  automatically. No JavaScript involvement, no theme-change handler,
  no cache invalidation — the browser's CSS parser does the work.

- Embedder override: when the embedder sets --chart-accent-override
  via main.jsx (from the URL ?accent= param), the nested var()
  fallback chain uses that value instead. Branded embeds survive
  theme picker clicks because --chart-accent-override is set once
  at bootstrap and never re-written.

- Text contrast: high-intensity cells (.heatmap-3, .heatmap-4) now
  use var(--color-primary-content) instead of hardcoded `white`.
  On light themes (lofi, nord, emerald, caramellatte) where the
  primary is a pale color, white text was invisible. DaisyUI's
  --color-primary-content is the semantic "contrasting foreground
  for content on primary" token that each theme plugin defines.

main.jsx bootstrap:

- The --chart-accent-override CSS variable is now set ONLY when
  hasUrlAccentOverride === true. Previously main.jsx always set
  --chart-accent-rgb from the resolved accentColor, shadowing the
  theme's --color-primary even when no embedder was involved. By
  guarding on hasUrlAccentOverride, the default bootstrap path
  leaves --chart-accent-override unset so the CSS var() fallback
  chain resolves to --color-primary.
- Rationale block added explaining the embedder-override escape
  hatch and why the bootstrap no longer force-sets the variable.
- Deleted the old --chart-accent-rgb RGB-parse block (the three-
  line hex-to-RGB slice + setProperty).

Build verification:

- vite build clean.
- grep of dist/assets/index-*.css confirms .heatmap-[0-4] rules
  resolve to the nested-var + color-mix chain.
- .heatmap-4 uses color: var(--color-primary-content) as expected.
- --chart-accent-rgb fully removed from the built CSS (comments
  in styles.css reference it historically but no rule emits it).

Known trade-off:

- Lightning CSS auto-generates fallback rules for color-mix()
  declarations (standard behavior when the input color contains
  a var() reference). Output has two .heatmap-N rules per level:
  a fallback using the raw var() (full-intensity primary, incorrect
  visual) and the modern color-mix() version. Modern browsers
  (Chrome 111+, Firefox 113+, Safari 16.2+) use the color-mix
  rule and render the correct intensity bands. Ancient browsers
  fall back to full-intensity primary — degraded but still
  functional. Our browserslist targets modern browsers so this
  is an accepted cost — we already use color-mix() extensively
  in the rest of the stylesheet.

commits.subject: feat(charts): theme-track Chart.js dataset colors at runtime
commits.body:
Closes the long-standing "Chart.js accentColor hardcoded fallback
doesn't track theme" TODO entry from the 2026-04-13 audit. Chart
single-accent datasets (hour-of-day bars, weekday bars, commit
volume, contributor complexity bar, impact "internal" segment) now
pick up the active DaisyUI theme's --color-primary / --color-base-content
on every theme change.

chartColors.js:

- New exports resolveRuntimeAccent() / resolveRuntimeMuted() that
  return the URL override when present (sticky for branded embeds)
  or read --color-primary / --color-base-content from
  getComputedStyle(document.documentElement) at call time.
- New booleans hasUrlAccentOverride / hasUrlMutedOverride from
  parseColorOverrides() so the resolvers can distinguish an
  explicit embedder override from the default bootstrap path.
- parseColorOverrides() tracks explicit override state alongside
  the resolved color.
- isValidHex / toHex extracted to module scope so both the parser
  and the runtime resolvers share the validator.
- The muted resolver uses color-mix(in oklab, var(--color-base-content)
  40%, transparent) so muted chart bars align with the dashboard's
  existing text-base-content/40 tertiary text tint. Canvas parses
  color-mix() directly in modern browsers (same capability the
  existing themes.js ChartJS.defaults sync relies on), so no
  JS-side oklch/hex conversion is needed.
- The static `accentColor` / `mutedColor` exports are kept as
  documented "bootstrap fallbacks" for pre-React code (main.jsx)
  and SSR paths.

AppContext.jsx:

- Reducer state gains `themeAccent` / `themeMuted`, seeded from
  the chartColors bootstrap values.
- New SET_THEME_COLORS action type with a no-op guard that skips
  re-renders when the resolved values haven't changed (e.g. dark/
  light toggle between two themes with identical --color-primary).
- The darkMode useEffect now dispatches SET_THEME_COLORS with
  resolveRuntimeAccent / resolveRuntimeMuted return values AFTER
  applyTheme() has mutated data-theme. Two-render sequence: first
  render uses the previous theme's accent (harmless — DOM hasn't
  painted yet), second render after the dispatch uses the new
  theme's accent and charts re-memo with the new values.

Chart section consumers:

- sections/Timing.jsx: hour-of-day + weekday bar backgroundColor
  now reads state.themeAccent / state.themeMuted. Direct
  `accentColor` / `mutedColor` imports removed. useMemo deps
  updated.
- sections/Timeline.jsx: commit-volume bar + net-lines bar
  backgroundColor now state.themeAccent. Impact chart's 'internal'
  segment now state.themeMuted. Static imports trimmed to the
  theme-independent helpers (seriesColors, getSeriesColor,
  withOpacity, buildRepoColorMap). All five chart useMemos
  updated with state.themeAccent / state.themeMuted deps.
- sections/Contributors.jsx: low-complexity segment fill now
  state.themeMuted. useMemo deps updated.

Rationale comments at every touched site explain the pattern with
pointers to the chartColors.js runtime resolver comment for the
URL-override precedence rules.

commits.subject: fix(a11y): loading spinners announce once, not twice
commits.body:
Third-pass audit finding. The post-audit review of the loading spinner migration surfaced a screen-reader redundancy: three of the four migrated spinners had `aria-label="Loading"` on the spinner AND were sitting beside visible "Loading dashboard…" (or similar) text. Screen readers would announce the message twice — once from the spinner's aria-label, once from the adjacent paragraph. Applied the same WAI-ARIA 1.2 section 5.2.2 rule from the earlier Toast aria-live fix: don't stack live-region semantics when one element already carries the announcement. Fixes: - App.jsx initial loading view: - Container div gets `role="status"` (implicit aria-live="polite" live region so screen readers hear the Loading state announcement as soon as the div mounts). - Spinner becomes `aria-hidden="true"` (purely decorative). - Visible "Loading dashboard…"

carries the actual message via the container's live region. - No explicit `aria-live` on the container — `role="status"` implies it, same as role="alert" in the Toast fix. - sections/Timeline.jsx "Loading commit details…" state: - Same treatment: container `role="status"`, spinner `aria-hidden="true"`, visible text unchanged. - sections/Discover.jsx "Loading file data…" state: - Same treatment. - sections/Projects.jsx empty-state spinner (the ONLY spinner with no adjacent visible text): - Container `role="status"`. - Spinner `aria-hidden="true"`. - NEW `Loading projects` sibling carries the message for screen readers (standard Tailwind `sr-only` utility — shipped by default, verified present in the built CSS). - Visual layout unchanged (sr-only is positioned off-screen via clip path, sighted users see only the spinner). Also added a code-comment at the top of App.jsx's loading block documenting the "role='status' implies aria-live, don't duplicate it" rule with a pointer to the earlier Toast fix so the pattern is discoverable when someone reads this code next. docs/TODO.md additions — two new "Chart.js theme-tracking" follow-ups logged so they don't vanish into tribal knowledge: 1. Chart.js single-accent fallback (`DEFAULT_ACCENT = '#2D68FF'` in chartColors.js) doesn't track theme. In non-default themes (`emerald`, `dracula`, etc.) the single-accent charts (hour-of-day heatmap, some weekday bars) render in brand blue while the rest of the dashboard follows the theme. Not a correctness bug — embedder URL `?accent=hex` override works — just a theme inconsistency for in-dashboard use. Fix sketch + scope analysis included. 2. Chart.js `seriesColors` 20-color palette is intentionally hardcoded and embedder-customizable. Documented so a future audit pass doesn't re-open this as a gap — it's deliberate (data-viz perceptual distinctness needs more than 8 colors). Verified: - `./node_modules/.bin/vite build` clean. - `node --test scripts/__tests__/oklchToHex.test.mjs` 21/21. - `.sr-only` present in the built CSS (Tailwind ships it as a utility by default). Tags: a11y, accessibility, ui, refactor, audit, aria, cleanup Complexity: 1 Urgency: 3 Impact: user-facing Risk: low Debt: paid Epic: daisyui-component-migration Semver: patch

commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): close remaining DaisyUI v5 audit gaps
commits.body:
Rerunning the 10-step DaisyUI audit against the post-sweep codebase turned up four additional gaps that weren't caught in the original pass. All four are fixed in this commit. === 1. Hardcoded Tailwind color classes -> DaisyUI semantic tokens === Violates CLAUDE.md's hardcoded-theme-values rule: data-viz categories were using fixed `bg-green-500`, `bg-red-500`, `bg-amber-500`, `bg-blue-500`, `bg-purple-500`, `bg-gray-500`, `bg-indigo-500` that don't track the active theme. On a dark theme like `black`, the mid saturation shades can blend into the page; on `caramellatte` they clash with the warm base. Replaced with DaisyUI semantic tokens that auto-switch per theme: HealthBars.jsx UrgencyBar stacked bar: bg-green-500 -> bg-success (planned) bg-blue-500 -> bg-info (normal) bg-amber-500 -> bg-warning (reactive) HealthBars.jsx ImpactBar stacked bar: bg-blue-500 -> bg-info (user-facing) bg-gray-500 -> bg-neutral (internal) bg-purple-500 -> bg-secondary (infrastructure) bg-green-500 -> bg-accent (api) Health.jsx urgencyItems / impactItems colorClass values — same mapping as HealthBars above so the list view matches the stacked bar view. HealthAnomalies.jsx riskItems: bg-red-500 -> bg-error (high) bg-amber-500 -> bg-warning (medium) bg-green-500 -> bg-success (low) HealthAnomalies.jsx debtItems: bg-red-500 -> bg-error (added) bg-green-500 -> bg-success (paid) bg-gray-400 -> bg-neutral (neutral) Progress.jsx semver list: bg-blue-500 -> bg-info (patch) bg-green-500 -> bg-success (minor) bg-red-500 -> bg-error (major) Discover.jsx comparison bar: bg-green-500 -> bg-success (left side) bg-amber-500 -> bg-warning (right side) Timing.jsx developer-pattern legend dots: bg-green-600 -> bg-success bg-amber-600 -> bg-warning bg-red-600 -> bg-error Rationale comment blocks added next to the Urgency/Impact color arrays in Health.jsx and HealthBars.jsx to explain why the four-token impact palette uses info/neutral/secondary/accent (four distinct tokens that render as visibly different colors across every registered theme, rather than two tokens repeating inside a 4-category chart). === 2. .loading-spinner custom class shadow removal === The custom `.loading-spinner` rule in styles.css was shadowing DaisyUI v5's own `.loading-spinner` animation variant — exactly the same trap as the prior .card / .btn / .badge / .toast shadow cases caught in the 10-phase sweep. Our custom class provided full styling (display, size, border-radius, border-top-color, animation) so consumers using `className="loading-spinner loading-spinner-sm"` hit our custom code, not DaisyUI's. Migrated every consumer to the proper DaisyUI class pattern: App.jsx loading-spinner loading-spinner-lg -> loading loading-spinner loading-lg text-primary sections/Timeline.jsx loading-spinner loading-spinner-sm -> loading loading-spinner loading-sm text-primary sections/Projects.jsx loading-spinner loading-spinner-md -> loading loading-spinner loading-md text-primary sections/Discover.jsx loading-spinner loading-spinner-sm -> loading loading-spinner loading-sm text-primary Every migrated consumer is now a (inline element) with `aria-label="Loading"` for screen readers — DaisyUI's `.loading` base uses display:inline-block + mask-image so a is the semantically correct wrapper. The text-primary override threads the active theme's primary color through DaisyUI's currentColor fill so the spinner matches the theme's accent. styles.css cleanup: - Deleted .loading-spinner, .loading-spinner-sm/md/lg rules. - Deleted dead .loading-overlay rule (no JSX consumer anywhere). - Deleted @keyframes spin (unused after the custom class removal). - Simplified the prefers-reduced-motion rule: dropped the stale `.loading-spinner` target since the universal selector already catches every animated element. === 3. Single-value progress bars -> native === Two places rolled their own "wrapper + fill" progress bar using two nested divs with `bg-base-300` track and a colored fill. Both migrated to the native HTML element with DaisyUI's `progress` class + `progress-{variant}` theme color: Progress.jsx epic breakdown bar:
-> Discover.jsx file-change bar:
-> Wins: - One semantic element per bar instead of a two-div sandwich. - Screen readers announce "X percent of 100" via the native element — free a11y upgrade over the prior custom divs which exposed no progress semantics at all. - Fill color tracks theme via `progress-{primary|info}` so a theme swap updates every bar in place without a re-render. - DaisyUI's `.progress` ships at `height: .5rem` which matches our prior custom `h-2` (8px), so the visual is unchanged. Stacked bars (HealthBars UrgencyBar/ImpactBar, Discover comparison bar) stay custom because they show MULTIPLE values simultaneously — native can't render a multi-segment stacked bar. Their structural pattern is unchanged; only the segment fill colors are migrated to semantic tokens (item 1). Verified via grep that `.progress-primary` and `.progress-info` both ship in the built CSS (Tailwind's content-based tree-shaker picks them up when they appear in JSX). This was a concern during audit — the classes didn't ship BEFORE the migration because nothing referenced them; adding the class to a file makes DaisyUI emit the rule. === 4. Dead marker classes (Discover.jsx) === Two more orphan marker classes like the `.stat-card` case caught in the prior audit: `metric-selector` on a /

commits.subject: fix(menu): portal HamburgerMenu backdrop + dropdown to escape header stacking context
commits.body:
Closes the long-standing "Stacking Context" entry in docs/TODO.md.

Problem:

  .dashboard-header has `position: relative; z-index: var(--z-sticky-header)`
  (z-21) which creates a stacking context. The hamburger menu's
  backdrop and dropdown were children of the header subtree, so their
  effective stacking-context z-index was clamped to z-21 from the
  document level — even though the CSS declared them at z-menu-backdrop=40
  and z-menu=50.

  Drawer overlays (.filter-sidebar, .detail-pane, .settings-pane)
  render at z-drawer=30 at the document root, which meant drawers
  appeared ABOVE the menu backdrop. Functionally rare because the
  menu auto-closes on item selection, so the overlap was hard to
  hit in practice — but it was a real regression trap waiting to
  bite if a drawer stayed open alongside the menu, or if the menu
  was kept open for theme picking (which it now routinely is via
  `keepOpen: true` — the trap was moving from theoretical to likely).

Fix — React Portal:

  HamburgerMenu.jsx imports `createPortal` from `react-dom` and
  renders the backdrop + dropdown via createPortal(portalContent,
  document.body). The portal target is document.body, which lives
  outside every stacking context, so the z-index values declared
  in the CSS now take effect at their literal document-root level.

  The trigger button stays inside the header subtree (it has to, so
  it sticks with the nav bar when the header is sticky-positioned).
  Only the dropdown surface + backdrop portal out.

Positioning — position:fixed with computed anchor:

  Because the dropdown is no longer a child of the trigger's
  containing block, its old `position: absolute; top: calc(100%
  + 6px); left: 0` anchor no longer works — there is no "100%" to
  refer to. Replaced with `position: fixed` and an inline style
  that sets `top` / `left` from the trigger's
  `getBoundingClientRect()`.

  Measurement runs in a useLayoutEffect (NOT useEffect) so the
  first frame of the open menu is already correctly placed. If we
  used useEffect, users would briefly see the dropdown flash at
  0,0 before the measurement ran.

  An additional `triggerPos` state guard prevents rendering the
  portal content at all until measurement has happened — on first
  open, the render returns null until useLayoutEffect fires, then
  re-renders with the computed position. This avoids a one-frame
  flash in the corner of the viewport.

  Position stays in sync while the menu is open via window resize
  + scroll (capture-phase) listeners. Capture-phase is required so
  that scrolling any nested scroll container (e.g. the dashboard's
  sticky-header offset changes during document scroll) also
  triggers an update, not just the document root.

styles.css update:

  .hamburger-dropdown rule: `position: absolute; top: calc(100% +
  6px); left: 0` replaced with `position: fixed` (no static
  offsets) and a comment block explaining the inline-style anchor
  pattern with a pointer to HamburgerMenu.jsx.

Alternatives rejected:

  - Popper.js / @floating-ui/react: would give auto-flip,
    auto-shift, collision detection out of the box, but adds a
    dependency (~15 KB gzipped) for a single menu with no complex
    placement needs. Our menu opens downward from a trigger that's
    always at the top of the viewport — no flip needed.
  - Remove the stacking context from .dashboard-header: can't
    drop z-index (header needs to stack above the sticky tabs-bar)
    or position: relative (::after pseudo-element requires a
    positioned parent). The stacking context is structurally
    required by the header's own design.
  - ResizeObserver on the trigger instead of window scroll: doesn't
    catch document scroll that moves the trigger under a sticky
    header. Need scroll listener anyway.

Verified:

  - `vite build` clean.
  - `createPortal`, `hamburger-dropdown`, and `hamburger-backdrop`
    all present in the built JS bundle.
  - `position: fixed` present in the built CSS for `.hamburger-dropdown`.
  - Keyboard nav (arrow keys, Enter, Escape, click-outside) unchanged.
  - keepOpen theme-picker behavior unchanged.

commits.subject: fix(toast): remove nested aria-live to stop double screen-reader announce
commits.body:
Post-migration audit finding. After Phase 2 of the DaisyUI sweep
(`1610c90`) the Toast component had two layers of aria-live region:

  - ToastContainer: aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="false"
  - Each ToastItem: role="alert" or role="status" + explicit aria-live

Per WAI-ARIA 1.2 section 5.2.2 ("Live Region Roles"), `role="alert"`
is an implicit live region with `aria-live="assertive"` and
`role="status"` is an implicit live region with `aria-live="polite"`.
Wrapping a live region in another live region creates a NESTED live
region, and most screen readers (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver tested in
prior audits of similar components) announce every new child TWICE
when the structure is nested — once via the parent's polite region,
once via the child's role-implied region.

Fixes:

- ToastContainer: removed `aria-live="polite"` and `aria-atomic="false"`.
  The container is now a pure positioning wrapper (`toast toast-bottom
  toast-center`) with no live-region semantics. Each child's role
  attribute provides the live region on its own.

- ToastItem: removed the explicit `aria-live={...}` since the role
  attribute already implies it (alert -> assertive, status -> polite).
  No behavior change — drops a redundant attribute that screen
  readers were already deriving from role.

- Documentation block added at the top of ToastItem explaining the
  rule with a reference to WAI-ARIA 1.2 section 5.2.2 so future
  edits don't re-add either layer.

Verified that `role="alert"` is still on error toasts (so urgent
errors are still announced assertively) and `role="status"` is on
info/success/warning toasts (so background updates are announced
politely without interrupting the user). Visual styling and
auto-dismiss timing are unchanged.

Post-migration audit finding. The Key Stats grid in Summary.jsx had a two-div sandwich per tile:
{metrics.features}
Features Built
The outer `stat-card` class had no CSS rule defined anywhere in the dashboard — it was a dead marker class that survived a prior cleanup. The interaction (role, tabIndex, aria-label, onClick, onKeyDown) was on the outer, and the visual (padding, background, rounding, text alignment) was on the inner. Functionally fine but a dead class is exactly the kind of "leave unused variables" trap CLAUDE.md's Cleanup rules forbid. Merged into a single
per tile carrying both the interaction and the visual classes:
{metrics.features}
Features Built
Added a rationale comment block at the top of the Key Stats section documenting why DaisyUI's `stat` component was rejected (horizontal stat-block vs our denser 1x4 / 2x2 tile grid) so future audits don't re-open the question. Affects all four tiles (Features Built, Bugs Fixed, Avg Urgency, % Planned). No behavior change — same click handlers, same aria labels, same visuals; just one less DOM element per tile and one less dead class in the codebase. commits.subject: fix(dashboard): drop DaisyUI v4 *-bordered cruft, clean stale comments
commits.body:
Post-migration audit of the 10-phase DaisyUI component-class sweep
found one real bug and a couple of stale documentation strings.

Real bug — `input-bordered` / `select-bordered` are NOT DaisyUI v5
classes:

- DaisyUI v5 (5.5.19) makes the border the default for `.input` and
  `.select` component classes. The `-bordered` modifier was a v4
  idiom that v5 replaced with `*-ghost` (for the no-border variant).
- Phase 8 used `select select-bordered select-sm` and
  `input input-bordered input-sm w-full`. The `-bordered` tokens
  did not exist in the generated DaisyUI CSS, so Tailwind silently
  dropped them. The controls still rendered bordered (v5's default),
  so the visual result was correct, but the tokens were dead cruft
  and the code lied about its intent.
- Grep verification after the fix: `grep -oE "\\.input-bordered|
  \\.select-bordered" dist/assets/index-*.css` returns zero matches
  both before and after the fix — confirming the tokens never
  contributed a single rule. The fix is a pure code-hygiene change
  with no behavioral delta.

Cleanup:
- SettingsPane.jsx: `select select-bordered select-sm` ->
  `select select-sm` for both work hour selects.
- FilterSidebar.jsx: `input input-bordered input-sm w-full` ->
  `input input-sm w-full` for both date inputs.
- Toast.jsx: header comment said `toast toast-top toast-center`
  while the implementation uses `toast toast-bottom toast-center`.
  Aligned the comment with the code.
- styles.css: the focus-visible comment block for `.tab-btn` /
  `.collapsible-header` / `.filter-multi-select-trigger` referenced
  "Phase 7 migrates this" and "Phase 10 stretch" as if those phases
  were still pending. Updated to describe the current state
  (composition layering alongside DaisyUI's `.tab`, custom listbox
  with no DaisyUI primitive available).

Verified:
- `./node_modules/.bin/vite build` clean.
- `node --test scripts/__tests__/oklchToHex.test.mjs` — 21/21 pass.
- Grep audit of dashboard/js confirms no other `*-bordered` tokens
  survive.

commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): migrate multi-select checkbox to DaisyUI checkbox
commits.body:
Phase 10 (final) of the DaisyUI component-class migration. Audit
the FilterSidebar MultiSelect widget, document why the listbox
structure cannot move to DaisyUI `dropdown` + `menu`, and migrate
the inner checkbox to DaisyUI for visual consistency with the rest
of the filter sidebar.

Findings:
- Wrong semantic match: the MultiSelect widget implements the
  WAI-ARIA combobox/listbox pattern (role="listbox", role="option",
  aria-multiselectable="true"). DaisyUI's `menu` component implies
  role="menu" which is semantically wrong for multi-select filter
  values. DaisyUI v5 provides no first-class listbox component.
- DaisyUI's `dropdown` shows/hides via CSS :focus, which conflicts
  with the React state (`open` + `highlightIndex`) needed for the
  arrow-key navigation.
- No shadowing: `.filter-multi-select-*` is a unique prefix; DaisyUI
  does not define any class under that namespace, so the custom
  styles are safe.
- Theme cleanliness: the existing rules already use
  `var(--color-base-*)` tokens and `color-mix()` — no hardcoded
  theme values were found in this widget.

Changes:
- FilterSidebar.jsx: rationale block added to the MultiSelect
  component explaining the rejection reasoning so future audits
  do not re-open this question.
- FilterSidebar.jsx: the inner `<input type="checkbox">` gains
  `className="checkbox checkbox-xs checkbox-primary"` so it renders
  in the active theme's primary color instead of the browser
  default, matching the visual language of the rest of the
  DaisyUI-migrated filter sidebar.
- styles.css: the `.filter-multi-select-option input[type="checkbox"]`
  rule is slimmed down to only reset margin and flex-shrink — size,
  color, and cursor are now handled by DaisyUI's `checkbox` class.

commits.subject: refactor(dashboard): audit HamburgerMenu for DaisyUI migration
commits.body:
Phase 9 of the DaisyUI component-class migration. Verify the
HamburgerMenu's custom `.hamburger-*` classes do not shadow any
DaisyUI component classes, document why a full migration to
DaisyUI `dropdown` + `menu` is deliberately rejected, and replace
the one remaining hardcoded theme value found in the audit.

Findings:
- No collision: the `.hamburger-*` prefix is unique; DaisyUI v5
  does not use it anywhere in its component set, so there is no
  shadowing risk like the one that prompted phases 5-6.
- Trigger button already migrated in phase 6 to
  `btn btn-ghost btn-square` — no further work needed there.
- Full migration to DaisyUI `dropdown` + `menu` rejected because:
  1. DaisyUI `dropdown` shows/hides via CSS `:focus` or the `[open]`
     attribute, which conflicts with our React-state-controlled
     `open` flag (needed for `keepOpen` theme picker items and
     async action error capture).
  2. DaisyUI `menu` implies `role="menu"`, which the shared
     BURGER_MENU pattern explicitly avoids because it forces
     screen readers into forms mode.
  The disclosure pattern is intentional and documented upstream in
  `docs/implementations/BURGER_MENU.md`.
- Hardcoded color fixed: `.hamburger-item-destructive:hover` used
  `rgba(239, 68, 68, 0.1)` which violates the CLAUDE.md rule against
  hardcoding theme values. Now uses
  `color-mix(in oklab, var(--color-error) 10%, transparent)` so the
  hover tint tracks the active theme's error color.
- Added a documentation block at the top of HamburgerMenu.jsx
  explaining the rejection reasoning so future audits do not
  re-open the same question.

commits.subject: feat(tabs): Phase 7 — TabBar adds DaisyUI tabs/tab class composition
commits.body:
Seventh of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. Small but
meaningful phase: TabBar already had proper ARIA roles (tablist/tab/
aria-selected), this phase adds DaisyUI's `tabs tabs-border` +
`tab tab-active` structural classes alongside the existing custom
`.tab-btn` / `.tab-btn-active` styling via class composition.

Changes:

TabBar.jsx:
- Outer wrapper gains `tabs tabs-border` DaisyUI classes alongside
  the existing `flex overflow-x-auto scrollbar-hide`. DaisyUI's tabs
  component applies its layout/border rules; our flex + overflow
  utilities handle the horizontal scroll on narrow viewports.
- Each tab button gains `tab` + `tab-active` (conditional) alongside
  our custom `tab-btn` / `tab-btn-active` classes. The custom classes
  provide the mono-font uppercase techy look; the DaisyUI classes
  provide the structural tabs behavior.
- Added `aria-label="Dashboard sections"` to the tablist container for
  screen reader context — "Dashboard sections, tablist, 6 items".
- Added a header comment documenting the composition rationale and
  rejected alternatives (pure DaisyUI tabs-lift/tabs-box have different
  typography; replacing .tab-btn with inline Tailwind would bloat the
  JSX).

Why keep .tab-btn / .tab-btn-active:

Our .tab-btn rules use:
  - font-family: var(--font-mono)
  - text-transform: uppercase
  - letter-spacing: 0.05em
  - font-size: 13px
  - font-weight: 500

And .tab-btn-active additionally applies a text-shadow glow in
var(--color-primary) that gives the active tab a distinctive "powered on"
look consistent with the dashboard's tech-focused aesthetic. DaisyUI's
default tab typography is sentence-case body font — clean and correct
for most apps, but doesn't match our visual language. Class composition
preserves both.

No CSS deletions this phase — .tab-btn / .tab-btn-active remain in
styles.css because they're not shadowing any DaisyUI class (DaisyUI uses
.tab, not .tab-btn, so they coexist).

Build verification:
  - npm run build: passes
  - DaisyUI .tabs, .tabs-border, .tab, .tab-active all present in built CSS

commits.subject: feat(btn): Phase 6 — migrate all buttons to DaisyUI btn (unshadow .btn-*)
commits.body:
Sixth of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. Large but
mechanical migration — every button in the codebase now uses DaisyUI
`btn btn-*` classes and we delete ~200 lines of custom .btn-* CSS that
was shadowing DaisyUI's built-in button component.

Component changes:

main.jsx                 — error screen Retry:      btn-icon btn-primary -> btn btn-primary
Header.jsx               — filter toggle:           .filter-toggle.active -> btn btn-ghost btn-square btn-active
                         — filter badge:            .filter-badge        -> badge badge-primary badge-xs
                         — settings button:         .btn-theme           -> btn btn-ghost btn-square
HamburgerMenu.jsx        — menu trigger:            .btn-theme           -> btn btn-ghost btn-square
ShowMoreButton.jsx       — pagination button:       .show-more-btn       -> btn btn-ghost btn-block btn-sm mt-3
FilterSidebar.jsx        — Include/Exclude toggle:  .filter-mode-toggle  -> join wrapper + join-item btn btn-xs
                                                                            (btn-active btn-primary for include,
                                                                             btn-active btn-error for exclude)
                         — Clear All button:        .btn-icon .btn-secondary .filter-clear-btn -> btn btn-outline btn-sm w-full mt-3
DetailPane.jsx           — close button:            .detail-pane-close   -> btn btn-sm btn-circle btn-ghost
SettingsPane.jsx         — close button:            .settings-pane-close -> btn btn-sm btn-circle btn-ghost
Projects.jsx             — Open link:               .project-link .project-link-primary   -> btn btn-primary btn-xs
                         — GitHub link:             .project-link .project-link-secondary -> btn btn-outline btn-xs

CSS deletions (~200 lines):

.btn-icon / .btn-primary / .btn-primary:hover / .btn-secondary /
.btn-secondary:hover / .btn-icon svg — shadowed DaisyUI's built-in
.btn-primary / .btn-secondary

.btn-theme / .btn-theme:hover / .btn-theme svg — icon button style

.show-more-btn / .show-more-btn:hover — pagination button style

.filter-toggle / .filter-toggle:hover / .filter-toggle.active /
.filter-toggle svg — filter sidebar toggle

.filter-preset-btn / :hover / .active — unused (no JSX consumer)

.filter-mode-toggle + child button rules (5 variants) — Include/Exclude
segmented toggle

.filter-clear-btn — Clear All button

.detail-pane-close / :hover — detail pane close icon button

.settings-pane-close / :hover — settings pane close icon button

.project-link / .project-link-primary / :hover / .project-link-secondary /
:hover — project list action links

.filter-toggle .filter-badge — badge nested inside filter toggle button

Mobile responsive block — removed .btn-icon / .btn-theme / .filter-toggle
mobile-size override (40px -> 36px). DaisyUI btn-square is 40px on
desktop which is fine for mobile too; if crowding becomes an issue
again, use btn-sm conditionally on mobile breakpoints instead.

Focus-visible selector list — removed .btn-icon / .filter-mode-toggle
button / .filter-clear-btn from the custom focus ring list. DaisyUI
btn handles its own :focus-visible ring (via btn base styles), so those
entries would double-apply or conflict. Kept .tab-btn (Phase 7
migrates this), .collapsible-header (custom), .filter-multi-select-trigger
(Phase 10 stretch).

Print media query — replaced `.show-more-btn, .hamburger-menu, .modal`
hide rule with `[aria-label^="Show "].btn, .hamburger-menu, .modal`
— the show-more-btn class no longer exists; the attribute selector
matches the button via its aria-label prefix without needing .btn to
be too broad (would hide every button on the page when printing).

Build verification:
  - npm run build: passes, 84 modules
  - All DaisyUI btn classes present: btn, btn-primary, btn-ghost,
    btn-outline, btn-square, btn-circle, btn-sm, btn-xs, btn-block,
    btn-active, btn-error
  - All deleted custom classes confirmed GONE from built CSS:
    .btn-icon, .btn-theme, .show-more-btn, .filter-toggle, .filter-clear-btn,
    .filter-preset-btn, .filter-mode-toggle, .project-link-primary,
    .detail-pane-close, .settings-pane-close, .filter-badge

Impact on visuals:

- All buttons now get DaisyUI's default btn styling: slight subtle
  shadow on hover, active depression, disabled state, loading spinner
  slot, focus-visible ring.
- btn-square (40x40 default) matches our previous .btn-theme and
  .filter-toggle dimensions exactly.
- btn-ghost for icon buttons gives transparent background with hover
  tint — same visual as before.
- btn-primary / btn-outline replace our custom hover glow effects;
  DaisyUI's hover is a subtle background shift, not a glow. Slightly
  less flashy, more consistent with the rest of the DaisyUI ecosystem.
- Include/Exclude segmented toggle now uses the DaisyUI `join`
  component for connected border-radius instead of hand-rolled
  first-child/last-child rules.

commits.subject: feat(card): Phase 5 — migrate .card to DaisyUI card + card-body (unshadow)
commits.body:
Fifth of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. This phase eliminates the .card shadow of DaisyUI's built-in card component by migrating all consumers and deleting the custom .card + related classes from styles.css. Changes: CollapsibleSection.jsx — the most impactful file in this phase because it wraps every dashboard section on every tab. Structure now:
...
...
card-body adds a DOM level but gives us DaisyUI's proper card structure. `p-6` (1.5rem) matches our previous 24px padding. `gap-0` overrides DaisyUI card-body's default 0.5rem flex gap which we don't want because our collapsible layout uses explicit margins. NOT migrating to DaisyUI's own `collapse` component — that's a stateless radio/checkbox mechanism that doesn't match React reducer state and doesn't support our chevron rotation / max-height transition. Documented as a rejected alternative in the component file. App.jsx — data-load error fallback migrated from .card to
The outer role="alert" was previously on the wrapper div (wrong element); moved onto the card itself where it semantically belongs. ErrorBoundary.jsx — section-level error fallback migrated the same way:
Projects.jsx — two migrations: 1. loadError fallback: .card projects-error -> card bg-base-200 + card-body items-center text-center py-8 2. ProjectCard component: .project-card -> card bg-base-200 border + card-body p-4 gap-0 (tighter padding than CollapsibleSection since project cards are denser, grid items) Discover.jsx — metric cards in the "Chew on this" section migrated from .card to card bg-base-200 border + card-body p-5 gap-0. styles.css — deleted ~35 lines: - .card / .card:hover / .card.cursor-pointer:hover (was shadowing DaisyUI's .card) - .card-loading (orphaned — no JSX consumer) - .projects-error (orphaned after the migration) - .project-card / .project-card:hover (migrated to DaisyUI card) - .error-boundary-card (orphaned) All replaced with one-line comments pointing at the component files. What's preserved: - 24px-ish visual padding via card-body's p-6/p-5/p-4 modifiers - bg-base-200 surface color - border-base-300 subtle border - hover:border-primary/40 transition on ProjectCard (explicit Tailwind) - collapsible-header + collapsible-content layout unchanged - All existing role="alert" / role="button" semantics What changed visually: - card-body adds an inner DOM layer (no visual effect) - The hover:border-color primary animation is no longer on .card by default — now only ProjectCard opts in via explicit hover:border-primary/40 - The `.card.cursor-pointer:hover { box-shadow: 0 0 20px ... }` glow effect from the old .card is not preserved — no current consumer needs it (Summary.jsx stat-card uses hover:ring-primary instead) Build verification: - npm run build: passes - DaisyUI .card and .card-body present in built CSS - .project-card, .projects-error, .error-boundary-card, .card-loading all confirmed GONE from built CSS Tags: ui, daisyui, refactor, card Complexity: 3 Urgency: 2 Impact: user-facing Risk: medium Debt: paid Epic: daisyui-component-migration Semver: minor https://claude.ai/code/session_018prUZYyhuCZQGS1azip9bG

commits.subject: feat(badges): Phase 4 — Timeline work-pattern badges to DaisyUI semantic variants
commits.body: Fourth of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. This phase eliminates the .badge + .badge-holiday / .badge-weekend / .badge-after-hours shadow of DaisyUI's built-in badge component. Changes: Timeline.jsx — three conditional work-pattern badges switched from Holiday to Holiday Mapping: isHoliday -> badge-accent (warm pink-ish — festive) isWeekend -> badge-info (cool blue — informational) isAfterHours (not weekend) -> badge-warning (amber — "attention") badge-sm keeps the footprint compact inside the commit metadata row where the badges appear alongside author/date/repo text. styles.css — deleted ~20 lines of custom badge CSS: - .badge (base rule: inline-flex, padding, radius, font-size, uppercase, letter-spacing) — was SHADOWING DaisyUI's .badge - .badge-after-hours (rgba-amber) - .badge-weekend (rgba-purple) - .badge-holiday (rgba-pink) Replaced with a one-line comment pointing at Timeline.jsx. Why this matters beyond Timeline: Our .badge rule was unlayered CSS that won every specificity contest against DaisyUI's @layer components badge. Deleting it means any future badge usage anywhere in the codebase (e.g. a new contributor role badge in Contributors section, status badges on Projects, etc.) will correctly get DaisyUI's component styles without having to remember that "we shadow .badge". One less footgun. Build verification: - npm run build: passes - .badge-holiday, .badge-weekend, .badge-after-hours confirmed GONE - DaisyUI .badge, .badge-accent, .badge-info, .badge-warning, .badge-sm confirmed present in built CSS Tags: ui, daisyui, refactor, badges Complexity: 1 Urgency: 1 Impact: user-facing Risk: low Debt: paid Epic: daisyui-component-migration Semver: patch https://claude.ai/code/session_018prUZYyhuCZQGS1azip9bG

commits.subject: feat(health): Phase 3 — migrate security summary containers to DaisyUI alert
commits.body:
Third of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. This phase converts the three "N security-related commits" summary containers in the Health section to DaisyUI `alert alert-error` with proper role="alert" ARIA semantics. Changes: Health.jsx — three summary containers (Phase-1 loading view, Executive view, Management view) switched from:
{count}
Security-related commits
to:
{count}
Security-related commits
Why: alert is a semantic notification component in both ARIA and DaisyUI. These containers tell the user "you have N security events to pay attention to" — the exact use case. With role="alert" screen readers announce the count immediately when the section renders; with `alert alert-error` we get DaisyUI's theme-aware error tint + `alert-content` text color pairing automatically, and we no longer need to hand-roll bg-error/10 + border-error/40 + text-error. Text colors changed from explicit text-base-content/80 and text-error to opacity-80 and opacity-60, inheriting from the alert-error `--alert-color` token so the text stays legible against the alert background regardless of the active DaisyUI theme (black, dim, coffee, dracula — each has its own error palette). The two individual-commit list-item containers (Phase-1 .slice(0,5) loop and Developer view securityCommits.map) stay as bespoke bg-error/10 divs — they're list items inside a security panel, not alerts themselves. Converting them to alert-error would produce three nested alerts per section, which is semantically wrong and visually noisy. flex flex-col items-center text-center layout is added inline because alert-error defaults to a row layout (icon slot + flex-1 message). Our summary containers stack the big number above the label, so we override to column with centered items. Build verification: - npm run build: passes - .alert-error class now present in built CSS Tags: ui, daisyui, a11y, refactor, alert Complexity: 1 Urgency: 1 Impact: user-facing Risk: low Debt: paid Epic: daisyui-component-migration Semver: patch https://claude.ai/code/session_018prUZYyhuCZQGS1azip9bG

commits.subject: feat(toast): Phase 2 — migrate Toast to DaisyUI toast + alert (unshadow .toast-*)
commits.body:
Second of 10 phases from the DaisyUI component-class audit. This phase
migrates the toast notification system to DaisyUI's `toast` positioning
wrapper + `alert alert-*` semantic variants, unshadowing the custom
.toast-* classes that were defined in styles.css and silently
overriding DaisyUI's built-in toast component.

Changes:

Toast.jsx — ToastItem now renders as `<div className="alert alert-${type}
shadow-lg transition-all duration-200 ease-out ...">` with the
type-to-class mapping in a small ALERT_CLASS_BY_TYPE object (success ->
alert-success, error -> alert-error, warning -> alert-warning, info ->
alert-info). Enter/exit animations are now Tailwind transition utilities
(opacity-0 translate-y-2 while exiting, opacity-100 translate-y-0
otherwise) instead of custom .toast-enter/.toast-exit keyframes. The
dismiss button is now `btn btn-ghost btn-xs btn-circle`. ToastContainer
uses `toast toast-bottom toast-center` (DaisyUI's positioning wrapper
classes) with an inline `style={{ zIndex: 'var(--z-toast)' }}` to pin
the stacking above other dashboard layers (DaisyUI's default z-index
doesn't stack above our debug pill or drawers).

styles.css — deleted ~65 lines of custom .toast-* CSS:
  - .toast-container (positioning)
  - .toast-item (layout, transitions)
  - .toast-enter / .toast-exit (animation states)
  - .toast-message
  - .toast-dismiss / .toast-dismiss:hover
  - .toast-success / .toast-error / .toast-info / .toast-warning
    (type colors that duplicated DaisyUI alert-* exactly)

All replaced with a one-line comment pointing at the component file.

Why this fixes the shadowing:

Our styles.css used to define .toast-container, .toast-item, etc.
UNLAYERED, which means they won ALL priority contests against DaisyUI's
own `.toast` / `.alert` definitions inside `@layer components`. Any
future `className="toast toast-top"` or `className="alert alert-error"`
usage elsewhere in the codebase would silently get our custom styles
instead of DaisyUI's component behavior. Deleting the custom classes
means DaisyUI's real `.toast` and `.alert` classes now apply, which is
what we actually want.

What's preserved:

- useToast() hook API and addToast/removeToast signatures — unchanged
- Auto-dismiss timer semantics (duration=0 means persistent)
- Two-stage isExiting state for animation-before-unmount ordering
- ARIA role="alert" for errors / role="status" for non-errors
- aria-live="assertive"/"polite" for screen reader urgency
- no-print class so toasts don't leak into PDF exports
- z-index: var(--z-toast) (= 70 from our scale, above drawers and menus)

Build verification:
  - npm test: 21 passing (no test regression)
  - npm run build: passes, 84 modules
  - DaisyUI .toast, .toast-bottom, .toast-center, .alert, .alert-success,
    .alert-error, .alert-warning, .alert-info, .alert-soft all present
    in built CSS
  - Old .toast-item / .toast-dismiss / .toast-message / .toast-enter /
    .toast-exit confirmed GONE from built CSS
  - Toast.jsx useToast API still referenced in the JS bundle
